1.title: Jesus
1.body: AD 1 – Birth of Jesus, as assigned by Dionysius Exiguus in his anno Domini era according to at least one scholar. However, most scholars think that Dionysius placed the birth of Jesus in the previous year, 1 BC. Furthermore, most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, placing the event several years earlier (see Chronology of Jesus).

1.title: aerarium militare
1.body: AD 6 – The Princeps Augustus sets up a treasury, the aerarium militare (170 million sestertii), with the specific purpose of paying bonuses to retiring legion veterans. This is financed by a 5% tax on inheritances, a system said to have been suggested in Julius Caesar's memoirs.

1.title: Roman Empire
1.body: AD 4 – A pact of non-aggression and friendship is signed between the Roman Empire, represented by Tiberius, and the German tribe the Cherusci, represented by their King Segimer. Arminius and Flavus, sons of Segimer, are brought into the Roman army as leaders of the auxiliary troops.

1.title: Gaius Julius Caesar Vipsanianus
1.body: AD 1 – Gaius Caesar meets with Phraates V, the king of Parthia, on the Euphrates. Rather than invading the Parthians, Gaius Caesar concludes peace with them; Parthia recognizes Roman claims to Armenia.

1.title: Tiberius
1.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

2.title: Jesus
2.body: AD 1 – Birth of Jesus, as assigned by Dionysius Exiguus in his anno Domini era according to at least one scholar. However, most scholars think that Dionysius placed the birth of Jesus in the previous year, 1 BC. Furthermore, most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, placing the event several years earlier (see Chronology of Jesus).

2.title: aerarium militare
2.body: AD 6 – The Princeps Augustus sets up a treasury, the aerarium militare (170 million sestertii), with the specific purpose of paying bonuses to retiring legion veterans. This is financed by a 5% tax on inheritances, a system said to have been suggested in Julius Caesar's memoirs.

2.title: Tiberius
2.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

However, most scholars think that Dionysius placed the birth of Jesus in the previous year, 1 BC. Furthermore, most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, placing the event several years earlier (see Chronology of Jesus).

3.title: Tiberius
3.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

4.title: Tiberius
4.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

Furthermore, most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, placing the event several years earlier (see Chronology of Jesus).

5.title: Tiberius
5.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

However, most scholars think that Dionysius placed the birth of Jesus in the previous year, 1 BC. Furthermore, most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, placing the event several years earlier (see Chronology of Jesus).

6.title: Roman Empire
6.body: AD 4 – A pact of non-aggression and friendship is signed between the Roman Empire, represented by Tiberius, and the German tribe the Cherusci, represented by their King Segimer. Arminius and Flavus, sons of Segimer, are brought into the Roman army as leaders of the auxiliary troops.

6.title: Gaius Julius Caesar Vipsanianus
6.body: AD 1 – Gaius Caesar meets with Phraates V, the king of Parthia, on the Euphrates. Rather than invading the Parthians, Gaius Caesar concludes peace with them; Parthia recognizes Roman claims to Armenia.

6.title: Tiberius
6.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

7.title: Publius Quinctilius Varus
7.body: Publius Quinctilius Varus is appointed governor of Germania, charged with organizing Germania between the Rhine and Elbe rivers. He carries out a census, devises tributes and recruits soldiers, all of which creates dissension among the Germanic tribes.

7.title: Puyang
7.body: Zhai Yi, Governor of the Commandery of Dong (modern Puyang, Henan) declares Liu Zin, Marquess of Yang Xiang (modern Tai'an, Shandong), emperor. This proves to be the largest of the rebellions against Emperor Ruzi of Han.

7.title: Vonones
7.body: Vonones I becomes ruler of the Parthian Empire (approximate date).

7.title: Illyrian tribes
7.body: Illyrian tribes in Pannonia and Dalmatia continue the Great Illyrian Revolt against Roman rule.

7.title: Wang Mang
7.body: Wang Mang puts down the rebellion during the winter. Zhai is captured and executed while Liu Xin escapes.

8.title: Jesus
8.body: AD 1 – Birth of Jesus, as assigned by Dionysius Exiguus in his anno Domini era according to at least one scholar. However, most scholars think that Dionysius placed the birth of Jesus in the previous year, 1 BC. Furthermore, most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, placing the event several years earlier (see Chronology of Jesus).

8.title: aerarium militare
8.body: AD 6 – The Princeps Augustus sets up a treasury, the aerarium militare (170 million sestertii), with the specific purpose of paying bonuses to retiring legion veterans. This is financed by a 5% tax on inheritances, a system said to have been suggested in Julius Caesar's memoirs.

8.title: Roman Empire
8.body: AD 4 – A pact of non-aggression and friendship is signed between the Roman Empire, represented by Tiberius, and the German tribe the Cherusci, represented by their King Segimer. Arminius and Flavus, sons of Segimer, are brought into the Roman army as leaders of the auxiliary troops.

8.title: Gaius Julius Caesar Vipsanianus
8.body: AD 1 – Gaius Caesar meets with Phraates V, the king of Parthia, on the Euphrates. Rather than invading the Parthians, Gaius Caesar concludes peace with them; Parthia recognizes Roman claims to Armenia.

8.title: Tiberius
8.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

9.title: Jesus
9.body: AD 1 – Birth of Jesus, as assigned by Dionysius Exiguus in his anno Domini era according to at least one scholar. However, most scholars think that Dionysius placed the birth of Jesus in the previous year, 1 BC. Furthermore, most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, placing the event several years earlier (see Chronology of Jesus).

9.title: aerarium militare
9.body: AD 6 – The Princeps Augustus sets up a treasury, the aerarium militare (170 million sestertii), with the specific purpose of paying bonuses to retiring legion veterans. This is financed by a 5% tax on inheritances, a system said to have been suggested in Julius Caesar's memoirs.

9.title: Roman Empire
9.body: AD 4 – A pact of non-aggression and friendship is signed between the Roman Empire, represented by Tiberius, and the German tribe the Cherusci, represented by their King Segimer. Arminius and Flavus, sons of Segimer, are brought into the Roman army as leaders of the auxiliary troops.

9.title: Gaius Julius Caesar Vipsanianus
9.body: AD 1 – Gaius Caesar meets with Phraates V, the king of Parthia, on the Euphrates. Rather than invading the Parthians, Gaius Caesar concludes peace with them; Parthia recognizes Roman claims to Armenia.

9.title: Tiberius
9.body: AD 1 – Tiberius, under order of Emperor Augustus, quells revolts in Germania (AD 1–5).

10.title: Gospel of Luke
10.body: According to the Gospel of Luke, Jesus visits Herod's Temple and gets lost.

10.title: Euthydemid dynasty
10.body: The Euthydemid dynasty, a Greek dynasty in Bactria, is brought to an end.

10.title: Germanic peoples
10.body: Differentiation of localized Teutonic tribes of the Irminones.

10.title: Ovid
10.body: Ovid completes Tristia III (the "Sorrows") describing the sadness of banishment.

10.title: Wang Mang
10.body: The usurper Wang Mang (who rules during a brief interregnum known as the Xin dynasty) outlaws the private purchase and use of crossbows. Despite this, Liu Xiu, the later Emperor Guangwu of Han, buys crossbows in the winter of AD 22 to aid the rebellion of his brother Liu Yan (styled Bosheng) and Li Tong.

11.title: Augustus
11.body: Emperor Augustus abandons his plan to create a defensive border at the Elbe, in order to reinforce the Roman defenses along the Rhine and the Danube.

11.title: Satakarni
11.body: Satakarni begins his reign as Emperor of the Andhra Empire (AD 11–29).

11.title: Divinatory
11.body: An edict is issued effecting an empire-wide ban on divinatory practices, especially astrology. The edict requires any consultation between a customer and a practitioner to be conducted with at least one third party witness present, and bans inquiry into anyone's death.

11.title: Germania Inferior
11.body: Germania Inferior and the Rhine are secured by Germanicus.

11.title: Artabanus II of Parthia
11.body: Artabanus II becomes ruler of Parthia.

12.title: Armenia
12.body: The Armenian Artaxiad dynasty is overthrown by the Romans.

12.title: Augustus
12.body: Augustus orders a major invasion of Germany beyond the Rhine.

12.title: Quirinius
12.body: Quirinius returns from Judea to become a counselor to Tiberius.

12.title: Fasti
12.body: Ovid stops writing Fasti, because of the lack of resources (being far from the libraries of Rome). He completes 6 books that detail festivals found in the Roman calendar.

12.title: Annius Rufus
12.body: Annius Rufus is appointed Prefect of Judea.

13.title: Strabo
13.body: Strabo publishes his book on the shape of the Earth.

13.title: Augustus
13.body: Emperor Augustus initiates his third census of the Roman Empire after 20 years.

13.title: Epistulae ex Ponto
13.body: Ovid publishes books 1-3 of his Epistulae ex Ponto.

13.title: senatus consultum
13.body: The Senate passes a senatus consultum restricting the reduced Vigintisexviri to the Ordo Equester.

13.title: Abgarus of Edessa
13.body: Abgarus of Edessa is reinstalled as king of Osroene.

14.title: Augustus
14.body: Augustus' third (and final) 20-year census of the Roman Empire reports a total of 4,973,000 citizens.

14.title: Tiberius
14.body: Tiberius succeeds his stepfather Augustus as Roman emperor.

14.title: Famine
14.body: Famine hits China; some citizens turn to cannibalism.

14.title: Rhine
14.body: Legions on the Rhine mutiny after the death of Augustus; Germanicus restores discipline amongst the legions.

14.title: Nauportus
14.body: The town and port of Nauportus are plundered by a mutinous Roman legion that was sent there to build roads and bridges.

15.title: Bios Kaisaros
15.body: Nicolaus of Damascus writes a biography of the Emperor Augustus (Bios Kaisaros).

15.title: Rome
15.body: In Rome, the election of magistrates passes from the people to the Emperor and the Senate.

15.title: aquila
15.body: Summer – Germanicus launches a two-pronged attack from Vetera and Moguntiacum. On his return journey, he recaptures the aquila of Legio XIX and visits the battlefield of the Teutoburg Forest. Germanicus arranges the burial for the remains of Varus' army.

15.title: Emona
15.body: Early (approx.) – Emona (on the site of modern-day Ljubljana) is founded by Legio XV Apollinaris.

15.title: Germanic peoples
15.body: May – As part of his campaign against the Germanic peoples, Germanicus captures Thusnelda, wife of Arminius.

16.title: Rhine
16.body: Germanicus employs the North Sea fleet to avoid dangerous rivers, embarking an army in the Rhine Delta, aboard circa 1,000 ships. He defeats the Germans at the Amisius river estuary and the Weser, but during its return, the Roman fleet is partially destroyed by storms.

16.title: Roman army
16.body: A Roman army of 50,000 men commanded by Germanicus gains a great victory at Idistaviso, defeating the German war chief Arminius, and recovering the lost eagles of Varus' legions.

16.title: Vonones I of Parthia
16.body: Vonones, the beleaguered king of Armenia, is summoned to Syria, by Roman governor Creticus Silanus.

17.title: Limes Tripolitanus
17.body: Tacfarinas, Numidian deserter from the Roman army, begins a guerrilla war against the Romans. He leads his own Musulamii tribe and a coalition of Berbers, attacking the Limes Tripolitanus, a fortified zone (limes) of the Roman Empire in Africa.

17.title: Tiberius
17.body: Emperor Tiberius sends Germanicus to the east, in order to lead a military campaign against Parthia.

17.title: Herod Antipas
17.body: Herod Antipas, son of Herod the Great, builds the city Tiberias on the western shore of the Sea of Galilee, in honor of Tiberius.

17.title: Cappadocia
17.body: Cappadocia (Asia Minor) becomes a Roman province.

17.title: Anatolia
17.body: An earthquake in Anatolia destroys the city of Sardis and damages several other cities.

18.title: Syria
18.body: Winter – Germanicus Caesar arrives in Syria, as new commander-in-chief for the Roman East.

18.title: Peace treaty
18.body: Germanicus concludes a peace treaty with Artabanus II of Parthia, in which he is recognized as king and friend of Rome.

18.title: Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso
18.body: Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso, governor of Syria, ignores the order of Germanicus to send Syrian-based legions, including Legio VI Ferrata and Legio X Fretensis, to Armenia to back him in his planned coronation of Artaxias III.

18.title: Yellow River
18.body: After a flooding of the Yellow River in China, farmers are forced to rebel. Emperor Wang Mang reacts by sending an army (some 100,000 men) against the agrarian rebels. The rebel leaders, concerned that during battle it will become impossible to tell friend from foe, order that their men color their eyebrows red – and this is where the name Chimei ("The Red Eyebrows") comes from.

18.title: Daemusin of Goguryeo
18.body: Daemusin becomes ruler of the Korean kingdom of Goguryeo.

19.title: Germanicus Julius Caesar
19.body: Germanicus Julius Caesar, commander in chief of the Roman legions in the East and beloved by the legionaries, falls ill and dies. On his deathbed he accuses Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso, the governor of Syria, of poisoning him.

19.title: Maroboduus
19.body: Maroboduus, king of the Marcomanni, is deposed by Catualda. This ends the threat to the Romans from Germanic tribes until the reign of Marcus Aurelius. Rome places them under its protection.

19.title: Tiberius
19.body: Emperor Tiberius expels the Egyptians from Rome, and deports 4,000 Jews from Sicily.

19.title: Agrippina the Elder
19.body: Agrippina the Elder accuses Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso of having assassinated her husband Germanicus Julius Caesar in Antioch. However, there is no credible evidence and the charge is never proven. (In ancient times, when medical science was not advanced, poison was usually suspected whenever a young, healthy person died suddenly. There was no way to pinpoint and trace the substance after death; therefore, it was considered a quick, easy and non-traceable form of homicide.)

19.title: Triumphal arch
19.body: A triumphal arch is built for Germanicus Julius Caesar in Saintes.

20.title: Galba
20.body: Galba, the future emperor, is a Roman praetor.

20.title: Tiberius
20.body: Emperor Tiberius is forced to order an investigation and a public trial in the Roman Senate, for the murder of Germanicus. Fearing he will be found guilty, Gnaeus Calpurnius Piso commits suicide.

21.title: Buffer state
21.body: The Romans create a buffer state in the territory of the Quadi, in southern Slovakia.

21.title: Pen
21.body: The manufacture of pens and metal writing tools begins in Rome (approximate date).

21.title: Praetorian Guard
21.body: Barracks are constructed for the Praetorian Guard, on the Quirinal (located on the Seven Hills of Rome).

21.title: Daeso
21.body: King Daeso of Dongbuyeo is killed in battle against the armies of Goguryeo, led by its third ruler, King Daemusin.

21.title: Aedui
21.body: The Aedui revolt under Julius Florus and Julius Sacrovir; the revolt is suppressed by Gaius Silius.

22.title: tribunicia potestas
22.body: Drusus Julius Caesar receives the tribunicia potestas (tribunician power).

23.title: Geographer
23.body: Greek geographer Strabo publishes Geographica, a work covering the world known to the Romans and Greeks at the time of Emperor Augustus – it is the only such book to survive from the ancient world.

23.title: Tiberius
23.body: Emperor Tiberius' son Drusus Julius Caesar dies. From that point forward, Tiberius seems to lose interest in the Empire and occupies himself with the pursuit of pleasure.

23.title: Gengshi Emperor
23.body: Liu Xuan, a descendant of the Han dynasty royal family and leader of insurgents against the Xin dynasty, proclaims himself emperor against Wang Mang.

23.title: Sejanus
23.body: Lucius Aelius Sejanus begins to dominate the Roman Senate and Tiberius, after the death of Drusus.

23.title: Emperor Guangwu of Han
23.body: July – After being under siege for two months, about 19,000 insurgents under Liu Xiu defeat 450,000 of Wang Mang's troops in the Battle of Kunyang, ushering in the fall of Wang Mang's Xin dynasty and restoration of the Han dynasty.

24.title: North Africa during the Classical Period
24.body: The Masinissa line of the rulers of Carthage ends.

24.title: Yuri of Silla
24.body: King Yuri ascends to the throne as ruler of Silla (Korea).

24.title: The factual accuracy of part of this article is disputed.
24.body: The factual accuracy of part of this article is disputed. 14 says this event took place in AD 23.. Please help to ensure that disputed statements are reliably sourced.

24.title: Numidia
24.body: The Roman war against Numidia and Mauretania ends with the annexation of the two African kingdoms.

24.title: chachaung
24.body: In the Kingdom of Silla, which compromises most of the eastern Korean peninsula, Yuri of the House of Park becomes the new monarch (the chachaung). King Yuri takes the throne at the capital, Seorabeo (now Gyeongju in South Korea) upon the death of his father, King Namhae.

25.title: Tiberius
25.body: Emperor Tiberius settles a dispute between Messenia and Sparta over the Ager Dentheliales on Mount Taygetus, awarding the land to Messenia.

25.title: Jianwu
25.body: The Han dynasty is restored in China as Liu Xiu proclaims himself Emperor Guangwu of Han, starting the Jianwu era (until AD 56).

25.title: Luoyang
25.body: Luoyang becomes the capital of the Houhan or Eastern Han dynasty.

25.title: Sejanus
25.body: Lucius Aelius Sejanus unsuccessfully attempts to marry Livilla.

26.title: Tiberius
26.body: Emperor Tiberius retires to Capri, leaving the Praetorian Guard under Lucius Aelius Sejanus in charge of the Roman Empire and the city of Rome.

26.title: Pontius Pilate
26.body: Pontius Pilate is appointed as prefect of Judea.

26.title: Jesus Christ
26.body: Jesus is baptized in the Jordan River by John the Baptist and begins his public ministry, according to some scholars.

26.title: Thracians
26.body: Romans crush an uprising of Thracian tribesmen.

26.title: Xinlun
26.body: Xinlun is presented by author Huan Tan to Emperor Guangwu of Han with its final chapter (on zither music) still unfinished.

27.title: John the Baptist
27.body: Using the dates and ranges listed in the Gospel of Luke, this year can be established as when John the Baptist begins preaching in the Jordan. It is also likely that Jesus was baptised by John in the final months of this year before his temptation and the first of three Passovers listed in the Gospel of John.

27.title: Rimini
27.body: An Arc of Triumph is erected in Rimini, in honor of the former Emperor Augustus.

27.title: Rome
27.body: A fire breaks out in Rome.

27.title: Baptism of Jesus
27.body: Possible date of the Baptism of Jesus by John the Baptist (another possibility is AD 29).

27.title: Amphitheatre
27.body: A poorly built amphitheatre in Fidenae collapses, killing 20,000 of the 50,000 spectators.

28.title: Gospel of Luke
28.body: According to the Gospel of Luke (Luke 3:1-2), the ministries of John the Baptist and Jesus begin at the earliest in this year, and more likely in AD 29.

28.title: Roman Empire
28.body: The Frisians negotiate a treaty with the Roman Empire at the River Rhine.

28.title: Roman legion
28.body: Roman legions in Germania are transported by fleet to the fortress of Flevum on the Rhine, to operate against the rebellious Frisians.

28.title: Daru of Baekje
28.body: King Daru of Baekje succeeds to the throne of Baekje in the Korean peninsula.

28.title: Battle of Baduhenna Wood
28.body: Frisians revolt against Roman authorities, hanging tax collectors and besieging a local fort. Frisians achieve victory and gain considerable prestige among other local tribes.

29.title: Gospel of Luke
29.body: According to the Gospel of Luke (Luke 3:1-2), the ministries of John the Baptist and Jesus is thought to have started this year. Jesus is baptized by John the Baptist.

29.title: Jesus
29.body: Jesus executed by crucifixion, according to Roman Catholic tradition and Tertullian's chronology.

29.title: Agrippina the Elder
29.body: Agrippina the Elder is exiled to the island of Pandataria, and her sons (except Caligula) are imprisoned by Lucius Aelius Sejanus.

29.title: Aulus Plautius
29.body: Aulus Plautius, later military leader of the invasion of Britain under Emperor Claudius, becomes suffect consul alongside Lucius Nonius Asprenas.

30.title: Good Friday
30.body: 7 April (Good Friday) – Jesus is crucified (according to one dating scheme). He is later reported alive by his disciples.

30.title: Agrippina the Elder
30.body: Agrippina the Elder (the wife of Germanicus) and two of her sons, Nero Julius Caesar and Drusus Caesar, are arrested and exiled on orders of Lucius Aelius Sejanus (the prefect of the Praetorian Guard), and later starved to death in suspicious circumstances. In Sejanus's purge of Agrippina the Elder and her family, her son Caligula, and her three daughters, Agrippina the Younger, Julia Drusilla and Julia Livilla are the only survivors.

30.title: Velleius Paterculus
30.body: Velleius Paterculus writes the general history of the countries known in Antiquity.

30.title: Kushan Empire
30.body: The Kushan Empire is founded (approximate date).

30.title: Phaedrus
30.body: Phaedrus translates Aesop's fables, and composes some of his own.

31.title: Sejanus
31.body: Lucius Aelius Sejanus is named co-Consul to Emperor Tiberius. However, Tiberius becomes aware of Sejanus' treachery and has him arrested and executed.

31.title: Naevius Sutorius Macro
31.body: Naevius Sutorius Macro becomes the leader of the Praetorian Guard after Sejanus is executed.

32.title: (Allegory)
32.body: Philo writes his symbolic interpretation of the Old Testament (Allegory).

33.title: Tiberius
33.body: Emperor Tiberius founds a credit bank in Rome.

33.title: Wang Mang
33.body: Although the usurpation of Wang Mang and the Chimei Rebellion are behind him, Emperor Guangwu now faces a new threat to the Han dynasty: the Rebellion of Gongsun Shu in the Sichuan province. Gongsun's naval forces are unsuccessful against Han General Cen Peng, so Gongsun decides to fortify his position by blockading the entire Yangtze River with a large floating pontoon bridge, complete with floating fortified posts. After Cen Peng is unable to break through, he constructs several "castle ships" with high ramparts and ramming vessels known as "colliding swoopers", which break through Gongsun's lines and allow Cen to quell his rebellion.

33.title: Colin Humphreys
33.body: According to Colin Humphrey's account, Jesus of Nazareth's Last Supper takes place.

33.title: Financial crisis of 33
33.body: A financial crisis hits Rome, due to poorly chosen fiscal policies. Land values plummet, and credit is increased. These actions lead to a lack of money, a crisis of confidence, and much land speculation.

34.title: Naevius Sutorius Macro
34.body: Naevius Sutorius Macro gains favour in the Roman Empire by prostituting his wife Ennia Thrasylla to Caligula.

34.title: Paul the Apostle
34.body: Saul of Tarsus, on the road to Damascus, is converted to Christianity, and becomes Paul the Apostle (possible date).

34.title: Dacia
34.body: The original inhabitants of Dacia revolt against the Sarmatian tribe of Iazyges, who had enslaved them.

34.title: Antioch
34.body: According to one dating scheme, Paul and Barnabas announce in Antioch that they will begin to evangelize the Gentiles.

35.title: Tiberius
35.body: Emperor Tiberius names Caligula (aka Gaius) and Gamellus, a grandson, as co-heirs.

35.title: Pliny the Elder
35.body: Pliny the Elder is brought to Rome before this year.

35.title: Tiridates III of Parthia
35.body: Tiridates III becomes king of Parthia (until AD 36).

36.title: Pontius Pilate
36.body: Pontius Pilate is recalled to Rome, after putting down a Samaritan uprising.

36.title: Herod Antipas
36.body: Herod Antipas suffers major losses in a war with Aretas IV of Nabatea, provoked partly by Antipas' divorce of Aretas' daughter. According to Josephus, Herod's defeat was popularly believed to be divine punishment for his execution of John the Baptist. Emperor Tiberius orders his governor of Syria, Vitellius, to capture or kill Aretas, but he is reluctant to support Herod and abandons his campaign upon Tiberius' death in AD 37.

36.title: Wu Han
36.body: Wu Han commands the forces of Emperor Guang Wu of the Eastern Han to conquer the separatist Chengjia Empire, reuniting China.

36.title: Marcellus
36.body: Marcellus becomes governor of Judaea and Samaria.

36.title: Lucius Vitellius the Elder
36.body: Lucius Vitellius defeats Artabanus III of Parthia in support of another claimant to the throne, Tiridates III.

37.title: Pharisee
37.body: The Pharisee Saul of Tarsus is converted to Christianity, after a vision. After the year 39, he is recognised as Saint Paul.

37.title: Saint Peter
37.body: Peter the Apostle founds the Church of Antioch (approximate date).

37.title: Roman Senate
37.body: The Roman Senate annuls Tiberius's will, and proclaims Caligula as Roman Emperor, nullifying the joint claim of Tiberius Gemellus. Caligula's attempt to have himself deified creates friction between himself and the Senate.

37.title: Abilene
37.body: Abilene is granted to King Agrippa I.

38.title: Apion
38.body: Apion heads a deputation to Emperor Caligula, to complain about the Jews in Alexandria.

38.title: Claudius
38.body: Claudius and Messalina are most likely married at this point in time.

38.title: Andrew the Apostle
38.body: Andrew the Apostle becomes the first patriarch of Constantinople, and resigns that position shortly thereafter (traditional date).

38.title: Alexandrian riots
38.body: An anti-Jewish riot breaks out in Alexandria, during a visit by King Herod Agrippa I; the mob wants to place statues of Caligula in every synagogue.

38.title: Stachys the Apostle
38.body: Stachys the Apostle becomes the second patriarch of Constantinople (traditional date).

39.title: Caligula
39.body: Caligula orders that a statue of himself be placed in the Temple in Jerusalem. The governor of Syria, Publius Petronius, who is responsible for having an erecting the statue, faces mass demonstrations by Jews of the region and manages to delay construction of the statue until the death of Caligula in AD 41.

39.title: Tigellinus
39.body: Tigellinus, minister and favorite of the later Roman emperor Nero, is banished for adultery with Caligula's sisters.

39.title: Philo
39.body: Philo leads a Jewish delegation to Rome to protest the anti-Jewish conditions in Alexandria.

39.title: Agrippa
39.body: Agrippa I, king of Judaea, successfully accuses Herod Antipas, tetrarch of Galilee and Perea, of conspiracy against Caligula. Antipas is exiled and Agrippa receives his territory.

39.title: Trung Sisters
39.body: The Trung Sisters resist the Chinese influences in Vietnam.

40.title: Noricum
40.body: Noricum and Mauretania are incorporated into the Roman Empire.

40.title: Vietnam
40.body: The Vietnamese Trưng Sisters rebel against the rule of the Chinese Emperor Guang Wu of Han.

40.title: Egypt
40.body: Christianity comes to Egypt as a church is founded in Alexandria. Mark the Evangelist founds the Church of Alexandria as the first Patriarch (traditional date).

40.title: Quadi
40.body: The Germanic Quadi tribe begin settling in present-day Moravia and Slovakia.

40.title: Caligula
40.body: Emperor Caligula is consul without colleague.

41.title: Caligula
41.body: January 24 Caligula, known for his eccentricity and cruel despotism, is assassinated by his disgruntled Praetorian Guards. Claudius succeeds his nephew, Caligula, as emperor.

41.title: Claudius
41.body: Claudius succeeds his nephew, Caligula, as emperor.

41.title: Messalina
41.body: Messalina, wife of Claudius, persuades Claudius to have Seneca the Younger banished to Corsica on a charge of adultery with Julia Livilla.

41.title: Emperor Guangwu of Han
41.body: Emperor Guang Wu of the Han Dynasty deposes his wife, Guo Shengtong, as empress, and makes his consort Yin Lihua empress in her place.

41.title: Rhine
41.body: An attack across the Rhine by the Germans is stopped by the Romans.

42.title: Algeria
42.body: The territories of the current Algeria and Morocco become a Roman province.

42.title: Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria
42.body: Traditional date of foundation of the Coptic Orthodox Church of Alexandria by the apostle Mark the Evangelist.

42.title: Claudius
42.body: Claudius begins construction of Portus, a harbour bearing a lighthouse on the right bank of the Tiber.

42.title: Ceuta
42.body: Romans take control of Ceuta, a port city on the North African side of the Strait of Gibraltar.

42.title: Dalmatia
42.body: Dalmatian legate Lucius Arruntius Camillus Scribonianus revolts, but his troops defect, and his rebellion quickly withers.

43.title: Roman conquest of Britain
43.body: Roman conquest of Britain: May – Aulus Plautius, crossing (probably) from Boulogne (Bononia) in the Classis Britannica, lands with four Roman legions (20,000 men) and the same number of auxiliaries at Rutupiae (probably modern Richborough) on the east coast of Kent. General (future emperor) Vespasian plays a major role in the defeat of the Britons led by the brothers Caratacus and Togodumnus (leaders of the Catuvellauni) in the 2-day Battle of the Medway (probably at the river near Rochester) and the Romans drive them back to and across the River Thames; Togodumnus dies soon after. Plautius halts at the Thames and sends for the Emperor.

43.title: Roman Empire
43.body: The Romans capture a Brythonic settlement at Kent and rename it Durovernum Cantiacorum (modern Canterbury); and establish a Roman fort to guard the crossing of the Kentish River Stour.

43.title: Vietnam
43.body: Vietnam is designated a province of China.

43.title: Mediterranean Sea
43.body: The Romans now have complete control of the Mediterranean Sea.

43.title: Huns
43.body: Warfare begins between the northern and southern Huns.

44.title: De situ orbis
44.body: Pomponius Mela writes De situ orbis, a geography of the Earth.

44.title: Claudius
44.body: Emperor Claudius returns from his British campaign in triumph, the southeast part of Britannia now held by the Roman Empire, but the war will rage for another decade and a half.

44.title: Rhodes
44.body: The Isle of Rhodes returns to the Roman Empire.

44.title: Judaea
44.body: (Approximate date, may be as late as 48) A famine takes place in Judea.

44.title: Judea
44.body: Judaea is controlled by Roman governors.

45.title: Sabaria
45.body: Claudius founds Savaria, today the Hungarian city of Szombathely.

45.title: municipium
45.body: Juvavum (modern Salzburg) is awarded the status of a Roman municipium.

45.title: Claudius
45.body: Emperor Claudius expels the Jews from Rome (estimated date, more probably later, 47-49 AD).

45.title: Ma Yuan
45.body: Chinese general Ma Yuan conducts an expedition against the Xiongnu and the Xianbei (in Manchuria).

45.title: Roman Senate
45.body: The Senate holds consultations regarding real estate speculation in Rome.

46.title: Famine
46.body: A large famine occurred across Syria, recorded by Orosius, Suetonius, Tacitus and Josephus, due to bad harvests.

46.title: Ancient Rome
46.body: Rome and its northeast border are reunited by the Danube Road.

46.title: Drought
46.body: A drought and an invasion of locusts hit the Mongolian steppes, causing a famine and a revolt at Xiongnu.

46.title: Census
46.body: A census shows that there are more than 6,000,000 Roman citizens.

46.title: Dobruja
46.body: Dobruja is annexed into Roman Moesia.

47.title: Fossa Corbulonis
47.body: Corbulo orders the construction of the canal Fossa Corbulonis, between the Rhine and Meuse in the Netherlands, which connects the city Forum Hadriani (Voorburg).

47.title: Martigny
47.body: Claudius founds the city Forum Claudii Vallensium (modern Martigny) in the Alpes Poeninae (Switzerland).

47.title: ludi saeculares
47.body: Claudius revives the censorship and ludi saeculares, and organises the order of the Haruspices, with 60 members.

47.title: Traiectum
47.body: Romans build the Traiectum fortification near the mouth of the Rhine, which will later grow to be the city of Utrecht.

47.title: Cauci
47.body: Cauci pirates led by the Roman deserter Gannascus ravage the Gallic coast; Corbulo uses the Rhine fleet against them. The Frisian revolt is suppressed.

48.title: Christianity
48.body: According to Christian tradition, Martha travels to Avignon.

48.title: Publius Ostorius Scapula
48.body: Publius Ostorius Scapula, governor of Britain, announces his intention to disarm all Britons south and east of the Trent and Severn. The Iceni, an independent, allied kingdom within that area, revolt but are defeated. Ostorius then moves against the Deceangli in north Wales, but is forced to abandon the campaign to deal with a revolt among the allied Brigantes.

48.title: Claudius
48.body: Emperor Claudius invests Agrippa II with the office of superintendent of the Temple in Jerusalem.

48.title: Emperor Guangwu of Han
48.body: Emperor Guang Wu of Han, restores Chinese domination of Inner Mongolia. The Xiongnu are made confederates and guard the Northern border of the empire.

48.title: Gauls
48.body: Gallic nobles are admitted to the Roman Senate. Claudius grants the rights of citizenship to the Aedui.

49.title: Acts of the Apostles
49.body: Christianity spreads into Europe, especially at Rome and at Philippi (probable date according to chronology derived from the Acts of the Apostles).

49.title: Jews
49.body: Likely date for the expulsion of the Jews from Rome.

49.title: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
49.body: Seneca the Younger becomes Nero's tutor.

49.title: Nero
49.body: Nero becomes engaged to Claudia Octavia, daughter of Claudius.

49.title: Paul the Apostle
49.body: The Apostle Paul begins his second missionary journey with Silas while Barnabas goes to Cyprus with Mark.

50.title: castellum
50.body: Utrecht is founded, and a Roman fortification (castellum) is constructed at the Rhine border in the present-day Netherlands.

50.title: Londinium
50.body: In Britain, governor Publius Ostorius Scapula begins his campaign against the recalcitrant Silures of south Wales, who are led by the former Catuvellaunian prince Caratacus. London (Londinium), Exeter (Isca Dumnoniorum), Tripontium (near modern Rugby) and the fort of Manduessedum (near modern Atherstone) are founded (approximate date).

50.title: Judaea
50.body: In Judaea, a Roman soldier seizes and burns a Torah scroll. Procurator Cumanus has the culprit beheaded, calming down the Jews and delaying for almost two decades the outbreak of their revolt.

50.title: Christianity
50.body: Christianity is introduced throughout Nubia by a high official of Queen Judith.

51.title: Sextus Afranius Burrus
51.body: Burrus, praetorian prefect (51–62 AD), charges Seneca with the education of Nero.

51.title: Roman Britain
51.body: In Britain, governor Publius Ostorius Scapula defeats Caratacus and the Silures in the territory of the Ordovices in central Wales. Caratacus seeks sanctuary with Cartimandua, queen of the Brigantes in northern England, but she is a Roman ally and hands him over to Ostorius. Despite the defeat, the Silures continue to fight.

51.title: Roman triumph
51.body: The captured Caratacus is exhibited in chains in Claudius' triumph in Rome, but his dignified demeanour persuades the emperor to spare his life and allow his family to live free in the capital for a short period of time.

51.title: Claudius
51.body: Emperor Claudius and future emperor Titus Flavius Vespasianus are Roman Consuls.

51.title: Vonones II of Parthia
51.body: Vonones II dies a few months after he had ascended to the throne. His son Vologases I becomes the new Parthian king.

52.title: Thomas the Apostle
52.body: Saint Thomas, one of the twelve disciples of Jesus, is believed to have landed in Kodungallur, India to preach the Gospel; the Syro-Malabar Catholic Church, the Malankara Mar Thoma Syrian Church, the Indian Orthodox Church, and the Assyrian Church of the East claim descent from him.

52.title: Ananias son of Nedebaios
52.body: Ananias, a high priest in Jerusalem, is sent to Rome after being accused of violence.

52.title: Roman Britain
52.body: In Britain, governor Publius Ostorius Scapula dies while campaigning against the Silures of south Wales. Following his death, the Roman Second Legion are heavily defeated by the Silures. His replacement is Aulus Didius Gallus, who quells the rebellion and consolidates the gains the Romans have so far made, but does not seek new ones.

52.title: Aqua Claudia
52.body: Claudius completes the Aqua Claudia and Anio Novus, two aqueduct projects started by Caligula

52.title: Ancient Rome
52.body: In Rome a law prohibits the execution of old and crippled slaves.

53.title: Tiridates I of Armenia
53.body: Tiridates I, brother of Vologases I, comes to power in Armenia as an adversary of the Romans.

53.title: Claudius
53.body: Emperor Claudius removes Herod Agrippa II from the tetrarchy of Chalcis in Greece.

53.title: Evodius
53.body: Evodius succeeds Saint Peter as Patriarch of Antioch.

53.title: Nero
53.body: Nero is adopted by Claudius as his son and marries his 14-year-old daughter Claudia Octavia.

53.title: Paul the Apostle
53.body: Paul the Apostle writes his epistle to the Galatians from Ephesus (approximate date)

54.title: Legio X Fretensis
54.body: Corbulo inspects a base of Legio X Fretensis in Syria, at Cyrrhus; the Roman legionaries are demoralized by a "long peace". Many soldiers sell their helmets and shields.

54.title: Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo
54.body: Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo arrives in the East and takes up an assignment as governor of Asia, with a secret brief from Nero and his chief ministers, Seneca and Burrus, to return Armenia to the Roman Empire.

54.title: Centurion
54.body: Two centurions are sent to the south of Egypt to find the source of the Nile, and possible new provinces. They report that while there are many cities in the desert, the area seems too poor to be worthy of conquest.

54.title: Claudius
54.body: Emperor Claudius dies (possibly after being poisoned by Agrippina, his wife and niece), and is succeeded by Nero.

54.title: Ancient Rome
54.body: Under Nero, Rome annexes Aden to protect the maritime route between Alexandria and Asia.

55.title: Nero
55.body: Emperor Nero becomes a Roman Consul.

55.title: Paul of Tarsus
55.body: The apostle Paul writes his First Epistle to the Corinthians.

55.title: Masurius Sabinus
55.body: The Roman jurist Sabinus writes three books on the rights of citizens.

56.title: Imperial Rome
56.body: War between Rome and Parthia breaks out due to the invasion of Armenia by King Vologases I, who has replaced the Roman-supported ruler with his brother Tiridates I of Armenia. (approximate date)

56.title: Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus
56.body: Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus becomes a consul in Rome.

56.title: Jianwu
56.body: The Jianwu era of the Eastern Han dynasty changes to the Jianwuzhongyuan era.

56.title: Epistle to the Romans
56.body: The apostle Paul writes his Epistle to the Romans, from Corinth.

56.title: Apostle Paul
56.body: The apostle Paul writes his second Epistle to the Corinthians, probably from Philippi.

57.title: Cilicia
57.body: Envoys from Cilicia come to Rome to accuse their late governor, Cossutianus Capito, of extortion; the Roman Senate is supported in the case by Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus.

57.title: Nero
57.body: Emperor Nero becomes a Roman consul again.

57.title: Roman Britain
57.body: In Britain, Quintus Veranius Nepos becomes governor in place of Aulus Didius Gallus. He begins a campaign against the Silures of south Wales.

57.title: Emperor Guangwu of Han
57.body: Emperor Guang Wu grants Nakoku (located around modern-day Fukuoka City) a golden seal, being the oldest evidence of writing in Japan. In return King Na sends an envoy to China.

57.title: Talhae of Silla
57.body: Accession of King Talhae as Korean ruler of Silla.

58.title: Volandum
58.body: Roman-Parthian War: Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo, commander in the East, launches his Armenian offensive against Parthia. He leads a Roman army (four legions) through the mountainous country of Armenia, against the fortress at Volandum, to the southwest of Artaxata. After a siege of eight hours Corbulo takes the city; the legionnaires massacre the defenders and plunder Volandum to their hearts' content.

58.title: Roman Empire
58.body: Romans learn the use of soap from the Gauls (approximate date).

58.title: Paul of Tarsus
58.body: The apostle Paul returns to Jerusalem with the money he has collected to give the Christian community there. However, he is accused of defiling the temple, and is arrested and imprisoned in Caesarea. He then invokes his Roman citizenship and is sent to Rome to be judged.

58.title: Nero
58.body: Emperor Nero and Marcus Valerius Messalla Corvinus become Roman consuls.

58.title: Agrippina the Younger
58.body: Agrippina the Younger is expelled from the imperial palace by her son Nero, who installs her in Villa Antonia in Misenum, and leaving more of the effective and real power of Empire in the hands of Nero.

59.title: Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo
59.body: Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo, Roman commander in the east, captures Tigranocerta in Mesopotamia. He installs Tigranes VI, a Cappadocian prince, as ruler of Armenia. For the next four years, a cohort from Legio VI Ferrata and Legio X Fretensis is stationed in the capital as bodyguard to the king, supported by fifteen hundred auxiliaries.

59.title: You almost persuade me to be a Christian
59.body: Paul the Apostle pleads his case and testifies to his Christianity before King Agrippa II of the Herodians, who responds "You almost persuade me to be a Christian."

59.title: Nero
59.body: Emperor Nero orders the murder of his mother Agrippina the Younger. He tries to kill her through a planned shipwreck, but when she survives, he has her executed and frames it as a suicide.

59.title: Pompeii
59.body: Rioting breaks out between the people of Pompeii and the people of Nuceria during a gladiator fight in Pompeii. Thousands are killed.

59.title: Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus
59.body: Publius Clodius Thrasea Paetus retires from the Roman Senate. He openly shows his disgust at the behaviour of Nero regarding the murder of Agrippina.

60.title: Roxolani
60.body: The Roxolani are defeated on the Danube by the Romans.

60.title: Roman Britain
60.body: The following events in Roman Britain (Britannia) take place in AD 60 or 61: Gaius Suetonius Paulinus, Roman governor of Britain, captures the island of Mona (Anglesey), the last stronghold of the Druids. Prasutagus, king of the Iceni (modern East Anglia), dies leaving a will which passes his kingdom to his two daughters and the Roman Empire. The Roman army, however, annexes the kingdom as if conquered, depriving the nobles of their hereditary lands and plundering the land.

60.title: Prasutagus
60.body: Prasutagus, king of the Iceni (modern East Anglia), dies leaving a will which passes his kingdom to his two daughters and the Roman Empire. The Roman army, however, annexes the kingdom as if conquered, depriving the nobles of their hereditary lands and plundering the land. The king's widow, Boudica, is flogged and forced to watch their daughters publicly raped.

60.title: Paul of Tarsus
60.body: Paul of Tarsus journeys to Rome, but is shipwrecked on Malta. He stays for three months and converts Publius, the first bishop of Malta.

60.title: Nero's exploration of the Nile
60.body: Nero's exploration of the Nile: Emperor Nero sends an expedition which reaches the historical city Meroë (Sudan). (approximate date)

61.title: Roman Britain
61.body: The following events in Roman Britain (Britannia) take place in AD 60 or 61: Gaius Suetonius Paulinus, Roman governor of Britain, captures the island of Mona (Anglesey), the last stronghold of the Druids. Prasutagus, king of the Iceni (modern East Anglia), dies leaving a will which passes his kingdom to his two daughters and the Roman Empire. The Roman army however annexes the kingdom as if conquered, depriving the nobles of their hereditary lands and plundering the land.

61.title: Prasutagus
61.body: Prasutagus, king of the Iceni (modern East Anglia), dies leaving a will which passes his kingdom to his two daughters and the Roman Empire. The Roman army however annexes the kingdom as if conquered, depriving the nobles of their hereditary lands and plundering the land. The king's widow, Boudica, is flogged and forced to watch their daughters publicly raped.

61.title: Gaius Suetonius Paulinus
61.body: Gaius Suetonius Paulinus, Roman governor of Britain, captures the island of Mona (Anglesey), the last stronghold of the Druids.

61.title: Battle of Watling Street
61.body: Battle of Watling Street: Paulinus defeats the rebels, using a flying wedge formation, and imposes wide-ranging punishments on native Britons, but is removed from office after an enquiry instituted by Gaius Julius Alpinus Classicianus (appointed procurator 61) and the Romanisation of Britain continues. Boudica either poisons herself or falls sick and dies.

61.title: Trinovantes
61.body: Boudica leads a rebellion of the Iceni against Roman rule in alliance with the Trinovantes, Cornovii, Durotriges and Celtic Britons. The Iceni and Trinovantes first destroy the Roman capital Camulodunum (Colchester), wipe out the infantry of the Legio IX Hispana (commanded by Quintus Petillius Cerialis) and go on to burn Londinium (London) (probably destroying London Bridge) and Verulamium (St Albans), in all cases massacring the inhabitants in their thousands.

62.title: Lucan
62.body: Lucan writes a history of the conflict between Julius Caesar and Pompey.

62.title: Armenia
62.body: The Parthians invade Armenia and lay siege to Tigranocerta. The city is well-fortified and garrisoned by the Romans. The assault fails and king Vologases I retreats.

62.title: Battle of Rhandeia
62.body: Battle of Rhandeia: The Roman army (two legions) is defeated by the Parthians under king Tiridates I. Paetus surrenders and withdraws his disheveled army to Syria.

62.title: Nero
62.body: Emperor Nero marries for the second time, to Poppaea Sabina, ex-wife of Marcus Salvius Otho.

62.title: Paul of Tarsus
62.body: Paul of Tarsus is released from imprisonment in Rome (approximate date).

63.title: Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo
63.body: Gnaeus Domitius Corbulo is restored to command after the Roman debacle at the Battle of Rhandeia. He invades Armenia and defeats Tiridates I, who accepts Roman sovereignty. Parthia withdraws from the war.

63.title: Joseph of Arimathea
63.body: According to legend, Joseph of Arimathea goes to Glastonbury on the first Christian mission to Britain.

63.title: Pompeii
63.body: Pompeii, the city at the foot of Mount Vesuvius, builds a new monumental forum, after the town is heavily damaged by a strong earthquake.

63.title: Vespasian
63.body: Vespasian becomes governor of Africa (estimated year).

64.title: Kushan Empire
64.body: The Kushan sack the ancient town of Taxila (in modern-day Pakistan).

64.title: Paul the Apostle
64.body: Paul leaves Titus in Crete as bishop (approximate date) Then goes to Asia Minor

64.title: Lyon
64.body: Lyon sends a large sum of money to Rome to aid in the reconstruction. However, during the winter of AD 64–65, Lyon suffers a catastrophic fire itself, and Nero reciprocates by sending money to Lyon.

64.title: Seneca the Younger
64.body: Seneca proclaims the equality of all men, including slaves.

64.title: Great Fire of Rome
64.body: Great Fire of Rome: A fire begins which destroyed three of fourteen of the administrative regions of Rome, more commonly known as the Palatine Hill, the Circus Maximus, and the Oppian hill. Also suffering severe damage were the Campus Martius and the Via Lata.

65.title: Christians
65.body: The first Christian community in Africa is founded by Mark, a disciple of Peter.

65.title: Milichus
65.body: The freedman Milichus betrays the Pisonian conspiracy led by Gaius Calpurnius Piso to kill Emperor Nero and all the conspirators are arrested.

65.title: Buddhism
65.body: In China, the first official reference to Buddhism is made.

65.title: Seneca the Younger
65.body: After a stage performance in which he appears and shocks the senatorial class considerably, Nero engages in a series of reprisals against Seneca the Younger and Tigellinus, pro-republican senators, and anyone else he distrusts.

65.title: Paul of Tarsus
65.body: Paul of Tarsus ordains Timothy as bishop of Ephesus (traditional date).

66.title: November – Battle of Beth-Horon
66.body: November – Battle of Beth-Horon: Gallus abandons the siege of Jerusalem and chooses, for uncertain reasons, to withdraw west to winter quarters, where he is ambushed and defeated by Judean rebels. Some 5,300 Roman troops are killed, as well as all their pack animals, their artillery (which is to serve the Jews of Jerusalem during Titus's siege operations four years later), and the greatest disgrace of all, the eagle standard of Legio XII Fulminata. Gallus abandons his troops in disarray, fleeing to Syria.

66.title: First Jewish–Roman War
66.body: October – The Jewish Revolt commences against the Roman Empire. The Zealots lay siege to Jerusalem and annihilate the Roman garrison (a cohort of Legio III Cyrenaica). The Sicarii capture the fortress of Masada overlooking the Dead Sea.

66.title: Cestius Gallus
66.body: Mid–late October – Cestius Gallus, legate of Syria, marches into Judea and leads a Roman army of 30,000 men to put down the Jewish rebellion. At its core is Legio XII Fulminata, plus 2,000 selected men from the other three Syrian legions, six more cohorts of infantry and four alae of cavalry, plus over 14,000 auxiliaries furnished by Rome's eastern allies, including Herod Agrippa II and two other client kings, Antiochus IV of Commagene and Sohaemus of Emesa, who lead their forces (largely archers and cavalry) in person.

66.title: Nero
66.body: Emperor Nero creates the Legio I Italica. He appoints Titus Flavius Vespasian as General of the army of Judea, and Governor of Judea which gives him command of three legions — V Macedonica, X Fretensis and XV Apollinaris.

67.title: Gaius Licinius Mucianus
67.body: Gaius Licinius Mucianus replaces Gaius Cestius Gallus as governor of Syria.

67.title: Jerusalem
67.body: Jewish leaders at Jerusalem are divided through a power struggle, and a brutal civil war erupts. The Zealots and the Sicarii execute anyone who tries to leave the city.

67.title: Greece
67.body: Nero travels to Greece, where he participates in the Olympic Games and other festivals.

67.title: Saint Peter
67.body: Apostles Peter and Paul are martyred in Rome (possible date).

67.title: Pope Linus
67.body: Linus succeeds Peter, as the second Bishop of Rome.

68.title: Annals
68.body: Final year that Tacitus records Annals, a written history of the Roman Empire.

68.title: Titus
68.body: Winter – Titus sets up camp at Jericho and the Romans cut off escape routes toward Jerusalem.

68.title: Saint Peter
68.body: Apostles Peter and Paul are martyred in Rome (latest possible date).

68.title: Nero
68.body: Emperor Nero commits suicide four miles outside Rome. He is deserted by the Praetorian Guard, and then stabs himself in the throat.

68.title: Buddhism
68.body: Buddhism officially arrives in China with the building of the White Horse Temple.

69.title: Batavian rebellion
69.body: Batavian rebellion: The Batavians in Germania Inferior (Netherlands) revolt under the leadership of Gaius Julius Civilis.

69.title: Judea
69.body: Judea: The Jewish Revolt – Vespasian lays siege to Jerusalem; the city is captured the following year by his son Titus.

69.title: The Year of the Four Emperors
69.body: The Year of the Four Emperors: After Nero's death, Galba, Otho, Vitellius and Vespasian succeed each other as emperor during the year. The year is marked by numerous instances of a breakdown in discipline and mutinous conduct amongst the Roman legions and the praetorian guard.

69.title: Tiberius Julius Alexander
69.body: Tiberius Julius Alexander orders his legions in Alexandria to swear allegiance to Vespasian as emperor.

69.title: Ancient Rome
69.body: Otho seizes power in Rome, proclaims himself emperor, and reigns for three months before committing suicide.

70.title: Punitive expedition
70.body: Romans make a punitive expedition against the Garamantes – they are forced to have an official relationship with the Roman Empire.

70.title: testudos
70.body: About April 21 – Titus opens a full-scale assault on Jerusalem, concentrating his attack on the city's Third Wall (HaHoma HaShlishit) to the northwest. The Roman army begins trying to breach the wall using testudos, mantlets, siege towers, and battering rams.

70.title: Macedonica
70.body: 14th of Xanthikos (14th of Nisan, about April 14) – Siege of Jerusalem: Titus surrounds the Jewish capital, with three legions (V Macedonica, XII Fulminata and XV Apollinaris) on the western side and a fourth (X Fretensis) on the Mount of Olives to the east. He puts pressure on the food and water supplies of the inhabitants by allowing pilgrims to enter the city to celebrate Passover and then refusing them egress.

70.title: Egypt
70.body: Expedition by the Roman Septimius Flaccus to southern Egypt. He probably reaches Sudan.

70.title: Pentecost
70.body: Pentecost (Shavuot, 6th of Sivan, about June 4) – Priests in the Temple in Jerusalem feel a quaking and hear "a sound as of a great multitude saying, Let us remove hence".

71.title: Rabbel
71.body: Reign of Rabel II, king of Nabataea. He makes Bostra, Syria, his second capital.

71.title: Legio IX Hispana
71.body: The Romans establish a fortress at York (Eboracum), as a base for their northern forces. Initially established solely for Legio IX Hispana, it expands later to include public housing, baths and temples.

71.title: Mithraism
71.body: Mithraism begins to spread throughout the Roman Empire.

71.title: Legio X Fretensis
71.body: Herodium, a Jewish fortress south of Jerusalem, is conquered and destroyed by Legio X Fretensis on their way to Masada.

71.title: Battle of Stanwick
71.body: Battle of Stanwick: Quintus Petillius Cerialis, governor of Britain, puts down a revolt by the Brigantes.

72.title: Roman Empire
72.body: The Romans lay siege to Masada, a desert fortress held by the Sicarii.

72.title: Colosseum
72.body: Vespasian starts the building of the Colosseum; the amphitheatre is used for gladiatorial games and public spectacles, such as sea battles, re-enactments of famous battles, and dramas of Classical mythology.

72.title: Legio X Fretensis
72.body: First Jewish–Roman War: The Roman army (Legio X Fretensis) under Sextus Lucilius Bassus lays siege to the Jewish garrison of Machaerus at the Dead Sea. After they capitulate, the Zealots are allowed to leave the fortress before it is destroyed.

72.title: Antiochus IV of Syria
72.body: Antiochus IV of Syria is deposed by Emperor Vespasian.

72.title: Titus
72.body: Vespasian and Titus are Roman Consuls.

73.title: Ban Chao
73.body: Ban Chao (Pan-Ch’ao), competing with the Xiongnu, imposes a Chinese protectorate on the kings of Lop Nor and Khotan in the Tarim Basin, with the aim of controlling the Silk Road.

73.title: Vespasian
73.body: Emperor Vespasian begins conquest of territory east of the upper Rhine and south of the Main. In addition, he reorganizes the defenses of the upper and lower Danube.

73.title: Han dynasty
73.body: February – The Chinese Han dynasty launches a major campaign against the Xiongnu, whom they confront in the Battle of Yiwulu in the Kumul oasis, an ultimate Han military victory led by General Dou Gu (d. AD 88).

73.title: Domitian
73.body: Titus Flavius Domitianus becomes Roman Consul.

73.title: Pliny the Elder
73.body: Pliny the Elder serves as procurator in Hispania Tarraconensis.

74.title: Black Forest
74.body: The Black Forest region is reattached to the Roman Empire.

74.title: Mesopotamia
74.body: Mesopotamia: The last known cuneiform text is written.

74.title: Vespasian
74.body: Emperor Vespasian and his son Titus Caesar Vespasianus become Roman Consuls.

74.title: Dou
74.body: Chinese generals Dou Gu (Teou Kou) and Geng Bing (Keng Ping) take control of Turpan.

74.title: Emperor Ming of Han
74.body: Emperor Ming of Han appoints Chen Mu to the revived office of Protector of the Western Regions.

75.title: Forum of Vespasian
75.body: The Temple of Peace, also known as the Forum of Vespasian, is built in Rome. The temple celebrates the conquest of Jerusalem (in AD 70) and houses the Menorah from Herod's Temple.

75.title: Armazi
75.body: Vespasian fortifies Armazi (Georgia) for the Iberian king Mithridates I. The Alans raid the Roman frontier in Armenia.

75.title: Sextus Julius Frontinus
75.body: Sextus Julius Frontinus becomes governor of Britannia and makes his headquarters in Isca Augusta (Wales).

75.title: Emperor Zhang of Han
75.body: Accession of Han Zhangdi of the Han dynasty (until AD 88).

75.title: Revolt against the Chinese in Tarim
75.body: Revolt against the Chinese in Tarim: Cachera and Turpan are besieged. Luoyang orders the evacuation of Tarim. Ban Chao makes the rebels retreat towards Khotan.

76.title: Pope Anacletus
76.body: Pope Anacletus I succeeds Pope Linus as the third pope of the Catholic Church (according to the official Vatican list).

76.title: Augusta
76.body: Governor Sextus Julius Frontinus subdues the Silures and other hostile tribes of Wales, establishing a fortress at Caerleon or Isca Augusta for Legio II Augusta, and makes a network of smaller forts for his auxiliary forces.

76.title: Jianchu
76.body: First year of Jianchu era of the Chinese Han dynasty. (Clarification needed as to the meaning of this)

76.title: Vespasian
76.body: Emperors Vespasianus Augustus and Titus Caesar Vespasianus become Roman Consuls.

76.title: Ban
76.body: Chinese historian Ban Gu develops a theory of the origins of the universe.

77.title: Gnaeus Julius Agricola
77.body: Gnaeus Julius Agricola is named governor of Britannia, a post he occupies until AD 84. He extends the Roman influence to the mouth of the River Clyde (Scotland), and builds fortifications.

77.title: Ordovices
77.body: Agricola subdues the Ordovices in Wales, and pursues the remnants of the tribe to Anglesey, the holy island of the Druids.

77.title: Giru of Baekje
77.body: King Giru of Baekje succeeds to the throne of Baekje in the Korean Peninsula.

77.title: Naturalis Historia
77.body: Pliny the Elder publishes the first ten books of Naturalis Historia.

77.title: Caledonians
77.body: The Caledonian tribes in Scotland form a confederacy of 30,000 warriors, under the leadership of Calgacus.

78.title: Sanskrit
78.body: Indian Prince Aji Caka introduces the Sanskrit language and Pallawa script, used to inscribe Javanese words and phrases, to the Indonesian islands.

78.title: Roman Empire
78.body: The Romans conquer the Ordovices, located in present-day northern Wales, as well as the Silures.

78.title: Year zero
78.body: This is the base year (year zero) of the Saka era used by some Hindu calendars, the Indian national calendar, and the Cambodian Buddhist calendar. It begins near the vernal equinox for the civil solar calendar, but begins opposite the star Spica for the traditional solar calendar.

78.title: Pacorus II of Parthia
78.body: Pacorus II becomes king of the Parthian Empire (r. 78–115).

78.title: Kujula Kadphises
78.body: Emperor Kadphises of the Kushan Empire sends a delegation to Rome, to seek support against the Parthians.

79.title: Diarrhea
79.body: Emperor Vespasian dies of fever from diarrhea; his last words on his deathbed are: "I think I'm turning into a god." Titus succeeds his father as Roman emperor. Titus' Jewish mistress, Berenice, comes to join him in Rome, but he exiles her to please the Senate.

79.title: Eruption of Mount Vesuvius in 79
79.body: August 24 or c. October 24 – Eruption of Mount Vesuvius: Mount Vesuvius erupts, destroying Pompeii, Herculaneum, Stabiae and Oplontis. The Roman navy (based at Misenum), commanded by Pliny the Elder, evacuates refugees.

79.title: castrum
79.body: Roman conquest of Britain: Gnaeus Julius Agricola campaigns in Britain: Chester is founded as a castrum or Roman fort with the name Deva Victrix. The fortress is built by Legio II Adiutrix and contains barracks, granaries, military baths and headquarters. Mamucium (the first Manchester) is founded as a frontier fort and settlement in the North West of England, a distance to the north of Chester.

79.title: Mamucium
79.body: Mamucium (the first Manchester) is founded as a frontier fort and settlement in the North West of England, a distance to the north of Chester.

79.title: Confucius
79.body: A commission of scholars canonizes the text of works of Confucius and his school.

80.title: Pasa of Silla
80.body: King Pasa becomes ruler of the Korean kingdom of Silla.

81.title: Domitian
81.body: Domitian succeeds his brother Titus as emperor. Domitian is not a soldier like his two predecessors, and his administration is directed towards the reinforcement of a monarchy. By taking the title of Dominus ("lord"), he scandalizes the senatorial aristocracy.

81.title: Silver
81.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius rises to 92% under emperor Domitian, up from 81% in the reign of Vitellius.

81.title: flamen Divi Augusti
81.body: Pliny the Younger is flamen Divi Augusti (priest in the cult of the Emperor).

81.title: Arch of Titus
81.body: The Arch of Titus is constructed.

81.title: First Epistle of Peter
81.body: Possible date of the First Epistle of Peter.

82.title: Dio Chrysostom
82.body: Dio Chrysostom is banished from Rome, Italy, and Bithynia, after advising one of the Emperor's conspiring relatives.

82.title: Hispana
82.body: Gnaeus Julius Agricola raises a fleet, and encircles the Celtic tribes beyond the Forth; the Caledonians rise in great numbers against the Romans. They attack the camp of Legio IX Hispana at night, but Agricola sends his cavalry in, and puts them to flight.

82.title: Calgacus
82.body: Calgacus unites the Picts (30,000 men) in Scotland, and is made chieftain of the Caledonian Confederacy.

83.title: Possible date of the Battle of Mons Graupius (AD 83 or 84)
83.body: Possible date of the Battle of Mons Graupius (AD 83 or 84): According to Tacitus, 10,000 Britons and 360 Romans are killed.

83.title: Castra
83.body: The Roman fort Inchtuthil is built in Scotland.

83.title: Ancient Rome
83.body: In Rome, the castration of slaves is prohibited.

83.title: Domitian
83.body: Emperor Domitian fights the Chatti, a Germanic tribe. His victory allows the construction of fortifications (Limes) along the Rhine-frontier.

83.title: Hebrides
83.body: Possible date that Demetrius of Tarsus visits an island in the Hebrides populated by holy men, possibly druids.

84.title: Limes
84.body: The construction of the limes, a line of Roman fortifications from the Rhine to the Danube, has begun.

84.title: Domitian
84.body: Emperor Domitian recalls Agricola back to Rome, where he is rewarded with a triumph and the governorship of the Roman province of Africa, but he declines it.

84.title: Jianchu
84.body: Change from Jianchu to Yuanhe era of the Chinese Eastern Han Dynasty.

84.title: sevir equitum Romanorum
84.body: Pliny the Younger is sevir equitum Romanorum (commander of a cavalry squadron).

84.title: Battle of Mons Graupius
84.body: Possible date of the Battle of Mons Graupius (AD 83 or 84), in which Gnaeus Julius Agricola defeats the Caledonians.

85.title: Dacia
85.body: Dacians under Decebalus engage in two wars against the Romans from this year to AD 88 or 89.

85.title: Domitian
85.body: Emperor Domitian repulses a Dacian invasion of Moesia.

85.title: Roman censor
85.body: Domitian appoints himself censor for life, which gives him the right to control the Senate. His totalitarian tendencies put the senatorial aristocracy firmly in opposition to him.

85.title: Baekje
85.body: Baekje invades the outskirts of Silla in the Korean peninsula. The war continues until the peace treaty of 105.

86.title: First Battle of Tapae
86.body: First Battle of Tapae: Roman legions face disaster in Dacia, when Roman general Cornelius Fuscus launches a powerful offensive that becomes a failure. Encircled in the valley of Timi, he dies along with his entire army. Rome must pay tribute to the Dacians in exchange for a vague recognition of Rome's importance.

86.title: Ban
86.body: Ban Gu (Pan Kou) and his sister Ban Zhao (Pan Tchao) compose the History of China.

86.title: Trajan
86.body: Roman general (and future emperor) Trajan begins a campaign to crush an uprising in Germania.

86.title: Domitian
86.body: Emperor Domitian introduces the Capitoline Games.

86.title: Germania
86.body: Germania is divided into two provinces, Germania Inferior and Germania Superior (approximate date).

87.title: Lyon
87.body: Lyon, a city in Gaul, has a population of over 100,000 citizens (approximate date).

87.title: Sextus Julius Sparsus
87.body: Sextus Julius Sparsus gains power in the Roman Senate (approximate date).

87.title: Romans in Sub-Saharan Africa
87.body: The Roman Julius Maternus explores western Africa (approximate date).

88.title: The First Dacian War ends
88.body: The First Dacian War ends: Decebalus becomes a client king of Rome, he receives money, craftsmen and war machines to protect the borders (limes) of the Roman Empire.

88.title: Pope Clement
88.body: Pope Clement I succeeds Pope Anacletus I as the fourth pope.

88.title: Emperor Zhang of Han
88.body: Emperor Han Zhangdi dies at age 31 after a 13-year reign in which Chinese military forces have become powerful enough to march against tribes who threaten their northern and western borders. Having used intrigue as well as armed might to achieve his ends, Zhangdi and his General Ban Chao have reestablished Chinese influence in Inner Asia, but court eunuchs have increased their power during the emperor's reign. Zhangdi is succeeded by his 9-year-old son Zhao, who will reign until 105 as emperor Han Hedi, but he will be a virtual pawn of Empress Dou (adoptive mother) and scheming courtiers who will effectively rule the Chinese Empire.

88.title: Obelisk
88.body: Two Egyptian obelisks are erected in Benevento in front of the Temple of Isis, in honour of Emperor Domitian.

88.title: Institutio Oratoria
88.body: Quintilian retires from teaching and from pleading, to compose his great work on the training of the orator (Institutio Oratoria).

89.title: Aquincum
89.body: Aquincum (old Budapest, Óbuda) is founded (approximate date).

89.title: Yongyuan
89.body: First year of Yongyuan era of the Chinese Han dynasty.

89.title: Lucius Antonius Saturninus
89.body: Lucius Antonius Saturninus incites a revolt against Emperor Domitian (it is suppressed by January 24).

89.title: June – Battle of Ikh Bayan
89.body: June – Battle of Ikh Bayan: The Han Chinese army under Dou Xian, allied with the southern Xiongnu, is victorious over the Northern Xiongnu.

89.title: Polycarpus I of Byzantium
89.body: Polycarpus I, Greek bishop of Byzantium, dies after a 20-year reign and is succeeded by Plutarch.

90.title: Young Flavian Woman
90.body: The Young Flavian Woman is made. It is now kept at Musei Capitolini, Rome (approximate date).

91.title: Ancient Rome
91.body: Rome is described by Statius in his poems.

91.title: Manius Acilius Glabrio
91.body: Manius Acilius Glabrio and Marcus Ulpius Traianus become Roman Consuls.

91.title: tribunus plebis
91.body: Pliny the Younger is named a tribunus plebis.

91.title: Ban Chao
91.body: Ban Chao is made protector-general of the Western Regions.

92.title: Roman army
92.body: The Roman army moves into Mesopotamia (modern Syria).

92.title: Domitian
92.body: Emperor Domitian becomes a Roman Consul.

92.title: Rapax
92.body: In spring, several tribes (probably Marcomanni, Quadi, Jazyges) cross the Danube and attack Pannonia, probably destroying Legio XXI Rapax. These tribes are defeated from May to December 92, and chased back over the river. The Romans do not pursue the retreating tribes.

92.title: Flavian Palace
92.body: The Flavian Palace is completed on the Palatine.

93.title: Xianbei
93.body: The Xianbei incorporates 100,000 Xiongnu, and establishes the Xianbei State in Mongolia (approximate date).

93.title: Jewish Antiquities
93.body: Josephus completes his Jewish Antiquities (or in AD 94).

93.title: Pliny the Younger
93.body: Pliny the Younger is named a praetor.

94.title: Stoicism
94.body: Domitian banishes all Stoic philosophers from Rome.

94.title: Ban Chao
94.body: The Chinese General Ban Chao completes his conquest of the Tarim Basin by taking Yānqi, which is located on the strategic Silk Road.

94.title: Statius
94.body: The Roman poet Publius Papinius Statius retires to Naples from Rome (approximate date).

94.title: Domitian
94.body: Emperor Domitian rebuilds and rededicates the Curia Julia (meeting place of the Roman Senate), which had burned down in AD 64.

95.title: Manius Acilius Glabrio
95.body: The Roman consul Manius Acilius Glabrio is ordered by Domitian to descend into the arena of the Colosseum to fight a lion. After he kills the animal, Domitian banishes and puts him to death.

95.title: Book of Revelation
95.body: The Book of Revelation is written (approximate date).

95.title: Ancient Rome
95.body: In Rome a severe form of malaria appears in the farm districts and will continue for the next 500 years, taking out of cultivation the fertile land of the Campagna, whose market gardens supply the city with fresh products. The fever drives small groups of farmers into the crowded city, bringing the malaria with them, and lowers Rome's live-birth rate while rates elsewhere in the empire are rising.

95.title: Domitian
95.body: Emperor Domitian and Titus Flavius Clemens become Roman Consuls.

95.title: Roman Senate
95.body: Domitian executes senators out of paranoiac fears that they are plotting to kill him.

96.title: Nerva
96.body: Nerva is declared emperor by the Roman Senate as the new ruler of the Roman Empire. He recalls citizens exiled by Domitian; this is the beginning of the Era of the Five Good Emperors. The Antonines Dynasty starts.

96.title: Buddhism
96.body: A schism in Buddhism creates a new, popular religion in India, Mahâyâna ("Great Vehicle").

96.title: Book of Revelation
96.body: The Book of Revelation is written (approximate date).

96.title: Arch of Titus
96.body: The Arch of Titus is completed in Rome.

96.title: Trajan
96.body: Marcus Ulpius Traianus becomes governor of Upper Germany.

97.title: nasi
97.body: Nerva recognizes the Sanhedrin of Jamnia as an official governmental body of the Jews, and the patriarch or nasi is designated as the representative of the Jewish people in Rome.

97.title: Nerva
97.body: Emperor Nerva recalls his general Marcus Ulpius Trajanus, age 44, from the German frontier and is forced by the Praetorian Guard to adopt him as his successor.

97.title: Cuicul
97.body: The Roman colony of Cuicul is started in Numidia.

97.title: Pope Evaristus
97.body: Evaristus succeeds Pope Clement I as the fifth pope (according to Catholic tradition; none of the popes until the mid second century is certain).

97.title: curator aquarum
97.body: Sextus Julius Frontinus is appointed superintendent of the aqueducts (curator aquarum) in Rome. At least 10 aqueducts supply the city with 250 million US gallons (950,000 m3) of water per day. The public baths use half the supply.

98.title: Fever
98.body: Nerva dies of a fever at his villa in the Gardens of Sallust and is succeeded by his adopted son Trajan. Trajan is the first Roman Emperor born in Italica, near Seville. A brilliant soldier and administrator, he enters Rome without ceremony and wins over the public.

98.title: Nerva
98.body: Emperor Nerva suffers a stroke during a private audience.

98.title: Silver
98.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius rises to 93 percent under emperor Trajan, up from 92 percent under Domitian.

98.title: civitas
98.body: Trajan elevates Ladenburg to city status (civitas).

99.title: Kushan Empire
99.body: Emissaries of the Kushan Empire reach the Roman Empire.

99.title: Pope Evaristus
99.body: Pope Evaristus succeeds Pope Clement I as the fifth pope according to Roman Catholic tradition.

99.title: Papyrus Oxyrhynchus 581
99.body: Papyrus Oxyrhynchus 581, recording the sale of a slave girl, is written.

100.title: Teotihuacan
100.body: Teotihuacan, a major city at the centre of modern-day Mexico, reaches a population of around 60,000–80,000.

100.title: Roman brick
100.body: Bricks become the primary building material in the Roman Empire.

100.title: Trajan's Market
100.body: Main hall, Trajan's Market, Rome, is made (until AD 112).

100.title: Lion
100.body: Lions have become extinct in Greece by this year.

100.title: Moche culture
100.body: The Moche culture emerges, and starts building a society in present-day Peru.

101.title: Dacia
101.body: Emperor Trajan starts an expedition against Dacia, exceeding the limits of the Roman Empire set by Augustus.

101.title: Third Battle of Tapae
101.body: Third Battle of Tapae: Roman forces, led by Trajan, defeat the Dacian king Decebalus in Transylvania.

101.title: The Discourses of Epictetus
101.body: Epictetus writes and publishes The Discourses of Epictetus (approximate date).

102.title: Trajan
102.body: Emperor Trajan returns to Rome after a successful campaign against Dacia, through which he reestablishes clear Roman sovereignty over King Decebalus.

102.title: Pannonia
102.body: Trajan divides Pannonia into two provinces, sometime between this year and 107.

102.title: Tarim Basin
102.body: Having organised the territories of the Tarim Basin, Chinese General Ban Chao retires to Luoyang and dies shortly thereafter.

102.title: Lucius Julius Ursus Servianus
102.body: Lucius Julius Ursus Servianus and Lucius Licinius Sura become Roman consuls.

102.title: Ostia Antica
102.body: Planning and surveying for the port of Ostia is undertaken.

103.title: Palmyra
103.body: In Palmyra, Syria, a Temple of the Sun is erected to the god Baal.

103.title: Gemina
103.body: Legio X Gemina moves to Vienna, where it remains until the 5th century.

103.title: Trajan
103.body: Emperor Trajan and Manius Laberius Maximus become Roman consuls.

103.title: Pliny the Younger
103.body: Pliny the Younger becomes a member of the college of Augurs (103–104).

104.title: Apollodorus of Damascus
104.body: Apollodorus of Damascus builds a stone bridge over the Danube more than 1,000 meters (3,300 feet) long, almost 20 meters (66 feet) high and 15 meters (49 feet) wide. The bridge connects what is now Serbia with Romania (at the time known as Dacia).

104.title: Ancient Rome
104.body: A fire breaks out in Rome.

104.title: Gautama Buddha
104.body: In India, figures of Buddha replace abstract motifs on decorative items.

104.title: Lacer
104.body: Trajan gives the order to have the Alcántara Bridge, constructed by the architect Lacer, built over the Tagus River at Alcántara (Hispania).

104.title: Pliny the Younger
104.body: Pliny the Younger continues as a member of the College of Augurs (103–104).

105.title: Pope Alexander
105.body: Pope Alexander I succeeds Pope Evaristus as the sixth pope according to Roman Catholic tradition (approximate date).

105.title: castrum
105.body: Permanent castrum of Legio II Adiutrix at Aquincum (modern Budapest) in Pannonia.

105.title: Emperor He of Han
105.body: Emperor He Di dies after a 17-year reign in which court eunuchs and the emperor's in-laws have regained influence. Empress Deng Sui places her son Shang Di (barely 3 months old) on the throne, as the fifth emperor of the Chinese Eastern Han dynasty.

105.title: Trajan
105.body: Emperor Trajan starts the second expedition against Dacia; he leaves with the Imperial Roman fleet from Brundusium.

105.title: Papermaking
105.body: Papermaking is refined by the Chinese eunuch Cai Lun, who receives official praise from the emperor for his methods of making paper from tree bark, hemp, remnant rags, and fish nets. Occasionally, skin from cadavers were also spread as a final layer. Paper had been made in China from the 2nd century BC, but Cai Lun's paper provides a writing surface far superior to pure silk and is much less costly to produce.

106.title: Ignatius of Antioch
106.body: Ignatius writes a letter to Christians in Smyrna, using the term Catholic Church (approximate date). This is the earliest surviving witness to the use of the term "Catholic Church".

106.title: Dacia
106.body: The south-eastern part of Dacia (modern Romania) becomes a Roman province: Roman Dacia. The veterans of the legions are given land in the new province for their service in the Roman army.

106.title: Nabataean Kingdom
106.body: Trajan annexes the Nabataean Kingdom (with its capital Petra) as the Roman province of Arabia Petraea. The epoch of the calendar of the province of Arabia begins on March 22.

106.title: Eilat
106.body: Romans construct a road between Eilat and Damascus.

106.title: Emperor An of Han
106.body: Han Shangdi dies after a 7-month reign and is succeeded by his 12-year-old cousin Han Andi as ruler of the Chinese Eastern Han dynasty (until 125).

107.title: Lucius Licinius Sura
107.body: Lucius Licinius Sura and Quintus Sosius Senecio become consuls of Rome.

107.title: yongchu
107.body: First year of the yongchu era of the Chinese Eastern Han Dynasty.

107.title: Trajan
107.body: An Indian ambassador is received by Emperor Trajan.

107.title: Emperor An of Han
107.body: Han Andi (An-ti) becomes emperor of China.

108.title: Histories
108.body: Tacitus writes Histories, which covers the period from AD 69 to AD 96.

108.title: Hypogeum of Yarhai
108.body: The Hypogeum of Yarhai, an underground tomb from the Syrian city of Palmyra dedicated to the family of Yarhai, is built.

108.title: Appius Annius Trebonius Gallus
108.body: Appius Annius Trebonius Gallus and Marcus Atilius Bradua become Roman Consuls.

109.title: Calends
109.body: The Baths of Trajan, built by the architect Apollodorus of Damascus, are dedicated during the Calends. The thermae are constructed on the platform of the Palace of Nero (Domus Aurea) in Rome.

109.title: Osroes I of Parthia
109.body: Osroes I of Parthia succeeds his brother Pacorus II, and rules over the western Parthian Empire.

109.title: Aqua Traiana
