109.body: The Aqua Traiana is inaugurated by Emperor Trajan; the aqueduct channels water from Lake Bracciano, 40 kilometers (25 mi) northwest of Rome.

109.title: Pliny the Younger
109.body: Pliny the Younger is legate to Bithynia.

109.title: Via Traiana
109.body: The Via Traiana is constructed at the Emperor Trajan's personal expense; the road connects Benevento with Brundisium (Brindisi).

110.title: Roman Empire
110.body: The Roman Empire has more than 75,000 kilometers (47,000 mi) of roads.

110.title: Camel train
110.body: Caravans make regular departures from Luoyang with Chinese ginger, cassia (a type of cinnamon), and silk to be bartered in Central Asia for gold, silver, glassware, pottery, cloth, and intaglio gems from Rome.

110.title: Trajan's Forum
110.body: The Forum of Trajan is constructed in Rome, by the Syrian architect Apollodorus of Damascus.

110.title: Suetonius
110.body: Suetonius, Roman historian, publishes Viris Illustribus ("On Famous Men" – in the field of literature).

111.title: legatus Augusti
111.body: Emperor Trajan sends Pliny the Younger to be governor (legatus Augusti) of Bithynia.

112.title: Hadrian
112.body: Hadrian succeeds Gaius Julius Cassius Steirieus as archon of Athens.

112.title: Trajan
112.body: Emperor Trajan and Titus Sextius Cornelius Africanus become Roman consuls.

112.title: Tacitus
112.body: Tacitus is named proconsul of the Roman province of Asia (112–113).

112.title: Jima of Silla
112.body: King Jima succeeds Pasa as ruler of the Korean kingdom of Silla.

112.title: Salonia Matidia
112.body: Salonia Matidia receives the title of Augusta upon the death of Marciana.

113.title: Mangala Devi Kannagi Temple
113.body: "Pattini dheivam" worship is inaugurated in Kannagi Temple in the Chera Kingdom in India, by Emperor Cenkuttuvan; the function is attended by GajaBahu, king of Central Sri Lanka (Mahavamso).

113.title: Trajan's Column
113.body: Trajan's Column near the Colosseum in Rome is completed to commemorate the Emperor's victory over the Dacians in the Second Dacian War.

113.title: Trajan
113.body: Emperor Trajan sails from Rome to begin his expedition against Parthia. He arrives in Athens where Parthian envoys greet him with olive branches, a sign of peace.

113.title: Yongchu
113.body: Last (7th) year of Yongchu era of the Chinese Eastern Han Dynasty.

113.title: Osroes I of Parthia
113.body: Osroes I of Parthia violates the treaty with Rome by installing a puppet ruler in Armenia. Emperor Trajan marches east, without first attempting to use diplomacy to resolve the disagreement.

114.title: Osroene
114.body: The kingdom of Osroene becomes a vassal kingdom of the Roman Empire.

114.title: Trajan
114.body: Emperor Trajan defeats the Parthians and overruns Armenia and northern Mesopotamia.

114.title: Philopappos
114.body: A monument to Philopappos, prince-in-exile of old Commagene (a buffer-state between Rome and Parthia) is erected in Athens.

114.title: Yuanchu
114.body: First year of Yuanchu era of the Chinese Eastern Han dynasty.

114.title: Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople
114.body: Change of Patriarch of Constantinople from Patriarch Sedecion to Patriarch Diogenes.

115.title: 115 Antioch earthquake
115.body: An earthquake destroys Apamea and Antioch in Syria. The local bishop is held responsible (he will be martyred and remembered as St. Ignatius).

115.title: Trajan
115.body: Emperor Trajan is cut off in southern Mesopotamia after his invasion of that region.

115.title: Pantheon, Rome
115.body: The Pantheon of Agrippa is reconstructed in Rome.

115.title: Great Britain
115.body: A revolt breaks out in Britain; the garrison at Eboracum (York) is massacred.

115.title: Alexandria
115.body: Alexandria in Egypt is damaged during the Jewish-Greek civil wars. Marcus Rutilius Lupus, the Roman governor, sends Legio XXII Deiotariana to protect the inhabitants of Memphis.

116.title: Legio III Cyrenaica
116.body: Trajan sends two expeditionary forces. One, consisting of elements of Legio III Cyrenaica, to suppress the revolt in Judea and the other Legio VII Claudia to restore order on Cyprus.

116.title: Syria
116.body: Trajan makes Syria a province of Rome and crosses the Tigris to annex Adiabene. He proceeds with his army to the Persian Gulf and conquers territory that becomes the province of Parthia.

116.title: Trajan
116.body: Emperor Trajan completes his invasion of Parthia by capturing the cities of Seleucia, Babylon, Ctesiphon and Susa, marking the high-water mark of the Roman Empire's eastern expansion.

116.title: Quintus Marcius Turbo
116.body: Quintus Marcius Turbo sails to Alexandria and defeats Jewish insurgents in several pitched battles.

116.title: laureatae
116.body: Trajan sends laureatae to the Roman Senate on account of his victories and being conqueror of Parthia.

117.title: Trajan
117.body: Emperor Trajan falls seriously ill and dies of a stroke at Selinus in Cilicia, age 63, while en route from Mesopotamia to Italy, leaving the Roman Empire at its maximal territorial extent.

117.title: Mesopotamia
117.body: Hadrian returns large parts of Mesopotamia to the Parthians as part of a peace settlement. He inaugurates a policy of retrenchment and cultural integration, giving up the policy of conquest of his predecessor in order to consolidate the empire.

117.title: Pantheon, Rome
117.body: Construction begins on the Pantheon in Rome.

117.title: Hadrian
117.body: On his death bed, Trajan allegedly adopts Hadrian, a fellow Spaniard, and designates him as his successor. Hadrian will reign until 138.

117.title: Silver
117.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius falls to 87 percent under emperor Hadrian, down from 93 percent in the reign of Trajan.

118.title: Osroene
118.body: Osroene is returned to native rule by the Roman Empire.

118.title: Pantheon, Rome
118.body: Pantheon, in Rome, starts to be built (approximate date).

118.title: Huns
118.body: The north-south feud between the Hun Dynasty ends.

118.title: Hadrian
118.body: Emperor Hadrian is also a Roman Consul.

118.title: Ancient Rome
118.body: Rome has a population exceeding 1 million, making it the largest city in the world.

119.title: Great Britain
119.body: Hadrian also visits Britain in this year at the request of governor of Britain Quintus Pompeius Falco.

119.title: Nahapana
119.body: Reign in Northern India by Nahapana, Scythian king. He attacks the kingdom of Andhra and annexes Southern Rajputana.

119.title: Victrix
119.body: Emperor Hadrian stations Legio VI Victrix in Roman Britain, to assist in quelling the resistance of a local rebellion. The legion is key in securing the victory, and eventually replaces Legio IX Hispana at Eboracum.

119.title: Salonia Matidia
119.body: Salonia Matidia (a niece of former Emperor Trajan) dies. Hadrian delivers her funeral oration, and grants her a temple in Rome.

120.title: The Scythians dominate western India
120.body: The Scythians dominate western India: Punjab, Sind, the north of Gujarat and a portion of central India.

120.title: Yuanchu
120.body: Change of era name from Yuanchu (7th year) to Yongning of the Chinese Eastern Han dynasty.

120.title: Legio IX Hispana
120.body: Approximate date Legio IX Hispana last known to be in existence. The Market Gate of Miletus is built at Miletos (moved in modern times to Staatliche Museen zu Berlin, Preußischer Kulturbesitz, Antikensammlung).

120.title: ab epistolis
120.body: Suetonius becomes Hadrian's secretary ab epistolis.

121.title: Hadrian
121.body: Emperor Hadrian fixes the border between Roman Britain and Caledonia, on a line running from the River Tyne to the Solway Firth.

121.title: Temple of Venus and Roma
121.body: Construction of the Temple of Venus and Roma begins in Rome.

121.title: Yongning
121.body: Era name changes from Yongning (2nd year) to Jianguang in the Chinese Eastern Han dynasty.(Needs clarification or deletion)

121.title: Wiesbaden
121.body: Roman settlement in present-day Wiesbaden, Germany, is first mentioned.

122.title: Hadrian
122.body: Emperor Hadrian orders that a 73-mile (117-kilometer) wall be built to mark the northern Roman Empire while personally visiting the area. Hadrian's Wall, as it comes to be known, is intended to keep the Caledonians, Picts and other tribes at bay.

122.title: Scotland
122.body: Hadrian gives up the territories conquered in Scotland.

122.title: Jianguang
122.body: Change of era name from Jianguang (2nd year) to Yanguang of the Chinese Eastern Han dynasty.

122.title: castrum
122.body: Vindolanda, a Roman auxiliary fort (castrum) in northern England, is garrisoned by cohort VIII Batavorum.

122.title: AD 122
122.body: The building of Hadrian's Wall begins.

123.title: Berbers
123.body: Hadrian leads a punitive campaign against Berbers who had been raiding Roman towns in Roman Mauretania.

123.title: Historia Augusta
123.body: Emperor Hadrian averts a war with Parthia by a personal meeting with Osroes I (according to the Historia Augusta, disputed).

123.title: Vercovicium
123.body: Housesteads Fort is constructed on Hadrian's Wall north of Bardon Mill.

123.title: Zhang Heng
123.body: Chinese scientist Zhang Heng adjusts the calendar to bring it into line with the four seasons.

123.title: Ban Yong
123.body: In China, Ban Yong, son of Ban Chao, reestablishes the Chinese control over the Tarim Basin.

124.title: Antinous
124.body: Antinous becomes Hadrian's beloved companion on his journeys through the Roman Empire.

124.title: Greece
124.body: During a voyage to Greece, Hadrian is initiated in the ancient rites known as the Eleusinian Mysteries.

124.title: Hadrian
124.body: Emperor Hadrian begins to rebuild the Olympeion in Athens.

124.title: Nahapana
124.body: In northern India, Nahapana, ruler of the Scythians, is defeated and dies in battle while fighting against King Gautamiputra Satakarni. This defeat destroys the Scythian dynasty of the Western Kshatrapas.

124.title: Gaius Bellicius Flaccus Torquatus Tebanianus
124.body: Gaius Bellicius Torquatus and Manius Acilius Glabrio begin the year as the new consuls, but the two are replaced in April.

125.title: Plague
125.body: Plague sweeps North Africa in the wake of a locust invasion that destroys large areas of cropland. The plague kills as many as 500,000 in Numidia and possibly 150,000 on the coast before moving to Italy, where it takes so many lives that villages and towns are abandoned. (or was it around 125 BC)

125.title: Pope Telesphorus
125.body: Pope Telesphorus succeeds Pope Sixtus I as the eighth pope according to Roman Catholic tradition.

125.title: Pantheon, Rome
125.body: The Pantheon is constructed (in Athens) as it stands today, by Ictinus.

125.title: Gautamiputra Satakarni
125.body: Gautamiputra Satakarni, a king of the Andhra dynasty, conquers the Konkan near Bombay. He then controls central India from coast to coast.

125.title: Yanguang
125.body: Last (4th) year of the Yanguang era of the Chinese Han dynasty.

126.title: Pantheon, Rome
126.body: The old Pantheon is demolished by Emperor Hadrian, and the construction of a new one begins (its date is uncertain, because Hadrian chooses not to inscribe the temple).

126.title: Yongjian
126.body: First year of the Yongjian era of the Chinese Han dynasty.

127.title: Hadrian
127.body: Emperor Hadrian returns to Rome, after a seven-year voyage to the Roman provinces.

127.title: Proconsul
127.body: Hadrian, acting on the advice of his proconsul of Asia, Gaius Minicius Fundanus, determines that Christians shall not be put to death without a trial.

127.title: Kanishka
127.body: Kanishka I starts to rule in the Kushan Empire (approximate date).

127.title: Carpocrates
127.body: The philosopher Carpocrates rejects ownership of private property as being un-Christian.

128.title: Bakery
128.body: Roman bakeries produce dozens of bread varieties, and the Romans distribute free bread for the poor.

128.title: Hadrian
128.body: Hadrian begins his inspection of the provinces of Greece, Asia Minor and Egypt.

128.title: Pantheon, Rome
128.body: The Pantheon in Rome is finished.

128.title: Augusta
128.body: Emperor Hadrian visits the Roman province of North Africa, in order to inspect Legio III Augusta stationed at Lambaesis. For strategic reasons, the legionnaires are located in the Aurès Mountains.

128.title: Hadrian's Wall
128.body: Hadrian's Wall is completed in Britain. Built mostly of stone in the east and with a wooden palisade in the west. They construct at least 16 forts, with about 15,000 legionaries digging ditches, quarrying rock and cutting stone, preventing idleness which led to unrest and rebellions in the ranks.

129.title: Hadrian
129.body: Emperor Hadrian continues his voyages, now inspecting Caria, Cappadocia and Syria.

129.title: Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople
129.body: Change of Patriarch of Constantinople, from Patriarch Diogenes to Eleutherius.

129.title: Augusta
129.body: A defense for Numidia is constructed at Lambaesis by Legio III Augusta.

130.title: Antinous
130.body: 130–138 – Antinous, from Hadrian's Villa at Tivoli, Italy, is made. It is now kept at Museo Gregoriano Egizio, Rome.

130.title: Scythians
130.body: The Scythian king Rudradaman I reconquers the lands annexed by Gautamiputra from the Andhra.

130.title: Hadrian
130.body: Emperor Hadrian visits the cities Petra and Gerasa (Jerash).

130.title: Temple of Olympian Zeus
130.body: The Temple of Olympian Zeus is completed at Athens.

130.title: Law
130.body: A law is passed in Rome banning the execution of slaves without a trial.

131.title: Italy
131.body: Italy is divided into legal districts managed by consuls, a direct blow to the power and prestige of the Senate.

131.title: Hadrian
131.body: Emperor Hadrian builds the city Aelia Capitolina, on the location of Jerusalem.

131.title: Circumcision
131.body: The Edict of Hadrian prohibits the practice of circumcision. Additionally, Hadrian prohibits public reading of the Torah under penalty of death, as well as observance of festivals and the Sabbath, the teaching of Judaic Law, and the ordination of rabbis.

131.title: Claudius
131.body: Hadrian restores the monarchist policy of Claudius and Domitian. The equestrian order is given full legal status, and attains the second order of the state.

131.title: Temple of Baalshamin
131.body: The Temple of Baalshamin is built in Palmyra.

132.title: Bar Kokhba revolt
132.body: Bar Kokhba revolt: The messianic, charismatic Jewish leader Simon bar Kokhba starts a war of liberation for Judea against the Romans, which is eventually crushed (in 135) by Emperor Hadrian; Rabbi Akiva is supportive of the rebellion.

132.title: Legio X Fretensis
132.body: Legio X Fretensis must evacuate Jerusalem, returning to Caesarea. The Jews enter the city, and re-establish their system of sacrifices. They strike coins to celebrate their independence, which will last for only 30 months.

132.title: Mausoleum of Hadrian
132.body: Construction begins on the Mausoleum of Hadrian in Rome (known today as Castel Sant'Angelo).

132.title: Yongjian
132.body: Change of era name from Yongjian (7th year) to Yangjia of the Chinese Han dynasty.

132.title: Merchant
132.body: Merchants in Britain build structures outside the forts of Hadrian's Wall, and offer goods and services (including brothels) to Roman soldiers, who receive salaries in a region that otherwise has virtually no ready money.

133.title: Bar Kokhba Revolt
133.body: Bar Kokhba Revolt: Sextus Julius Severus, Roman governor of Britain, is sent to Judea (in 136 renamed Syria Palaestina) to quell the revolt. Jewish rebels, led by Simon bar Kokhba and Eleazar, cut off the vital supply lines and Roman garrisons in Palestine. Despite Roman reinforcements from Syria and Egypt, they establish an independent state in Judea.

134.title: Jerusalem
134.body: The Romans retake Jerusalem. The largely-destroyed city is renamed Aelia Capitolina.

134.title: Law
134.body: A law improving the lot of free workers is passed in Rome.

134.title: Flavius Arrianus Xenophon
134.body: Arrianus, Roman governor of Cappadocia, repulses an attack of the Alani, a nomadic tribe from southeastern Russia.

134.title: Sextus Julius Severus
134.body: Summer – Sextus Julius Severus, Roman governor of Judea, begins a campaign against the Jewish rebel strongholds in the mountains.

134.title: Ilseong of Silla
134.body: Ilseong becomes ruler of the Korean kingdom of Silla.

135.title: diaspora
135.body: A Jewish diaspora begins, as Emperor Hadrian bars Jews from Jerusalem, and has survivors of the massacre dispersed across the Roman Empire. Many join Mediterranean ports.

135.title: Yangjia
135.body: Last (4th) year of Yangjia era of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

135.title: Colonia Aelia Capitolina
135.body: Jerusalem is renamed Colonia Aelia Capitolina, in honor of Hadrian. Legio VI Ferrata rebuilds the legionary fortress in the city, and constructs a Roman temple at Golgotha.

135.title: Jupiter
135.body: An altar to Jupiter is erected, on the site of the Temple in Jerusalem.

135.title: Alans
135.body: Alans threaten Cappadocia; they are repulsed by Arrian.

136.title: Bar Kokhba revolt
136.body: Following the Bar Kokhba revolt, Emperor Hadrian expels the Jews from Judea, and receives a triumphal arch near Scythopolis. The Roman province of Iudaea (plus Galilee) becomes Syria Palaestina (the name Palestine as a designation for this land has been used since at least the 5th century BC (mentioned by Herodotus)).

136.title: Memoirs
136.body: Hadrian dictates his memoirs at his villa near Tivoli (Tibur) outside Rome.

136.title: Yonghe
136.body: First year of Yonghe era of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

136.title: Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople
136.body: Change of Bishop of Byzantium from Bishop Eleutherius to Bishop Felix.

136.title: Suebi
136.body: The war against the Suebi begins.

137.title: Tax law
137.body: Tax laws are passed for trade in Palmyra. The caravan city grows rich by importing rare products from the Persian Gulf, and by exporting items manufactured by the Mediterranean world to the East.

137.title: Jiaozhi
137.body: In Jiaozhi (present-day northern Vietnam) during the Second Era of Northern Domination, a rebellion of the Cham people broke out and lasted for one year, until the peace agreement was made between the Han dynasty governors and the rebels in 138.

138.title: Hadrian
138.body: Emperor Hadrian makes Antoninus Pius his successor, on condition that he adopt Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus.

138.title: Heart failure
138.body: Hadrian dies after a heart failure at Baiae, and is buried at Rome in the Gardens of Domitia beside his wife, Vibia Sabina.

138.title: Silver
138.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius falls to 75 percent under Emperor Antoninus Pius, down from 87 percent under Hadrian.

138.title: Amman
138.body: Construction begins on the Theater of Philadelphia (Amman).

138.title: Roman Emperor
138.body: Antoninus Pius succeeds Hadrian as Roman Emperor, and asks the Senate to confer divine honors for Hadrian.

139.title: 139 Mcurn earthquake
139.body: 139 Mcurn earthquake, listed in bibliographical records of seismology as having affected the city of Mcurn (modern Hösnek, Turkey). The earthquake reportedly took place in the vicinity of Mount Ararat. The region was part of the historic Kingdom of Armenia, corresponding to eastern areas of modern Turkey.

139.title: Caesar
139.body: Marcus Aurelius is named Caesar. He marries the 9-year-old Faustina the Younger, daughter of Antoninus Pius.

139.title: Castel Sant'Angelo
139.body: The Tomb of Hadrian in Rome is completed; Emperor Antoninus Pius cremates the body of Hadrian, and places his ashes, together with that of his wife Vibia Sabina and his adopted son, Lucius Aelius, in the mausoleum.

139.title: Gaius Bruttius Praesens Lucius Fulvius Rusticus
139.body: Antoninus Pius and Gaius Bruttius Praesens become Roman Consuls.

140.title: Antoninus Pius
140.body: Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Caesar become Roman Consuls.

140.title: Mithridates V of Parthia
140.body: King Mithridates V dies; Vologases IV claims the throne and extends his rule throughout the Parthian Empire.

140.title: Quadi
140.body: Antoninus Pius recognizes the king of the Quadi, who becomes an ally of Rome.

140.title: Marcion
140.body: Marcion arrives in Rome, bringing Evangelikon and Apostolikon to the Christian community.

141.title: Temple of Antoninus and Faustina
141.body: The Temple of Antoninus and Faustina is constructed in Rome; the temple is dedicated to Empress Faustina the Elder.

141.title: Yonghe
141.body: Last (6th) year of Yonghe era of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

141.title: 141 Lycia earthquake
141.body: The 141 Lycia earthquake affects most of the Roman provinces of Lycia and Caria and the islands of Rhodes, Kos, Simi and Serifos. It triggers a severe tsunami, which causes major inundation.

141.title: Apsis
141.body: 6th recorded perihelion passage of Halley's Comet.

141.title: Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople
141.body: Change of Patriarch of Constantinople from Felix of Byzantium to Polycarpus II of Byzantium.

142.title: Physicians
142.body: Municipal doctors are named throughout the Roman Empire.

142.title: Antoninus Pius
142.body: Emperor Antoninus Pius orders the construction of the Antonine Wall. The wall stretches 39 miles (63 km), from Old Kilpatrick in West Dunbartonshire on the Firth of Clyde, to Carriden near Bo'ness on the Firth of Forth (Scotland). The Romans build 19 forts and smaller fortlets (milecastles), to protect the border against the Caledonians.

142.title: Marcion
142.body: Marcion proclaims that the Old Testament is incompatible with Christianity.

142.title: Hanan
142.body: First year of the Hanan era of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

142.title: Kinship of the Three
142.body: The Chinese Taoist alchemist Wei Boyang, author of the Kinship of the Three, is the first to describe an early form of gunpowder solution.

143.title: Antoninus Pius
143.body: Antoninus Pius serves as Roman Consul.

143.title: Brigantes
143.body: A revolt of the Brigantes tribe in Britannia is suppressed by Quintus Lollius Urbicus.

143.title: Antyllus
143.body: The Roman doctor Antyllus performs the first arteriotomy.

144.title: Vietnam
144.body: In Vietnam during the second Era of Northern Domination, rebellion of Cham people broke out in Jiaozhi and Jiuzhen. The rebels then surrendered to the Jiaozhi's governor in the same year.

144.title: Hanan
144.body: Change of era name from Hanan (3rd year) to Jiankang era of the Chinese Han dynasty.

144.title: Huvishka
144.body: Reign of Huvishka, emperor of the Kushan Empire.

144.title: Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople
144.body: Change of Patriarch of Constantinople from Polycarpus II to Athendodorus (until 148).

144.title: Marcion of Sinope
144.body: Marcion of Sinope is excommunicated; a sect, Marcionism, grows out of his beliefs.

145.title: Antoninus Pius
145.body: Antoninus Augustus Pius and Marcus Aurelius Caesar become Roman Consuls.

145.title: Han Chongdi
145.body: Change of emperor from Han Chongdi to Han Zhidi of the Chinese Han dynasty.

145.title: Faustina the Younger
145.body: Marcus Aurelius marries Faustina the Younger, the daughter of Antoninus Pius.

146.title: Marcus Aurelius
146.body: Marcus Aurelius receives the imperium proconsular.

146.title: Yongxi
146.body: Change of era name from Yongxi (1st year) to Benchu era of the Chinese Han dynasty.

146.title: Chadae of Goguryeo
146.body: Chadae becomes ruler of the Korean kingdom of Goguryeo.

146.title: Augusta
146.body: Faustina the Younger is given the title Augusta, and becomes Roman Empress.

146.title: Han Huandi
146.body: Han Huandi succeeds Han Zhidi as emperor of the Chinese Han dynasty.

147.title: Marcus Aurelius
147.body: Marcus Aurelius receives imperial powers, from Emperor Antoninus Pius.

147.title: Vologases IV of Parthia
147.body: King Vologases IV, son of Mithridates V of Parthia, unites the Parthian Empire under his rule.

147.title: Ancient Rome
147.body: Festivals to celebrate the 900th anniversary of the founding of Rome begin.

147.title: Jianhe
147.body: First year of Jianhe of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

147.title: Vologases III of Parthia
147.body: King Vologases III dies after a 42-year reign, in which he has contended successfully with his rivals.

148.title: Antoninus Pius
148.body: Emperor Antoninus Pius hosts a series of grand games, to celebrate Rome's 900th anniversary.

148.title: Euzois of Byzantium
148.body: Euzois succeeds Athendodorus, as Patriarch of Constantinople.

150.title: Municipium Aelium Cananefatium
150.body: The Roman town Forum Hadriani (Voorburg) receives the title of Municipium Aelium Cananefatium, "the town of the Cananefates" (modern Netherlands). The town is awarded with rights to organize markets.

150.title: Pyramid of the Sun
150.body: The Great Pyramid of the Sun is constructed in Teotihuacan. It is the tallest pre-Columbian building in the Americas.

150.title: Chinese era name
150.body: First and only year of the "Heping" era of the Han dynasty in China.

150.title: Carpathian Mountains
150.body: The Germans of the east move south, into the Carpathians and Black Sea area.

150.title: Geography
150.body: The earliest atlas (Ptolemy's Geography) is made (approximate date).

151.title: Han dynasty
151.body: First year of Yuanjia of the Chinese Han dynasty.

151.title: Mytilene
151.body: Mytilene and Smyrna are destroyed by an earthquake.

151.title: Detail from a rubbing of a stone relief in Wu family shrine (Wuliangci), Jiaxian…
151.body: Detail from a rubbing of a stone relief in Wu family shrine (Wuliangci), Jiaxiang, Shandong, is made (Han dynasty).

152.title: Tarim Basin
152.body: The Chinese domination of the Tarim Basin weakens.

153.title: Yuanjia
153.body: Change of era name from Yuanjia (3rd year) to Yongxing of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

153.title: Roman Egypt
153.body: Minor uprisings occur in Roman Egypt against Roman rule.

154.title: Polycarp
154.body: Anicetus meets with Polycarp of Smyrna to discuss the Computus, the date of Easter in the Christian liturgical calendar.

154.title: Pope Anicetus
154.body: Anicetus becomes pope of Rome (approximate date).

154.title: Bosporan Kingdom
154.body: King Eupator of Bosphorus pays tribute to Rome, due to the threat posed by the Alani.

154.title: Yongxing
154.body: Last (2nd) year of Yongxing era of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

154.title: Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople
154.body: Change of Patriarch of Constantinople from Patriarch Euzois to Patriarch Laurence.

155.title: Pope Anicetus
155.body: The Council of Rome, a pre-ecumenical church council, is held and presided over by Bishop of Rome Anicetus.

156.title: La Mojarra Stela 1
156.body: The La Mojarra Stela 1 is produced in Mesoamerica.

156.title: Heresiarch
156.body: The heresiarch Montanus first appears in Ardaban (Mysia).

157.title: Dacia
157.body: A revolt against Roman rule begins in Dacia.

157.title: Jiuzhen
157.body: In Jiuzhen (modern day Northern Vietnam), during the second Era of Northern Domination, a rebellion led by Chu Đạt breaks out and lasts for two to three years, until it is quelled by the Han Dynasty, under emperor Huan.

158.title: Sol Invictus
158.body: The earliest dated use of Sol Invictus, in a dedication from Rome.

158.title: Yongshou
158.body: Change of era name from Yongshou to Yangxi of the Chinese Han dynasty.

158.title: Dacia
158.body: A revolt against Roman rule in Dacia is crushed.

159.title: Satavahana
159.body: In India, the reign of Shivashri Satakarni, as King Satavahana of Andhra, begins.

160.title: Roman Empire
160.body: In Roman Empire, the Antonine Wall in Britain is retaken by Roman legions.

160.title: Ancient Rome
160.body: In Rome, the manufacturing of soap containing grease, lime and ashes begins.

160.title: Buddhist
160.body: The first Buddhist monks arrive in China.

160.title: Roman History
160.body: Appian writes Ρωμαικα, known in English as the Roman History, in which he includes the history of each nation conquered up until the moment of its conquest.

160.title: Jiuzhen
160.body: In Jiuzhen (modern day Northern Vietnam) during the second Era of Northern Domination, a rebellion broke out but was swiftly quelled when the rebels surrendered to the newly appointed governor of Jiaozhi, who had previously governed the commandery in the 140s.

161.title: Parthian Empire
161.body: Autumn – The Parthians invade Armenia, and install their own candidate on the throne. A legion (perhaps Legio IX Hispana) is destroyed at Elegeia.

161.title: Antoninus Pius
161.body: Emperor Antoninus Pius dies, and is succeeded by Marcus Aurelius, who shares imperial power with Lucius Verus, although Marcus retains the title Pontifex Maximus.

161.title: Trajan
161.body: Marcus Aurelius, a Spaniard like Trajan and Hadrian, is a stoical disciple of Epictetus, and an energetic man of action. He pursues the policy of his predecessor and maintains good relations with the Senate. As a legislator, he endeavors to create new principles of morality and humanity, particularly favoring women and slaves.

161.title: Silver
161.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius falls to 68 percent under Emperor Marcus Aurelius, down from 75 percent under Antoninus Pius.

161.title: Aureus
161.body: Aurelius reduces the weight of a goldpiece, the aureus, from 7.81 grams to 7.12 grams.

162.title: Lucius Verus
162.body: Lucius Verus begins a war with the Parthians, due to the invasion of Syria and Armenia by King Vologases IV of Parthia.

162.title: Arrian
162.body: Arrian, Greek historian and writer, publishes Indica, a work on India and its people.

163.title: Marcus Statius Priscus
163.body: Marcus Statius Priscus re-conquers Armenia; the capital city of Artaxata is ruined.

164.title: Ctesiphon
164.body: Ctesiphon is captured by the Romans, but returns to the Parthians after the end of the war.

164.title: Antonine Wall
164.body: The Antonine Wall in Scotland is abandoned by the Romans.

164.title: Marcus Aurelius
164.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius gives his daughter Lucilla in marriage to his co-emperor Lucius Verus.

164.title: Avidius Cassius
164.body: Avidius Cassius, one of Lucius Verus' generals, crosses the Euphrates and invades Parthia.

164.title: Seleucia on the Tigris
164.body: Seleucia on the Tigris is destroyed.

165.title: Antonine Plague
165.body: Antonine Plague: A pandemic breaks out in Rome, after the Roman army returns from Parthia. The plague significantly depopulates the Roman Empire and China.

165.title: Italica
165.body: Legio II Italica is levied by Emperor Marcus Aurelius.

165.title: Nisibis
165.body: Avidius Cassius takes Nisibis, and conquers the north of Mesopotamia.

165.title: Garrison
165.body: The Romans establish a garrison at Doura Europos on the Euphrates, a control point for the commercial route to the Persian Gulf.

165.title: Ancient Rome
165.body: Marcus Aurelius creates 4 legal districts (iuridici) in Italy (5 if Rome is included).

166.title: Lombards
166.body: The Lombards invade Pannonia (modern Hungary). They are quickly dispatched by the Roman Army.

166.title: Small pox
166.body: A plague (possibly small pox) comes from the East and spreads throughout the Roman Empire, lasting for roughly twenty years.

166.title: Sino-Roman relations
166.body: A Roman envoy arrives by sea in Rinan commandery, in southern China (central Vietnam). He travels to the Chinese capital Luoyang, and is greeted by Emperor Huan of the Han dynasty.

166.title: End of the war with Parthia
166.body: End of the war with Parthia: The Parthians leave Armenia and eastern Mesopotamia, which both become Roman protectorates.

166.title: Marcus Aurelius
166.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius appoints his sons Commodus and Marcus Annius Verus as co-rulers (Caesar), while he and Lucius Verus travel to Germany.

167.title: Marcomanni
167.body: The Marcomanni tribe wages war against the Romans at Aquileia. They destroy aqueducts and irrigation conduits. Marcus Aurelius repels the invaders, ending the Pax Romana (Roman Peace) that has kept the Roman Empire free of conflict since the days of Emperor Augustus.

167.title: Germans
167.body: The Germans devastate the Balkans and ransack the sanctuary of Eleusis, near Athens.

167.title: Yanxi
167.body: Change of era name from Yanxi to Yongkang of the Chinese Han dynasty.

167.title: Macedonica
167.body: The Vandals (Astingi and Lacringi) and the Sarmatian Iazyges invade Dacia. To counter them, Legio V Macedonica, returning from the Parthian War, moves its headquarters from Troesmis in Moesia Inferior to Potaissa in Dacia Porolissensis.

167.title: Lucius Verus
167.body: Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus and Marcus Ummidius Quadratus Annianus become Roman Consuls.

168.title: Marcus Aurelius
168.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius and his adopted brother Lucius Verus leave Rome, and establish their headquarters at Aquileia.

168.title: Roman army
168.body: The Roman army crosses the Alps into Pannonia, and subdues the Marcomanni at Carnuntum, north of the Danube.

168.title: Jianning
168.body: Emperor Ling of Han succeeds Emperor Huan of Han as the emperor of the Chinese Han Dynasty; the first year of the Jianning era.

169.title: Moors
169.body: Northern African Moors invade what is now Spain.

169.title: Marcomannic Wars
169.body: Marcomannic Wars: Germanic tribes invade the frontiers of the Roman Empire, specifically the provinces of Raetia and Moesia.

169.title: Marcus Aurelius
169.body: Marcus Aurelius becomes sole Roman Emperor upon the death of Lucius Verus.

169.title: Confucian
169.body: Confucian scholars who had denounced the court eunuchs are arrested, killed or banished from the capital of Luoyang and official life during the second episode of the Disasters of Partisan Prohibitions, which does not formally end until 184 with the onslaught of the Yellow Turban Rebellion.

169.title: Galen
169.body: Galen moves back to Rome for good.

170.title: Christians
170.body: Marcus Aurelius orders humane treatment for Christians and slaves throughout the Roman Empire.

170.title: Meditations
170.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius writes in Sirmium (Pannonia) his first of 12 books of the Meditations in Koine Greek.

170.title: Costoboci
170.body: The Costoboci cross the Danube (Dacia) and ravage Thrace in the Balkan Peninsula. They reach Eleusina, near Athens, and destroy the temple of the Eleusinian Mysteries.

170.title: Suebian
170.body: The Suebian tribes of the Marcomanni cross the Danube and invade Northern Italy. The Roman army (20,000 men) is destroyed near Carnuntum in Pannonia.

170.title: Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
170.body: An Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius is erected in Rome.

171.title: Armenia
171.body: Armenia and Mesopotamia become protectorates of the Roman Empire.

171.title: praetentura Italiae et Alpium
171.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius forms a new military command, the praetentura Italiae et Alpium. Aquileia is relieved, and the Marcomanni are evicted from Roman territory.

171.title: Costoboci
171.body: The Costoboci cross the Danube (Dacia) and ravage Thrace in the Balkan Peninsula. They reach Eleusis, near Athens, and destroy the temple of the Eleusinian Mysteries.

171.title: Quadi
171.body: Marcus Aurelius signs a peace treaty with the Quadi and the Sarmatian Iazyges. The Germanic tribes of the Hasdingi (Vandals) and the Lacringi become Roman allies.

171.title: Aelius Aristides
171.body: May – June – Aelius Aristides, Greek orator, delivers a public speech in Smyrna, lamenting the damage recently inflicted to the sacred site of Eleusis.

172.title: Bucolic War
172.body: The Bucolic War broke out in Egypt. The Egyptians, led by Isidorus, fought against the Roman Empire under Marcus Aurelius. Avidius Cassius, governor of Syria, suppresses the revolt and is made supreme commander of the Roman army in the East.

172.title: Marcus Aurelius
172.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius crosses the Danube with an expeditionary force. He subdues the Marcomanni and their allies and then, in a pact signed with the Germanic tribes, he imports them into the Roman Empire to occupy areas that have been depopulated by the plague.

172.title: Jianning
172.body: Last (5th) year of Jianning era and start of Xiping era of the Chinese Han dynasty.

172.title: Battle of Jwawon
172.body: Battle of Jwawon: Goguryeo Prime Minister Myeongnim Dap-bu defeats the Chinese Han dynasty forces in Manchuria.

172.title: Miracle in Moravia
172.body: Miracle in Moravia: As the Roman army is encircled by the Quadi under intense heat, a violent thunderstorm sweeps away the Quadi in a torrent of water and mud, and refreshes the parched legionaries.

173.title: Eastern Empire
173.body: Given control of the Eastern Empire, Avidius Cassius, the governor of Syria, crushes an insurrection of shepherds known as the Boukoloi.

173.title: Gnaeus Claudius Severus
173.body: Gnaeus Claudius Severus and Tiberius Claudius Pompeianus become Roman Consuls.

174.title: Mater Castrorum
174.body: Empress Faustina the Younger accompanies her husband, Marcus Aurelius, on various military campaigns and enjoys the love of the Roman soldiers. Aurelius gives her the title of Mater Castrorum ("Mother of the Camp").

174.title: Fulminata
174.body: Marcus Aurelius officially confers the title Fulminata ("Thundering") to the Legio XII Fulminata.

174.title: Pope Eleuterus
174.body: Pope Eleuterus succeeds Pope Soter as the thirteenth pope (approximate date).

174.title: Satavahana
174.body: Reign in India of Yajnashri Satakarni, Satavahana king of the Andhra. He extends his empire from the center to the north of India.

174.title: Meditations
174.body: Meditations by Marcus Aurelius is written, in Greek, while on military campaigns in Pannonia (approximate date).

175.title: Marcus Aurelius
175.body: Marcus Aurelius suppresses a revolt of Avidius Cassius, governor of Syria, after the latter proclaims himself emperor.

175.title: Assassination
175.body: Avidius Cassius fails in seeking support for his rebellion and is assassinated by Roman officers. They sent his head to Aurelius, who persuades the Senate to pardon Cassius's family.

175.title: Lucius Aelius Aurelius Commodus
175.body: Commodus, son of Marcus Aurelius and his wife Faustina, is named Caesar.

175.title: M. Sattonius Iucundus
175.body: M. Sattonius Iucundus, decurio in Colonia Ulpia Traiana, restores the Thermae of Coriovallum (modern Heerlen). There are sources that state this happened in the 3rd century.

175.title: Confucian
175.body: Confucian scholars try to ensure their capacity in the royal court of China. They are massacred by the eunuchs.

176.title: Imperator
176.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius grants his son Commodus the rank of Imperator, and makes him Supreme Commander of the Roman legions.

176.title: Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
176.body: The Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius is made. It is now kept at Museo Capitolini in Rome (approximate date).

176.title: Ancient Rome
176.body: Marcus Aurelius and Commodus enter Rome after a campaign north of the Alps, and receive a triumph for their victories over the Germanic tribes.

177.title: Christianity
177.body: A systematic persecution of Christians begins in Rome; the followers take refuge in the catacombs.

177.title: Gaul
177.body: The churches in southern Gaul are destroyed after a crowd accuses the local Christians of practicing cannibalism.

177.title: Commodus
177.body: Lucius Aurelius Commodus Caesar (age 15) and Marcus Peducaeus Plautius Quintillus become Roman Consuls.

177.title: Central Asia
177.body: Chinese troops suffer a crushing defeat against a confederacy of Central Asian tribes, led by the Xianbei (see Wu Hu).

177.title: Lyon
177.body: Forty-eight Christians are martyred in Lyon (Saint Blandina and Pothinus, bishop of Lyon, are among them).

178.title: Marcus Aurelius
178.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius and his son Commodus arrive at Carnuntum in Pannonia, and travel to the Danube to fight against the Marcomanni.

178.title: Augusta
178.body: Bruttia Crispina marries Commodus, and receives the title of Augusta.

178.title: Xiping
178.body: Last (7th) year of Xiping era and start of Guanghe era of the Chinese Han dynasty.

178.title: Kushan Empire
178.body: In India, the decline of the Kushan Empire begins. The Sassanides take over Central Asia.

178.title: Montanism
178.body: The Montanist heresy is condemned for the first time.

179.title: Marcus Aurelius
179.body: Marcus Aurelius drives the Marcomanni over the Danube and reinforces the border. To repopulate and rebuild a devastated Pannonia, Rome allows the first German colonists to enter territory controlled by the Roman Empire.

179.title: Adiutrix
179.body: Roman legionaries of Legio II Adiutrix engrave on the rock of the Trenčín Castle (Slovakia) the name of the town Laugaritio, marking the northernmost point of Roman presence in that part of Europe.

179.title: Castra
179.body: The Roman fort Castra Regina ("fortress by the Regen river") is built at Regensburg, on the right bank of the Danube in Germany.

179.title: Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art
179.body: Han dynasty China: The full title of the Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art appears on two bronze standard measures dated to this year, yet there is speculation that the same book existed beforehand only under different titles. In the 3rd century, Liu Hui would provide commentary on this important early Chinese mathematical treatise.

179.title: Gogukcheon of Goguryeo
179.body: Gogukcheon succeeds his father Shindae as King of Goguryeo.

180.title: Lake Taupō
180.body: Lake Taupō in New Zealand erupts, forming ash clouds as far as China and Europe.

180.title: Zoroastrianism
180.body: Commodus creates an official cult of the Zoroastrian god Mithra.

180.title: Marcus Aurelius
180.body: Emperor Marcus Aurelius dies after a week's illness at his camp in Vindobona (modern Vienna). He is succeeded by his son Commodus (age 18).

180.title: Rome
180.body: Rome creates a 4-mile wide buffer zone by the Danube.

181.title: Taupō Volcano
181.body: The volcano associated with Lake Taupō in New Zealand erupts, one of the largest on Earth in the last 5,000 years. The effects of this eruption are seen as far away as Rome and China.

181.title: Commodus
181.body: Imperator Lucius Aurelius Commodus and Lucius Antistius Burrus become Roman Consuls.

181.title: Antonine Wall
181.body: The Antonine Wall is overrun by the Picts in Britannia (approximate date).

181.title: June
181.body: June: Liang Long is captured and beheaded, and his rebellion is suppressed by general Zhu Juan.

181.title: Nanhai Commandery
181.body: Nanhai inspector Kong Zhi led thousands of residents to join Liang Long's rebellion against the Han dynasty.

182.title: Commodus
182.body: Emperor Commodus escapes death at the hands of assassins, who have attacked him at the instigation of his sister Lucilla and a large group of senators. He puts many distinguished Romans to death on charges of being implicated in the conspiracy; Lucilla is exiled to Capri.

183.title: Jiaozhi
183.body: In Jiaozhi (present-day northern Vietnam), during the Second Era of Northern Domination, the local army launches a coup, captures the governor, and subsequently executes him. After the coup, Emperor Ling appoints a new Jiaozhi governor, who brings peace to the region, and is praised by the residents.

184.title: Zhang Jue
184.body: Zhang Jue leads the peasant revolt against Emperor Ling of Han of the Eastern Han dynasty. Heading for the capital of Luoyang, his massive and undisciplined army (360,000 men), burns and destroys government offices and outposts.

184.title: Goguryeo
184.body: King Gogukcheon (Gaonanwu) of Goguryeo (Gaogouli) pushes Chinese armies back to Liaodong.

184.title: Yellow Turban Rebellion
184.body: The Yellow Turban Rebellion and Liang Province Rebellion break out in China.

184.title: He Jin
184.body: June – Ling of Han places his brother-in-law, He Jin, in command of the imperial army and sends them to attack the Yellow Turban rebels.

185.title: Commodus
185.body: Nobles of Britain demand that Emperor Commodus rescind all power given to Tigidius Perennis, who is eventually executed.

185.title: Tigidius Perennis
185.body: Tigidius Perennis, his family and many others are executed for conspiring against Commodus.

185.title: Zhi Yao
185.body: Zhi Yao, a Kushan Buddhist monk of Yuezhi ethnicity, translates Buddhist texts into the Chinese language during the Han dynasty.

185.title: Ancient Rome
185.body: Commodus drains Rome's treasury to put on gladiatorial spectacles and confiscates property to support his pleasures. He participates as a gladiator and boasts of victory in 1,000 matches in the Circus Maximus.

185.title: Astrological Annals of the Houhanshu
185.body: A supernova now known as SN 185 is noted by Chinese astronomers in the Astrological Annals of the Houhanshu, making it the earliest recorded supernova.

186.title: Gaul
186.body: Peasants in Gaul stage an anti-tax uprising under Maternus.

186.title: Pertinax
186.body: Roman governor Pertinax escapes an assassination attempt, by British usurpers.

186.title: Hatepe eruption
186.body: The Hatepe volcanic eruption extends Lake Taupō and makes skies red across the world. However, recent radiocarbon dating by R. Sparks has put the date at 233 AD ± 13 (95% confidence).

187.title: Septimius Severus
187.body: Septimius Severus marries Julia Domna (age 17), a Syrian princess, at Lugdunum (modern-day Lyon). She is the youngest daughter of high-priest Julius Bassianus – a descendant of the Royal House of Emesa. Her elder sister is Julia Maesa.

187.title: Clodius Albinus
187.body: Clodius Albinus defeats the Chatti, a highly organized German tribe that controlled the area that includes the Black Forest.

187.title: Olympianus of Byzantium
187.body: Olympianus succeeds Pertinax as bishop of Byzantium (until 198).

188.title: Pertinax
188.body: Publius Helvius Pertinax becomes pro-consul of Africa from 188 to 189.

188.title: Shingi Waō
188.body: Queen Himiko (or Shingi Waō) begins her reign in Japan (until 248).

189.title: Pantaenus
189.body: Pantaenus, who was sent by the bishop of Alexandria to India to preach Christianity, meets with little success.

189.title: Pope Victor
189.body: Pope Victor I succeeds Pope Eleuterus as the fourteenth pope, the first from Africa.

189.title: Smallpox
189.body: Plague (possibly smallpox) kills as many as 2,000 people per day in Rome. Farmers are unable to harvest their crops, and food shortages bring riots in the city.

189.title: Liu Bian
189.body: Liu Bian succeeds Emperor Ling, as Chinese emperor of the Han dynasty.

189.title: "Treatise on the various temperaments"
189.body: Galen publishes his "Treatise on the various temperaments" (aka On the Elements According to Hippocrates).

190.title: Egypt
190.body: Egypt (under Roman rule) is impoverished due to an inflation rate of 100% during the previous decade.

190.title: Colonia Commodiana
190.body: A part of Rome burns; Emperor Commodus orders the city to be rebuilt, under the name Colonia Commodiana.

190.title: Osroes II of Parthia
190.body: Osroes II, controlling Media, claims the throne of the Parthian Empire. King Vologases IV of Parthia puts down the rebellion and restores order.

190.title: Chuping
190.body: First year of the Chuping era of the Chinese Han Dynasty.

190.title: Freedman
190.body: At the climax of his power, the freedman Marcus Aurelius Cleander appoints 25 men as Roman consuls. Amongst these is Lucius Septimius Severus, who later becomes emperor during the Year of the Five Emperors).

191.title: Hercules
191.body: 191–192 – The sculpture of Commodus as Hercules, from Esquiline Hill, Rome, is made (it is now kept at Palazzo dei Conservatori, Rome).

191.title: Hangu Pass
191.body: A coalition of Chinese warlords from the east of Hangu Pass launches a punitive campaign against the warlord Dong Zhuo, who seized control of the central government in 189, and held the figurehead Emperor Xian hostage. After suffering some defeats against the coalition forces, Dong Zhuo forcefully relocates the imperial capital from Luoyang to Chang'an. Before leaving, Dong Zhuo orders his troops to loot the tombs of the Han emperors, and then destroy Luoyang by fire, to leave behind nothing for the coalition.

191.title: Battle of Jieqiao
191.body: Battle of Jieqiao: Yuan Shao narrowly defeats Gongsun Zan, in northern China.

191.title: Vologases IV of Parthia
191.body: King Vologases IV of Parthia dies after a 44-year reign, and is succeeded by his son Vologases V.

192.title: Champa
192.body: The kingdom of Champa begins to control south and central Vietnam (approximate date).

192.title: Syriac Christianity
192.body: Syriac Christians establish an early Christian community in Kerala, India.

192.title: Commodus
192.body: Emperor Commodus alarms the Senate, by appearing dressed as a gladiator for his new consulship on January 1. His mistress Marcia finds her name on the imperial execution list, and hires champion wrestler Narcissus to assassinate Commodus; the Antonines Dynasty ends.

192.title: Civil war
192.body: Civil war again strikes Ancient Rome (192–193).

192.title: Lü
192.body: Lü Bu assassinates warlord Dong Zhuo, who has controlled the central government of the Han Dynasty (since 189).

193.title: Pertinax
193.body: Pertinax is assassinated by members of the Praetorian Guard, who storm the imperial palace. The Empire is auctioned off; Marcus Didius Julianus the highest bidder, offers 300 million sesterces for the throne. Roman governors Clodius Albinus (Britannia) and Pescennius Niger (Syria) claim, with support of their troops, the imperial throne.

193.title: Roman Empire
193.body: Septimius Severus enters the capital, and has Julianus put to death. He replaces the Praetorian Guard with a 15,000-man force from the Danubian legions, and gains control of the Roman Empire, beginning the Severan dynasty.

193.title: Silver
193.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius falls to 50 percent under emperor Septimius Severus, down from 68 percent under Marcus Aurelius.

193.title: Septimius Severus
193.body: Lucius Septimius Severus is proclaimed Emperor by his troops at Carnuntum, in Pannonia Superior (Balkans). He marches with his army to Rome.

193.title: Year of the Five Emperors
193.body: Year of the Five Emperors: The Roman Senate chooses Publius Helvius Pertinax, against his will, to succeed the late Commodus as Emperor. Pertinax is forced to reorganize the handling of finances, which were wrecked under Commodus, to reestablish discipline in the Roman army, and to suspend the food programs established by Trajan, provoking the ire of the Praetorian Guard.

194.title: Battle of Issus
194.body: Battle of Issus: Septimius Severus marches with his army (12 legions) to Cilicia, and defeats Pescennius Niger, Roman governor of Syria. Pescennius retreats to Antioch, and is executed by Severus' troops.

194.title: Xingping
194.body: First year of the Xingping era, during the Han Dynasty in China.

194.title: Battle of Yan Province
194.body: Battle of Yan Province: Warlords Cao Cao and Lü Bu fight for control over Yan Province; the battle lasts for over 100 days.

194.title: Byzantium
194.body: Septimius Severus besieges Byzantium (194–196); the city walls suffer extensive damage.

194.title: Irenaeus
194.body: Irenaeus declares Gnostic doctrines to be heretical (approximate date).

195.title: Syria
195.body: The Roman province of Syria is divided and the role of Antioch is diminished. The Romans annex the Syrian cities of Edessa and Nisibis. Severus re-establishes his headquarters and the colonies there.

195.title: Septimius Severus
195.body: Emperor Septimius Severus has the Roman Senate deify the previous emperor Commodus, in an attempt to gain favor with the family of Marcus Aurelius.

195.title: Caracalla
195.body: Lucius Septimius Bassianus (or Caracalla), age 7, changes his name to Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, to solidify connections with the family of Marcus Aurelius, and is given the title Caesar.

195.title: Clodius Albinus
195.body: Clodius Albinus, who had been proclaimed emperor in Britain, crosses into Gaul with his legions, while at the same time recruiting new soldiers. He is soon the head of an army of 150,000 men, according to Cassius Dio. Severus, still in Mesopotamia, hastily returns to Rome.

195.title: Xingping
195.body: Last (2nd) year of the Xingping era during the Han Dynasty.

196.title: Caesarea Maritima
196.body: A church council is held in Caesarea over the date of Easter.

196.title: Roman legion
196.body: In order to assure the support of the Roman legion in Germany on his march to Rome, Clodius Albinus is declared Augustus by his army while crossing Gaul.

196.title: Hadrian's Wall
196.body: Hadrian's Wall in Britain is partially destroyed.

196.title: Septimius Severus
196.body: Emperor Septimius Severus attempts to assassinate Clodius Albinus but fails, causing Albinus to retaliate militarily.

196.title: Luoyang
196.body: The Xian Emperor returns to war-ravaged Luoyang and seeks the protection of warlord Cao Cao. He is advised to move the capital to Xuchang; the emperor becomes a pawn in the hands of the Chinese warlords.

197.title: Roman navy
197.body: Septimius Severus forms new naval units, manning all the triremes in Italy with heavily armed troops for war in the East. His soldiers embark on an artificial canal between the Tigris and Euphrates.

197.title: Roman Empire
197.body: Septimius Severus, who had spared the Senate at the beginning of his reign, now excludes it from controlling the Roman Empire by declaring a military dictatorship.

197.title: Christianity
197.body: A Christian council is held in Edessa.

197.title: Ancient Rome
197.body: Septimius Severus returns to Rome and has about 30 of Albinus's supporters in the Senate executed. After his victory he declares himself the adopted son of the late Marcus Aurelius.

197.title: Roman army
197.body: The Roman army marches east to repel a Parthian invasion of Mesopotamia; they loot the royal palace at Ctesiphon and capture an enormous number of its inhabitants as slaves.

198.title: Publius Septimius Geta
198.body: January 28 Publius Septimius Geta, son of Septimius Severus, receives the title of Caesar. Caracalla, son of Septimius Severus, is given the title of Augustus.

198.title: Caracalla
198.body: Caracalla, son of Septimius Severus, is given the title of Augustus.

198.title: Winter – Battle of Xiapi
198.body: Winter – Battle of Xiapi: The allied armies led by Cao Cao and Liu Bei defeat Lü Bu; afterward Cao Cao has him executed.

198.title: Marcus I of Byzantium
198.body: Marcus I succeeds Olympianus as Patriarch of Constantinople (until 211).

199.title: Mesopotamia
199.body: Mesopotamia is partitioned into two Roman provinces divided by the Euphrates, Mesopotamia and Osroene.

199.title: Pope Zephyrinus
199.body: Pope Zephyrinus succeeds Pope Victor I, as the 15th pope.

199.title: Geodeung of Geumgwan Gaya
199.body: Geodeung succeeds Suro of Geumgwan Gaya, as king of the Korean kingdom of Gaya (traditional date).

199.title: Septimius Severus
199.body: Emperor Septimius Severus lays siege to the city-state Hatra in Central-Mesopotamia, but fails to capture the city despite breaching the walls.

199.title: Roman legion
199.body: Two new legions, I Parthica and III Parthica, are formed as a permanent garrison.

200.title: Severan Tondo
200.body: The Severan Tondo, depicting Septimius Severus, Julia Domna and their children Geta and Caracalla, from Fayum, Egypt, is made. It is now kept at Staatliche Museen zu Berlin, Preußischer Kulturbesitz, Antikensammlung.

200.title: Jew
200.body: Jewish Eretz Yisraeli scholar Judah ha-Nasi compiles tracts of the Mishnah, creating Talmudic law.

200.title: Maya civilization
200.body: The Classic Age of Maya civilization begins (around this year).

201.title: Battle of Cangting
201.body: Battle of Cangting: Warlord Cao Cao defeats his rival, Yuan Shao.

201.title: Edessa, Mesopotamia
201.body: November – A flood in Edessa destroys a Christian church, killing over 2,000 people.

201.title: Lucius Annius Fabianus
201.body: Lucius Annius Fabianus and Marcus Nonius Arrius Mucianus become Roman Consuls.

202.title: Augusta
202.body: Severus launches a campaign in Africa; Legio III Augusta under Quintus Anicius Faustus fights a guerrilla war against the Garamantes along the Limes Tripolitanus. They capture several settlements such as Cydamus, Gholaia and their capital Garama, 600 km south of Lepcis Magna. The province of Numidia is enlarged: the Romans annex Castellum Dimmidi, Gemellae and Vescera.

202.title: Christianity
202.body: An edict bans conversions to Christianity and all Christian propaganda.

202.title: Septimius Severus
202.body: Emperor Septimius Severus returns to Rome after a five-year absence. Festivals are held to celebrate his six-year reign. Severus undertakes changes in the imperial government, giving the Roman army a dominant role, raising pay in the legions and permitting legionaries to marry in order to secure their loyalty.

202.title: Slavery
202.body: Some 400,000 slaves perform the menial work of Rome, with middle-class citizens often owning eight; the rich from 500 to 1,000; an emperor as many as 20,000. Free urban workers enjoy 17 to 18 hours of leisure each day, with free admission to baths, sport events and gladiatorial games.

203.title: Origen of Alexandria
203.body: Origen of Alexandria replaces Clement as the head of the Christian school in Alexandria.

203.title: Andhra Pradesh
203.body: Prince Vijaya becomes king of the Andhra Empire. During his reign, the empire is broken apart into smaller independent principalities.

203.title: Septimius Severus
203.body: Emperor Septimius Severus rebuilds Byzantium, and expands the southern frontier of Africa, with the metropolis Carthage re-fortified.

203.title: Battle of Xiakou
203.body: Battle of Xiakou: Warlord Sun Quan battles his rival Huang Zu along the Yangtze River near Wuhan.

203.title: Arch of Septimius Severus
203.body: An arch dedicated to Septimius Severus is erected near the Forum.

204.title: Recession
204.body: A trade recession in the Leptis Magna region (Africa) is alleviated by Emperor Septimius Severus, who buys up the country's olive oil for free distribution in Rome.

204.title: Gongsun Kang
204.body: Gongsun Kang, Chinese warlord of Liaodong, establishes the Daifang Commandery in northern Korea.

204.title: Battle of
204.body: Battle of Ye: Warlord Cao Cao lays siege to and captures the military headquarter of Yuan Shao in Ye.

204.title: Lucius Fabius Cilo
204.body: Lucius Fabius Cilo and Marcus Annius Flavius Libo become Roman Consuls.

205.title: Hadrian's Wall
205.body: Hadrian's Wall is restored, after heavy raids by Caledonian tribes had overrun much of northern Britain.

205.title: Caracalla
205.body: Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and his brother Publius Septimius Geta Caesar become Roman Consuls.

205.title: Gaius Fulvius Plautianus
205.body: Gaius Fulvius Plautianus, a praetorian prefect and father-in-law of Caracalla, is assassinated.

205.title: Battle of Nanpi
205.body: Battle of Nanpi: Warlord Cao Cao defeats and kills Yuan Tan, the eldest son of his rival Yuan Shao.

205.title: Papinian
205.body: Aemilius Papinianus becomes praetorian prefect, after the death of Gaius Fulvius Plautianus.

206.title: Hadrian's Wall
206.body: Hadrian's Wall is retaken for the first time, since the Pictish uprising of 180.

206.title: Septimius Severus
206.body: Emperor Septimius Severus goes to Britain, with his sons Caracalla and Geta.

207.title: Battle of White Wolf Mountain
207.body: Battle of White Wolf Mountain: Warlord Cao Cao defeats the Wuhuan tribes, sending the Wuhuan into decline.

208.title: Britain is divided
208.body: Britain is divided: in the north, Lower Britain (Britannia Inferior) is administered from the fortress at Eburacum (modern York), and in the south, Upper Britain (Britannia Superior) is controlled by the legions at Deva Victrix (Chester) and Isca Augusta (Caerleon) with its capital at Londinium (London).

208.title: Septimius Severus
208.body: Emperor Septimius Severus leads an expedition (20,000 men) into Britannia, crosses Hadrian's Wall and moves through eastern Scotland. The Roman army pushes the Caledonians back to the River Tay and Severus signs a peace treaty. He repairs the Antonine Wall (his repairs are sometimes called the Severan Wall).

208.title: Caracalla
208.body: Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and his brother Publius Septimius Geta Caesar become Roman Consuls.

208.title: Cao Cao
208.body: October Warlord Cao Cao marches south with his army, and captures the enemy fleet at Jiangling. Battle of Changban: Warlord Liu Bei escapes from Cao Cao.

208.title: Battle of Changban
208.body: Battle of Changban: Warlord Liu Bei escapes from Cao Cao.

209.title: Scotland
209.body: Septimius Severus makes plans to subdue the land to the north of Scotland, ravaging it severely. Road-building and forest-clearing, the Roman army reaches Aber; Scottish tribes begin guerrilla warfare.

209.title: Satavahana dynasty
209.body: Chandra Shri Satakarni begins his reign, as ruler of the Satavahana Dynasty in Andhra Pradesh (approximate date).

209.title: Publius Septimius Geta
209.body: Publius Septimius Geta receives the titles of Imperator and Augustus from his father, Emperor Septimius Severus.

210.title: Scotland
210.body: Having suffered heavy losses since invading Scotland in 208, Emperor Septimius Severus sends his son - Caracalla - to systematically wipe out and torture the Scots into submission.

211.title: Eboracum
211.body: Eboracum becomes the capital of Britannia Inferior, a northern province of the Roman Empire.

211.title: Septimius Severus
211.body: Emperor Septimius Severus, having fallen ill, dies in Eboracum (modern-day York) while on campaign in Britain after an 18-year reign. He is later deified by the Senate. His sons Caracalla and Geta succeed him as joint Roman Emperors.

211.title: Praetorian Guard
211.body: Geta is lured to come without his bodyguards to meet Caracalla, to discuss a possible reconciliation. When he arrives the Praetorian Guard murders him, and he dies in the arms of his mother Julia Domna.

211.title: Ràng Xiàn Zì Míng Běn Zhì Lìng
211.body: January – Warlord Cao Cao writes Ràng Xiàn Zì Míng Běn Zhì Lìng (讓縣自明本志令)

211.title: Marcus I of Byzantium
211.body: Marcus I is succeeded by Philadelphus, as Patriarch of Constantinople.

212.title: Constitutio Antoniniana
212.body: The edict of Emperor Caracalla (Constitutio Antoniniana) extends Roman citizenship to all free inhabitants of the Roman Empire, with the exception of a limited group that may include Egyptians. The Jewish people are among those who receive citizenship. All free women in the Empire are given the same rights as Roman women.

212.title: Papinian
212.body: Roman jurist Papinian, one of the famous jurists who flourished during the reign of the late emperor Septimius Severus, refuses to write a legal defence for the murder of Caracalla's brother, Publius Septimius Geta. He is beheaded in Rome, in Caracalla's presence.

212.title: Edessa
212.body: Edessa in Mesopotamia becomes a Roman province.

212.title: Roman army
212.body: Caracalla quiets the objections of the Roman army to Geta's murder, by huge donations.

212.title: Baths of Caracalla
212.body: Construction begins on the Baths of Caracalla in Rome.

213.title: caracalla
213.body: Emperor Caracalla leaves Rome and expels some Germanic marauders from Gaul, while his mother, Julia Domna, rules the Empire. He defends the northern Rhine frontier against the Alamanni and the Chatti. Caracalla wins over the Germanic tribes on the banks of the River Main, and gives himself the title "Germanicus".

213.title: Wei Gong
213.body: Cao Cao, the prime minister of the Han dynasty, is titled Wei Gong (Duke of Wei) and given a fief of ten cities under his domain. This later becomes the Kingdom of Wei.

213.title: Battle of Ruxu
213.body: Battle of Ruxu: Cao Cao clashes against Sun Quan.

214.title: Osroene
214.body: The kingdom of Osroene becomes a province of the Roman Empire.

214.title: Korea
214.body: The Korean kingdom of Baekje attacks the Mohe tribes.

214.title: Caracalla
214.body: Caracalla's victories in Germany ensure his popularity within the Roman army.

214.title: Liu Bei
214.body: Liu Bei takes Yi Province from his clansman Liu Zhang, forming the later basis for Shu Han during the Three Kingdoms period.

214.title: Battle of Xiaoyao Ford
214.body: Battle of Xiaoyao Ford: General Zhang Liao under the command of Cao Cao beats back Sun Quan at Hefei.

215.title: Caracalla
215.body: Caracalla's troops massacre the population of Alexandria, Egypt, beginning with the leading citizens after the Alexandrian Revolts. The emperor was angry about a satire, produced in Alexandria, mocking his claim that he killed Geta in self-defense.

215.title: Roman coins
215.body: A coin, the Antoninianus, is introduced. The weight of this coin is a mere 1/50 of a pound. Copper disappears gradually, and by the middle of the third century, with Rome's economy in crisis, the Antonianus will be the only official currency.

215.title: Caucasian Albania
215.body: Vachagan I becomes king of Caucasian Albania.

215.title: Zhang Liao
215.body: Zhang Liao holds off Sun Quan's invasion force at the Battle of Xiaoyao Ford in Hefei, China.

216.title: Artabanus
216.body: Caracalla provokes a war with Artabanus V (of Parthia) to imitate his idol Alexander the Great. He crosses the Tigris, destroys towns and spoils the tombs of Arbela. The Roman army annexes Armenia.

216.title: Former Wei
216.body: Chinese warlord Cao Cao is made a vassal and ruler of Wei (Former Wei) by Emperor Xian, the last ruler of the Han Dynasty.

216.title: Thermae
216.body: The Baths of Caracalla in Rome are completed with public baths (Thermae), reading rooms, auditoriums, running tracks, and public gardens that cover 20 acres.

216.title: Caracalla
216.body: Emperor Caracalla tricks the Parthians by accepting a marriage proposal. He slaughters his bride and the wedding guests after the celebrations.

216.title: Leptis Magna
216.body: The basilica of Leptis Magna, ordered by Septimius Severus, is completed.

217.title: Stephen Glover
217.body: According to a tradition noted by 19th-century historian Stephen Glover, the earliest recorded game of association football (soccer) took place in Derby, England as a celebration on Shrove Tuesday, the day before commencement of the Lent season on Ash Wednesday, and 47 days before Easter Sunday

217.title: Papacy
217.body: The papacy of Zephyrinus ends. Callixtus I is elected as the sixteenth pope, but is opposed by the theologian Hippolytus who accuses him of laxity and of being a Modalist, one who denies any distinction between the three persons of the Trinity.

217.title: Julia Domna
217.body: Empress Julia, wife of Septimius Severus and mother of Caracalla and Geta, commits suicide.

217.title: Colosseum
217.body: The Colosseum is badly damaged by fire (lightning) which destroys the wooden upper levels of the amphitheater.

217.title: Artabanus V of Parthia
217.body: King Artabanus V signs a peace treaty with Rome after he receives 200 million sesterces, for the rebuilding of towns destroyed during the war in Parthia.

218.title: Julia Maesa
218.body: Julia Maesa, an aunt of the assassinated Emperor Caracalla, is banished to her home in Syria by the self-proclaimed emperor Macrinus, and declares her grandson Elagabalus, age 14, emperor of Rome.

218.title: Diadumenian
218.body: Diadumenianus, son of Macrinus, escapes to the Parthian court, but is captured at Zeugma and also put to death.

218.title: Battle of Antioch
218.body: Battle of Antioch: Elagabalus defeats, with the support of the Syrian legions, the forces of Macrinus. Macrinus flees, but is captured near Chalcedon and later executed in Cappadocia.

218.title: Silver
218.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius falls to 43 percent under the reign of Elagabalus, down from 50 percent under Septimius Severus, as he empties the treasury.

218.title: Ji Ping
218.body: Spring – Ji Ben (or Ji Ping), Chinese court physician, plots a rebellion in the imperial capital Xu (modern-day Xuchang), but the revolt is suppressed and the conspirators are captured and executed.

219.title: Cao Cao
219.body: Cao Cao controls the Yellow River basin and northern China. Sun Quan rules southern China. Liu Bei controls Yi Province (covering present-day Sichuan and Chongqing).

219.title: Satavahana
219.body: The reign of Pulona, Satavahana king of Andhra, begins in India.

219.title: Imperator
219.body: Imperator Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus (Elagabalus) and Quintus Tineius Sacerdos become Roman Consuls.

219.title: Gallica
219.body: Legions III Gallica and IV Scythica are disbanded by Elagabalus after their leaders, Verus and Gellius Maximus, rebel.

219.title: Guan
219.body: Guan Yu floods the fortress at Fan (present-day Fancheng District, Xiangyang, Hubei) in the Battle of Fancheng, while Lü Meng captures his base in Jing Province. Guan Yu retreats to Maicheng, falls into an ambush, and gets captured by Sun Quan's forces.

220.title: Goths
220.body: The Goths invade Asia Minor and the Balkans.

220.title: Cao Wei
220.body: The Wei dynasty gives official recognition to Taoism as its religious sect, and the sect's celestial masters reciprocate, by giving spiritual approbation to the Wei as successors to the Han. By the end of the century, most powerful families in northern China have subscribed to Daoist principles.

220.title: Ardashir
220.body: King Ardashir I, founder of the Sassanid dynasty, gains support from some Parthian sub-kings and revolts against the rule of Vologases VI. Ardashir, a grandson of Sasan, had ruled Persis since 208 and six years earlier gained control of the region surrounding Persepolis.

220.title: Cao
220.body: Cao Pi receives the abdication of Emperor Xian of Han and proclaims himself emperor of Cao Wei. This ends the Han dynasty, the former emperor being created Duke of Shanyang.

220.title: Elagabalus
220.body: An Indian delegation visits the Roman emperor Elagabalus.

221.title: Liu Bei
221.body: Liu Bei, Chinese warlord and descendant of the imperial clan of the Han dynasty, proclaims himself emperor in Chengdu, Sichuan, and establishes the state of Shu Han.

221.title: Elagabalus
221.body: Emperor Elagabalus adopts his cousin Alexander Severus as his heir, and receives the title of Caesar.

221.title: Aquilia Severa
221.body: July – Elagabalus is forced to divorce Aquilia Severa, and marries his third wife Annia Faustina. After five months he returns to Severa, and claims that the original divorce is invalid. The marriage is symbolic, because Elagabalus appears to be homosexual or bisexual.

222.title: Alexander Severus
222.body: Alexander Severus succeeds Elagabalus. He is only 13 years old; his mother, Julia Avita Mamaea, governs the Roman Empire with the help of Domitius Ulpianus and a council composed of 16 senators.

222.title: Elagabalus
222.body: Emperor Elagabalus is assassinated, along with his mother, Julia Soaemias, by the Praetorian Guard during a revolt. Their mutilated bodies are dragged through the streets of Rome before being thrown into the Tiber.

222.title: Silver
222.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius falls to 35 percent under emperor Alexander Severus, down from 43 percent under Elagabalus.

222.title: Pope Callixtus
222.body: Pope Callixtus I is killed by a mob in Rome's Trastevere after a 5-year reign in which he has stabilized the Saturday fast three times per year, with no food, oil, or wine to be consumed on those days. Callixtus is succeeded by Cardinal Urban I.

222.title: Battle of Xiaoting/Yiling
222.body: Battle of Xiaoting/Yiling: The Chinese state of Shu Han is defeated by Eastern Wu.

223.title: Battle of Dongkou
223.body: Battle of Dongkou: The Chinese state of Cao Wei is defeated by Eastern Wu.

224.title: Battle of Hormozdgan
224.body: Battle of Hormozdgan: King Ardashir I defeats Artabanus V, destroying the Parthian Empire, and establishing the Sassanid Dynasty. Artabanus V's brother Vologases VI will continue to rule, with Armenian and Kushan support, over outlying parts of Parthia.

224.title: bing'yin
224.body: (Huang'chu era, 4th year, 12th month and the bing'yin day) At the Cao Weis kingdom's capital at Xuchang, in what is now the Henan province of China, the Emperor Cao Pi issues a decree demoting the former Emperor Xian of Han to "Duke of Shanyang" and gives Xian's late daughter, Liu Man the posthumous title of "Princess of Changle"

224.title: Gaius Bellicius Flaccus Torquatus Tebanianus
224.body: Gaius Bellicius Torquatus and Appius Claudius Julianus II begin their one year terms as the new Roman consuls.

224.title: Sun Quan
224.body: September – In the Eastern Wu Kingdom, the Emperor Sun Quan orders the demotion of General Zhang Wen and his removal from command, commenting in the decree, "Oh, Zhang Wen, you are so fortunate to be spared from death." Two other officers, General Ji Yan and General Xu Biao are arrested and permitted to commit suicide rather than to be executed.

225.title: Ancient Rome
225.body: The first Christian paintings appear in Rome, decorating the Catacombs.

225.title: Alexander Severus
225.body: Emperor Alexander Severus marries Sallustia Orbiana, and possibly raises her father Seius Sallustius to the rank of Caesar.

226.title: Roman Empire
226.body: A merchant from the Roman Empire, called "Qin Lun" by the Chinese, arrives in Jiaozhi (modern Hanoi), and is taken to see King Sun Quan of Eastern Wu, who requests him to make a report on his native country and people. He is given an escort for the return trip, including a present of ten male and ten female "blackish-colored dwarfs." However, the officer in charge of the Chinese escort dies, and Qin Lun has to continue his journey home alone.

226.title: Ctesiphon
226.body: Ctesiphon, until now capital of the Parthian Empire, falls into the hands of the Sasanian Empire, who also make it their capital, after putting an end to the Parthian Dynasty in Iran.

227.title: Ardashir
227.body: King Ardashir I annexes his new empire from the east to the northwest. He conquers, with his army, the provinces of Chorasmia, Sistan and the island Bahrain in the Persian Gulf. The kings of the Kushan Empire and Turan recognize Ardashir as their overlord.

227.title: Dongcheon of Goguryeo
227.body: Dongcheon becomes ruler over the Korean kingdom of Goguryeo.

227.title: Seius Sallustius
227.body: Seius Sallustius is executed for the attempted murder of his son-in-law, Emperor Alexander Severus. Sallustius' daughter, as well as Alexander's wife, Sallustia Orbiana, is exiled to Libya.

227.title: Cormac mac Airt
227.body: The rule of High King Cormac mac Airt begins (approximate).

228.title: Ulpian
228.body: Domitius Ulpianus, a Roman jurist and prefect, is assassinated by the Praetorian Guard, in the presence of Emperor Severus Alexander. His curtailment of the privileges of the palace guard becomes Ulpianus' downfall, who in the course of a riot at Rome is murdered, between the soldiers and the mob.

228.title: Ardashir
228.body: King Ardashir I, four years after establishing the Sassanid Persian Empire, completes his conquest of Parthia.

228.title: February – May – Battle of Jieting
228.body: February – May – Battle of Jieting: The Cao Wei Kingdom decisively defeats the Shu Han Kingdom.

228.title: June –October – Battle of Shiting
228.body: June –October – Battle of Shiting: The Eastern Wu Kingdom defeats the Cao Wei Kingdom.

229.title: Merchant
229.body: Eastern Wu merchants reach Vietnam; ocean transport is improved to such an extent that sea journeys are made to the Liaodong Peninsula and the island of Yizhou.

229.title: Ammonius Saccas
229.body: Ammonius Saccas renews Greek philosophy by creating Neoplatonism.

229.title: Sun Quan
229.body: Chinese warlord Sun Quan formally declares himself emperor of the Eastern Wu state. The city of Jianye (modern Nanjing) is founded as the capital of Eastern Wu. The independent kingdoms in Cambodia and Laos become Eastern Wu vassals.

229.title: February – May – Battle of Jianwei
229.body: February – May – Battle of Jianwei: The state of Shu Han is victorious over the state of Cao Wei.

229.title: Alexander Severus
229.body: Emperor Alexander Severus and Dio Cassius are joint Consuls.

230.title: Ardashir
230.body: King Ardashir I of the Persian Empire invades the Roman province of Mesopotamia, and unsuccessfully besieges the fortress town of Nisibis (Turkey). His army threatens the border outposts of Syria and Cappadocia.

230.title: Alexander Severus
230.body: Emperor Alexander Severus decides that Thessaly should be a separate province from Macedonia. He increases taxes, in order to maintain the war against the Sassanids, and strengthen the defenses of the Roman Empire.

230.title: Pope Pontian
230.body: Pope Pontian succeeds Pope Urban I, as the 18th pope of Rome.

230.title: Roman army
230.body: Alexander Severus assembles the Roman army, and establishes his headquarters at Antioch. He attempts a diplomatic solution, but the Persians decline and choose war.

230.title: Castinus
230.body: Patriarch Castinus succeeds Ciriacus I as patriarch Constantinople.

231.title: Alexander Severus
231.body: Emperor Alexander Severus accompanies his mother Julia Mamaea to Syria, and campaigns against the Persians. Military command rests in the hands of his generals, but his presence gives additional weight to the empire's policy.

231.title: Origen
231.body: Origen, disciple of Ammonius Saccas, founder of Neoplatonism, is exiled in Caesarea.

231.title: March – August – Battle of Mount
231.body: March – August – Battle of Mount Qi: The Chinese state of Shu Han gains a tactical victory, and the state of Cao Wei a strategic victory.

232.title: status quo
232.body: Roman–Persian Wars: Emperor Alexander Severus launches a three-pronged counterattack against the Persian forces of King Ardashir I, who have invaded Mesopotamia. However, the Roman army advancing through Armenia is halted. Alexander gives the order to march to the capital at Ctesiphon, but the Romans are defeated, and withdraw to Syria.

232.title: Origen of Alexandria
232.body: Origen founds a school of Christian theology in Palestine.

232.title: Relic
232.body: Relics of St. Thomas are brought to Edessa from India.

232.title: Pope Heraclas of Alexandria
232.body: Pope Heraclas of Alexandria is the first Bishop of Alexandria to use the appellation of "Pope".

233.title: Limes Germanicus
233.body: Emperor Alexander Severus celebrates a triumph in Rome to observe his "victory" the previous year over the Persians (in reality, Severus Alexander advanced towards Ctesiphon in 233, but as corroborated by Herodian, his armies suffered a humiliating defeat against Ardashir I). He is soon summoned to the Rhine frontier, where the Alamanni invade what is now modern-day Swabia. German tribes destroy Roman forts, and plunder the countryside at the Limes Germanicus.

234.title: Alexander Severus
234.body: Emperor Alexander Severus and his mother Julia Mamaea move to Moguntiacum (modern Mainz), the capital of Germania Superior. His generals have planned a military offensive and built a bridge across the Rhine. Alexander prefers to negotiate for peace by buying off the Alemanni.
