234.title: Battle of Wuzhang Plains
234.body: Battle of Wuzhang Plains between the Chinese states of Shu Han and Cao Wei: The army of Shu Han retreats, following an inconclusive result.

234.title: Saban of Baekje
234.body: Saban becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Baekje. He is succeeded by Goi of Baekje later in the same year.

235.title: Pope Pontian
235.body: Pope Pontian resigns, the first to abdicate, because he and Hippolytus, church leader of Rome, are exiled to the mines of Sardinia. Emperor Maximinus persecutes the Christians.

235.title: Pope Anterus
235.body: Anterus succeeds Pontian as the nineteenth pope of Rome.

235.title: Severus Alexander
235.body: Emperor Severus Alexander and his mother Iulia Mamaea are murdered by their own soldiers. The soldiers proclaim Maximinus Thrax as emperor. The Severan dynasty ends, marking the beginning of the Crisis of the Third Century.

236.title: Pope Fabian
236.body: Pope Fabian succeeds Pope Anterus as the twentieth pope.

236.title: Ancient Rome
236.body: Fabian separates Rome into seven deaconships.

236.title: Missionary
236.body: Fabian sends seven missionaries to Gaul to evangelize in the large cities.

236.title: Maximinus Thrax
236.body: Emperor Maximinus Thrax and Marcus Pupienus Africanus Maximus become Roman consuls.

236.title: Dacians
236.body: Maximinus campaigns against Dacians and Sarmatians from his supply depot at Sirmium.

237.title: Maximinus Thrax
237.body: Emperor Maximinus Thrax campaigns on the rivers Danube and Rhine in Germania, defeating the Alemanni, and never visits Rome. He is accepted by the Roman Senate, but taxes the rich aristocracy heavily, and engenders such hostility among them, that they plot against him. He had a road rebuilt in what is now the Golan Heights, between 236 and 237.

237.title: Eugenius I of Byzantium
237.body: Patriarch Eugenius I succeeds Patriarch Castinus as Patriarch of Constantinople.

237.title: Saint Babylas
237.body: Saint Babylas becomes Patriarch of Antioch.

237.title: Ardashir I of Persia
237.body: King Ardashir I of Persia renews his attacks on the Roman province of Mesopotamia.

238.title: Parthica
238.body: Maximinus advances to the town Aquileia in northern Italy; his army suffers from famine and disease, while the city is besieged. Soldiers of Legio II Parthica kill him in his tent, along with his son Maximinus (who is appointed co-emperor). Their corpses are decapitated and their heads carried to Rome.

238.title: Ukraine
238.body: The Goths, coming from Ukraine, cross the Danube and devastate the Roman Empire up to the border with Anatolia.

238.title: Augusta
238.body: April – Battle of Carthage: Numidian forces loyal to Maximinus invade Africa with support of Legio III Augusta. Gordian II is killed and after a siege, Gordian I commits suicide by hanging himself with his belt.

238.title: Colosseum
238.body: The Colosseum is restored after being damaged.

238.title: Maximinus Thrax
238.body: Emperor Maximinus Thrax campaigns against the Carpians on the Danube in Moesia (Balkans). In spite of the payment of a tribute, the Romans fail to persuade the Goths and the Germanic tribes.

239.title: Eastern
239.body: A Chinese expeditionary force from the Eastern Wu state discovers the island of Taiwan.

239.title: Origen
239.body: Origen publishes the Old Testament in five languages (approximate date).

239.title: Cao Fang
239.body: Cao Fang succeeds his adoptive father Cao Rui as emperor of the Cao Wei state, in the Three Kingdoms period of China.

239.title: Yamataikoku
239.body: Queen of Yamataikoku (Japan), Himiko was given Golden signature stamp by Cao Wei (China)

240.title: Roman Empire
240.body: The Roman Empire is threatened on several fronts at the same time. Africa revolts and tribes in northwest Germania, under the name of the Franks, are raiding the Rhine frontier.

240.title: Shapur
240.body: Prince Shapur I becomes co-ruler of the Sasanian Empire with his father King Ardashir I.

240.title: Mani
240.body: Mani, a young mystic of Ctesiphon, proclaims himself a prophet at the court of Ardashir I. He preaches his doctrine, Manichaeism, throughout the Sassanid Empire.

240.title: Gupta
240.body: Maharaja Sri-Gupta becomes ruler of the Gupta Empire (approximate date).

240.title: Siege of Hatra
240.body: Siege of Hatra: The Sasanians besiege the capital of the Kingdom of Hatra ruled by Sanatruq II.

241.title: Kushan Empire
241.body: Shapur I annexes parts of the Kushan Empire. The ancient city of Bagram (modern Afghanistan) is abandoned.

241.title: Dura-Europos church
241.body: The Dura-Europos church is converted from a house in Syria (approximate date).

241.title: de facto
241.body: Gaius Furius Sabinius Aquila Timesitheus becomes praetorian prefect and de facto ruler of the Roman Empire.

241.title: Samhain
241.body: The Battle of Samhain is fought in Ireland (approximate date).

241.title: Gordian III
241.body: Winter – Emperor Gordian III reaches Antioch and, with his army, prepares an offensive against the Sassanids.

242.title: Cimmerians
242.body: Gordian III evacuates the Cimmerian cities in the Bosphorus (Crimea), as the territory is now controlled by the Goths.

242.title: Gordian III
242.body: Emperor Gordian III begins a campaign against King Shapur I, and leads victories at Antioch, Carrhae, Nisibis, and Resaina.

242.title: Antioch
242.body: Shapur I makes a pre-emptive attack on Antioch to drive out the Romans. Gordian's father-in-law, Timesitheus, leads a Roman army to defeat the Sassanids at Carrhae and Nisibis.

242.title: Titus of Byzantium
242.body: Patriarch Titus succeeds Eugenius I as Patriarch of Constantinople (until 272).

242.title: Ardashir
242.body: King Ardashir I, founder of the Sassanid Empire, dies after a 30-year reign. He is succeeded by his son and co-ruler Shapur I.

243.title: Battle of Resaena
243.body: Battle of Resaena: A Roman army under Timesitheus defeats the Sassanids at Resaena (modern Syria); King Shapur I is forced to flee to the Euphrates.

243.title: Gordian III
243.body: Emperor Gordian III appoints Philip the Arab as his new praetorian prefect (after the death of Timesitheus) and proceeds with his campaign in Mesopotamia.

243.title: Sassanid Empire
243.body: Timesitheus becomes ill and dies under suspicious circumstances. Shapur I retreats to the Sassanid Empire, giving up all the territories he has conquered.

243.title: Kingdom of Funan
243.body: Fan Chan of Funan sends a tribute mission to China (approximate date).

243.title: Cohors I Ubiorum
243.body: Cohors I Ubiorum, the garrison at Capidava in Scythia Minor, is replaced by Cohors I Germanorum Civium Romanorum, until the end of the 3rd century AD.

244.title: Dura-Europos synagogue
244.body: 244–245 – Last phase of construction of the house-style Dura-Europos synagogue in Syria, one of the oldest to survive (wall-paintings in the National Museum of Damascus, Syria).

244.title: Philip the Arab
244.body: Philip the Arab (Marcus Julius Philippus) declares himself co-emperor, and makes a controversial peace with the Sassanian Empire, withdrawing from their territory and giving Shapur 500,000 gold pieces. The Sassanians occupy Armenia.

244.title: Gordian III
244.body: Emperor Gordian III is murdered by mutinous soldiers in Zaitha (Mesopotamia). A mound is raised at Carchemish in his memory.

244.title: Plotinus
244.body: Plotinus, Greek philosopher, escapes the bloodshed that accompanies the murder of Gordianus III and makes his way to Antioch. Back in Rome he founds his Neoplatonist school and attracts disciples like Porphyry, Castricius Firmus and Eustochius of Alexandria.

244.title: Silver
244.body: The silver content of the Roman denarius falls to 0.5 percent under emperor Philippus I, down from 28 percent under Gordian III.

245.title: Plotinus
245.body: The philosopher Plotinus goes to live in Rome.

245.title: Philip the Arab
245.body: Emperor Philip the Arab entrusts Trajan Decius with an important command on the Danube.

245.title: Roman Britain
245.body: In Britain, many thousands of acres of modern-day Lincolnshire are inundated by a great flood.

245.title: Lady Triệu
245.body: Lady Triệu, a Vietnamese warrior, begins her 3-year resistance against the invading Chinese.

246.title: Philip the Arab
246.body: Emperor Philip the Arab fights the Germans along the Danube River.

246.title: Councils of Arabia
246.body: The first of two Councils of Arabia in the Roman Christian Church is held in Bostra, Arabia Petraea.

246.title: Baekje
246.body: Baekje Kingdom under King Goi of Baekje attacks the Chinese commandery of Daifang.

247.title: Ludi Saeculares
247.body: Rome becomes 1,000 years old. The 1,000th anniversary is commemorated with the Ludi Saeculares festivals, celebrated throughout the Roman Empire.

247.title: Goths
247.body: The Goths appear on the lower Danube frontier; they invade Ukraine and Romania.

247.title: Philip the Arab
247.body: Marcus Julius Philippus Augustus and his 10-year-old son Marcus Julius Philippus Caesar become Roman Consuls.

247.title: Cheomhae of Silla
247.body: Cheomhae becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Silla.

247.title: Councils of Arabia
247.body: The last of the two Councils of Arabia in the Roman Christian Church is held in Bostra, Arabia Petraea.

248.title: ludi saeculares
248.body: The Roman Empire continues the celebration of the 1,000th anniversary of Rome, with the ludi saeculares, organized by Philip the Arab.

248.title: Origen
248.body: Origen writes an eight-volume work, criticizing the pagan writer Celsus.

248.title: Pacatianus
248.body: The revolts of Pacatianus in Moesia and Iotapianus in Syria are put down by Senator Trajan Decius, by order of Emperor Philip the Arab.

248.title: Jungcheon of Goguryeo
248.body: Jungcheon becomes ruler of the Korean kingdom of Goguryeo (until 270).

249.title: Decius
249.body: Trajan Decius puts down a revolt in Moesia and Pannonia. Loyal legionaries proclaim him emperor, and he leads them into Italy.

249.title: Decian persecution
249.body: Decian persecution: Decius begins persecuting Christians, and others refusing to sacrifice publicly to the Roman gods for the well-being of the emperor.

249.title: Cyprian
249.body: Cyprian, Christian writer of Berber descent, becomes bishop of Carthage.

249.title: Alexandria
249.body: In Alexandria, the populace pillages the homes of Christians.

249.title: Battle of Verona
249.body: Battle of Verona: Decius defeats and kills Emperor Philip the Arab.

250.title: Ethiopia
250.body: An epidemic begins in Ethiopia, moves into Egypt and the Roman colonies in North Africa, and spreads through the Roman Empire (named the Plague of Cyprian, after St. Cyprian, bishop of Carthage).

250.title: Decian persecution
250.body: Decian persecution of Christians is initiated when Emperor Decius orders everyone in the Roman Empire (except Jews) to perform a sacrifice to the gods of religion in ancient Rome. On January 20, Pope Fabian becomes one of the first martyrs of this persecution.

250.title: Saint Denis of Paris
250.body: Possible date – Denis, a bishop of Paris, is martyred by beheading.

250.title: Family of Vunnerius Keramus
250.body: Approximate date The family portrait medallion, traditionally called the Family of Vunnerius Keramus, is made (it is later placed in the Brescia Cross, and then in the Museo Civico dell'Etá Cristiana, Brescia). The Ludovisi Battle sarcophagus, depicting battle between the Romans and the Barbarians, is made for use in Rome (it is later moved to the collection of the National Roman Museum). The Igel Column is erected at Trier in Germany.

250.title: Ludovisi Battle sarcophagus
250.body: The Ludovisi Battle sarcophagus, depicting battle between the Romans and the Barbarians, is made for use in Rome (it is later moved to the collection of the National Roman Museum).

251.title: Sassanid
251.body: Sassanid King Shapur I orders an invasion of Syria, with the intent of finally capturing the city of Antioch during the campaign of 251–254.

251.title: Plague of Cyprian
251.body: A 15-year plague begins in the Roman Empire.

251.title: Pope Cornelius
251.body: March – Pope Cornelius succeeds Pope Fabian as the 21st pope.

251.title: Ancient Rome
251.body: In Rome, Hostilian, son of Decius, succeeds his father, while Trebonianus Gallus is proclaimed Emperor by the troops. Gallus accepts him as co-emperor, but an outbreak of plague strikes the city, and kills Hostilian.

251.title: Battle of Abritus
251.body: Battle of Abritus: The Goths defeat Emperor Decius and his son Herennius Etruscus, on swampy ground in the Dobruja (Moesia).

252.title: Rebellion
252.body: Shapur I puts down the revolt in Khorasan (Iran and Turkmenistan), and rejoins his army.

252.title: Battle of Barbalissos
252.body: Battle of Barbalissos: King Shapur I defeats the Roman army (some 70,000 men) at Barbalissos in Syria (approximate date).

252.title: Armenia
252.body: Shapur I invades Armenia, and appoints Artavazd VI as the new Armenian king.

252.title: Georgia
252.body: Georgia submits peacefully to Shapur I, and becomes a vassal of the Sassanid Empire.

252.title: Pope Cornelius
252.body: Pope Cornelius is exiled to Centumcellae, by Emperor Trebonianus Gallus.

253.title: Battle of Barbalissos
253.body: Battle of Barbalissos: King Shapur I, defeats a Roman field army at Barbalissos in Syria.

253.title: Pope Lucius
253.body: Pope Lucius I succeeds Pope Cornelius as the 22nd pope.

253.title: Roman legion
253.body: The legions who have campaigned against the Goths on the Danube elect Marcus Aemilius Aemilianus as new emperor. He advances on Rome along the Flaminian Way, to meet his opponent emperor Trebonianus Gallus and his son Volusianus. For the most part, generals in the border regions are proclaimed emperor by their armies to halt the invasion of Germanic tribes.

253.title: Augustus
253.body: Aemilianus is murdered at Spoletium and Publius Valerianus, age 60, is recognised as the new emperor by the Rhine legions. He gives his son Publius Licinius Egnatius Gallienus the title Augustus. Valerianus I dispatches him to the Danube where the Goths have violated the treaty signed with Rome and invaded Moesia.

253.title: Augusta
253.body: Valerian reforms Legio III Augusta to fight the "five peoples", a dangerous coalition of Berber tribes in Africa.

254.title: The Roman Empire is threatened by several peoples on their borders
254.body: The Roman Empire is threatened by several peoples on their borders: the Germanic confederations, such as the Franks on the Middle Rhine, the Alemanni on the upper Rhine and Danube, and the Marcomanni facing the provinces at Noricum and Raetia. On land the confederation of Goths threaten the lower Danube provinces, and on the sea they threaten the shores of Thracia, Bithynia et Pontus, and Cappadocia. In the eastern provinces, the Sassanid Persians had the previous year defeated a Roman field army at Barballisos, and afterwards plundered the defenseless provinces.

254.title: Pope Stephen
254.body: Pope Stephen I succeeds Pope Lucius I.

255.title: Ma Jun
255.body: Ma Jun, Chinese mechanical engineer from Cao Wei, invents the south-pointing chariot, a path-finding directional compass vehicle that uses a differential gear, not magnetics.

255.title: Sima Zhao
255.body: Sima Zhao, Sima Shi's younger brother, inherits his brother's authority.

255.title: Sima Shi
255.body: Sima Shi quells Guanqiu Jian and Wen Qin's rebellion.

256.title: Shapur
256.body: King Shapur I of the Sasanian Empire invades Mesopotamia and Syria. He conquers and plunders Antioch, destroys Dura-Europos, and sacks the Anatolian city of Zeugma on the Euphrates. A devastating fire and an earthquake soon follow, causing Zeugma to be abandoned.

256.title: Goths
256.body: The Goths invade Asia Minor. Dacia is lost for the Roman Empire, and the Goths appear at the walls of Thessalonica.

256.title: Franks
256.body: The Franks cross the Rhine; the Alemanni reach Mediolanum (Milan) (disputed date).

256.title: Pope Stephen
256.body: Pope Stephen I threatens to excommunicate Cyprian, bishop of Carthage, and other bishops in Africa and Asia Minor, unless they stop rebaptizing heretics. Cyprian attacks the Pope in a treatise that gains support from the Council of Carthage. He sends envoys to Rome, raising the specter of a schism between the Roman and Carthaginian Churches.

256.title: Africa
256.body: In Africa, the Berbers massacre Roman colonists.

257.title: Antioch
257.body: Emperor Valerian recovers Antioch, Syria from King Shapur I of Persia.

257.title: Limes Germanicus
257.body: In Bavaria the Limes Germanicus (Upper Raetian Limes) along the river Iller is abandoned by the Romans.

257.title: Pope Sixtus
257.body: Pope Sixtus II succeeds Pope Stephen I as the 24th pope.

257.title: Gallienus
257.body: Emperor Gallienus brings some order to the Danube area.

257.title: Aurelian
257.body: Future emperor Aurelian defeats the Goths and brings many prisoners back to Rome.

258.title: Edict
258.body: A second Imperial edict prohibits Christianity in the Roman Empire. This edict divides Christians into four categories: priests, who are to be put to death; senators and equestrians, who are to be stripped of their positions and their property confiscated; nuns, who are to be exiled; and imperial civil servants, who are condemned to forced labour.

258.title: Sixtus
258.body: Pope Sixtus II, bishop of Rome, is martyred.

258.title: Goths
258.body: The Goths ravage Asia Minor and Trapezus.

258.title: Silver
258.body: The amount of silver in the Roman currency of the denarius falls below 10%. The crisis ruins craftsmen, tradesmen, and small farmers, who are forced into bartering; landowners grow richer by buying up cheap land.

258.title: Cyprian
258.body: Cyprian, bishop of Carthage, is martyred (decapitation).

259.title: Postumus
259.body: Postumus revolts against Gallienus in Gaul. The western provinces of Britain and Spain join his independent realm—which is called in modern times the Gallic Empire.

259.title: Valerian
259.body: Emperor Valerian leads an army (70,000 men) to relieve Edessa, besieged by the forces of Persian King Shapur I. An outbreak of a plague kills many legionaries, weakening the Roman position in Syria.

259.title: Franks
259.body: The Franks, who invaded the Roman Empire near Cologne in 257, reach Tarraco in Hispania.

259.title: Mesopotamia
259.body: Mesopotamia: Odaenathus, the ruler of the kingdom of Palmyra, sacks the city of Nehardea, destroying its great yeshiva.

259.title: Pope Dionysius
259.body: Pope Dionysius is elected as the pope.

260.title: Mediolanum
260.body: Gallienus establishes himself at Mediolanum (modern Milan); he reorganizes the army, supported by elite cavalry, and dispatches troops to the Rhine frontier.

260.title: Gallienus
260.body: Gallienus becomes the sole emperor of Rome; during his reign the Pannonian governor Ingenuus revolts on the Danube.

260.title: Augustus
260.body: Saloninus, son of Gallienus, is proclaimed Augustus by his troops. Postumus besieges Cologne, where Silvanus is praetorian prefect and Roman ruler of Gaul.

260.title: Pope Dionysius
260.body: Pope Dionysius convenes a synod at Rome to demand an explanation from bishop Dionysius of Alexandria, who has been charged with separating the members of the Trinity as three distinct deities.

260.title: coup d'état
260.body: Cao Mao is killed in a coup d'état against Sima Zhao. The 14-year-old Cao Huan becomes ruler of Former Wei, but the Sima clan controls the state.

261.title: Gallienus
261.body: Emperor Gallienus crushes the Alemanni at Milan (approximate date).

261.title: Gallienus usurpers
261.body: Gallienus usurpers: The rebellion of Macrianus Major, Macrianus Minor, and Quietus against Gallienus comes to an end. They march from Asia to Europe but they are defeated in Thrace by Gallienus' general Aureolus, and both Macrianus Major and Macrianus Minor are killed. Quietus flees to Emesa, where he is killed by Odaenathus of Palmyra.

261.title: Michu of Silla
261.body: Michu of Silla ascends to the Korean throne of Silla, becoming the first ruler of the Long Kim line.

261.title: Roman–Persian Wars
261.body: Roman–Persian Wars: Balista, Roman usurper, collects ships from Cilician ports and defeats a Persian raiding force near Pompeiopolis.

261.title: Edict
261.body: Gallienus repeals the edict of 258, which led to the persecution of the Christians.

262.title: Heruli
262.body: The Heruls accompany the Goths, ravaging the coasts of the Black Sea and the Aegean.

262.title: Goths
262.body: The Goths invade Asia Minor and destroy the Temple of Artemis in Ephesus.

262.title: 262 Southwest Anatolia earthquake
262.body: An earthquake strikes Ephesus and Pergamon and another strikes Cyrene.

263.title: The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art
263.body: Chinese mathematician Liu Hui writes a commentary on The Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art, describing what will later be called Gaussian elimination, computing pi, etc.

263.title: Dux Orientalis
263.body: King Odenathus of Palmyra declares himself ruler of the area west of the River Euphrates and is declared Dux Orientalis by the Roman emperor Gallienus.

263.title: Conquest of Shu by Wei
263.body: Conquest of Shu by Wei: The Chinese state of Cao Wei conquers Shu Han, one of its two rival states.

263.title: Duke of Jin
263.body: Sima Zhao, regent of the Cao Wei state, receives and accepts the nine bestowments, state chancellorship, and the title Duke of Jin from Cao Huan.

264.title: Sima Zhao
264.body: Sima Zhao, regent of the Chinese state of Cao Wei, styles himself the "King of Jin", the penultimate step before usurpation.

264.title: Sun Hao
264.body: Sun Hao succeeds Sun Xiu as emperor of the Chinese state of Eastern Wu.

264.title: Zhong Hui's Rebellion
264.body: Zhong Hui's Rebellion in China is quelled.

265.title: Gallienus
265.body: Emperor Gallienus tries twice to crush the usurper Postumus, but on the first occasion Aureolus, commander of the elite cavalry, carelessly lets him escape. The second time, Gallienus sustains an arrow wound and has to break off his siege of a Gallic town where Postumus has holed up. He makes no other serious attempt to overcome his rival, instead devoting his attention to the political and military problems in the Danube and eastern parts of the Roman Empire.

265.title: Goths
265.body: Gallienus repels the invasion of the Goths in the Balkans.

265.title: Milan
265.body: Gallienus gives the order to fortify Milan and Verona.

265.title: Ancient Rome
265.body: Postumus makes no move to march on Rome and claim his territory south of Gaul.

265.title: Sima Zhao
265.body: Sima Zhao, who had been the regent and de facto primary authority of the state of Cao Wei for little over 10 years by this point, passes away, leaving his authority to his eldest son, Sima Yan, who will go on to disestablish the state of Cao Wei in February 266, founding the Jin dynasty.

266.title: Odaenathus
266.body: King Odaenathus of Palmyra invades Persia to conquer the capital, Ctesiphon, and twice comes as far as the walls of the Persian capital, but fails to take it. After his victories in the East, he pronounces himself with the title "king of kings".

266.title: Emperor Wu of Jin
266.body: Sima Yan, regent of the Chinese state of Cao Wei, forces the last Cao Wei emperor Cao Huan to abdicate in his favour. The Cao Wei state's existence comes to an end. Sima Yan establishes the Jin Dynasty, and becomes its first emperor on 8 February, and is historically known as "Wu of Jin".

266.title: Cormac mac Airt
266.body: The rule of High King Cormac mac Airt ends (approximate date).

266.title: Flood of the Nile
266.body: A powerful tropical volcanic eruption around this year brings a below-average flood of the Nile next year.

267.title: First Gothic invasion
267.body: First Gothic invasion: The Goths, originally from Scandinavia, with the Sarmatians (from modern Iran), invade the Balkans and Greece. They ravage Moesia and Thrace.

267.title: Aureolus
267.body: Aureolus, charged with defending Italy, defeats Victorinus (co-emperor of Gaul), is proclaimed emperor by his troops, and begins his march on Rome.

267.title: Athens
267.body: The Goths sack several cities of southern Greece including Athens, Corinth, Argos and Sparta. After the Sack of Athens, an Athenian militia force (2,000 men), under the historian Dexippus, pushes the invaders to the north where they are intercepted by the Roman army under emperor Gallienus. He wins an important victory near the Nestos River, on the boundary between Macedonia and Thrace.

267.title: Heruli
267.body: The Heruli invade the Black Sea coast; they unsuccessfully attack Byzantium and Cyzicus. The Roman fleet defeats the Herulian fleet (500 ships) but allows them to escape into the Aegean Sea, where they raid the islands of Lemnos and Skyros.

267.title: Odaenathus
267.body: King Septimius Odaenathus of Palmyra makes plans for a campaign in Cappadocia against the Goths. He is assassinated, along with his eldest son, most probably by his nephew due to a previous altercation between him and Odaenathus. His wife Zenobia succeeds him, and rules Vaballathus (the Palmyrene Empire) with her young son.

268.title: Alamanni
268.body: The Alamanni invade Italy north of the Po River.

268.title: Victorinus
268.body: Victorinus is declared emperor of the Gallic Empire by the legions at Augusta Treverorum (Trier), following the murders of his predecessors. He is recognized by the provinces of Gaul and Britain, but Hispania might have reunited with the Roman Empire.

268.title: September – Battle of Naissus
268.body: September – Battle of Naissus: Emperor Gallienus, aided by Aurelian, defeats a Gothic coalition (50,000 warriors) near Naissus (Niš, modern Serbia).

268.title: Mediolanum
268.body: Gallienus is killed by his senior officers at Mediolanum (Milan) while besieging his rival Aureolus. Aureolus is murdered in turn by the Praetorian guard.

268.title: Claudius Gothicus
268.body: Marcus Aurelius Claudius, who may have murdered Gallienus, becomes the new emperor of Rome and will reign as Claudius II.

269.title: Zenobia
269.body: Queen Zenobia conquers Syria, Palestine, Lebanon, parts of Mesopotamia and Anatolia and Egypt, giving her control of Rome's grain supply. The library at Alexandria is partly burned during a raid by Zabdas, general of Zenobia.

269.title: Heruli
269.body: The Heruli capture Athens and raid the Aegean Islands as far as Crete and Rhodes.

269.title: Second Gothic invasion
269.body: Second Gothic invasion: The Goths and other German tribes attack Bosphorean towns on the coast of the Black Sea. Some 2,000 ships and 320,000 men from the Danube enter Roman territory. Emperor Claudius II defeats the invaders and receives the title Gothicus for his triumph.

269.title: Pope Felix
269.body: Pope Felix I succeeds Dionysius as the 26th pope of Rome.

269.title: Sirmium
269.body: Claudius II travels to Sirmium and prepares a war against the Vandals, who raid Pannonia.

270.title: Aurelian
270.body: Lucius Domitius Aurelianus (or Aurelian), the cavalry commander who distinguished himself in the previous year at the Battle of Naissus (Serbia), usurps power in Sirmium and marches against Quintillus in Aquileia. Quintillus commits suicide.

270.title: Vandals
270.body: Claudius dies of plague in Sirmium while preparing to fight the Vandals and Sarmatians, who have invaded Pannonia. He is succeeded by his brother Quintillus, who briefly holds power over the Roman Empire.

270.title: Anthony the Great
270.body: Anthony the Great, a Christian saint from Egypt, regarded as "Father of All Monks", enters the wilderness to become ascetic.

270.title: Kingdom of Aksum
270.body: The Kingdom of Aksum (modern Ethiopia) begins minting its own gold coins to facilitate international trade, following the model of Roman coinage.

270.title: Zenobia
270.body: Zenobia seizes control of Roman Arabia and Egypt.

271.title: Shapur
271.body: King Shapur I builds the Academy of Gundishapur (Iran), which becomes the intellectual center of the Sassanid Empire.

271.title: Germanicus Maximus
271.body: Battle of Placentia: The Iuthungi invade Italy and sack the city of Piacenza. A Roman army under Emperor Aurelian is ambushed and defeated. Battle of Fano: The Iuthungi move towards a defenseless Rome.

271.title: Dacia
271.body: Aurelian withdraws Rome's administrative and military presence from Dacia (modern Romania), thereby rationalizing the Danube frontier and freeing resources for the forthcoming campaign against Zenobia.

271.title: Victorinus
271.body: Victorinus, Emperor of the Gallic Empire, is assassinated by one of his officers, Attitianus, reportedly for reasons of personal revenge. He is succeeded by Tetricus I, who is elevated with the help of Victorinus' mother Victoria.

271.title: Aurelian
271.body: After an indecisive battle, Emperor Aurelian defeats the Vandals, and forces them from Pannonia, and across the Danube.

272.title: clibanarii
272.body: Battle of Immae: Aurelian defeats the Palmyrene heavy cavalry (clibanarii) near Antioch. Queen Zenobia flees under cover of darkness to Emesa (Syria).

272.title: Saint Denis of Paris
272.body: Saint Denis, first Bishop of Paris, and two of his disciples are beheaded on the road to the Temple of Mercury that stands atop a hill outside of the city. The hill will later be called Montmartre (Mountain of Martyrs) in Lutetia (modern Paris).

272.title: Palmyra
272.body: Aurelian besieges Palmyra. Zenobia attempts to escape to Persia but is captured on the Euphrates. Palmyra surrenders soon after.

272.title: Aurelian
272.body: Emperor Aurelian launches a two-pronged invasion of the Palmyrene Empire, sending his commander Marcus Aurelius Probus to restore Roman rule in Egypt while he marches into Asia Minor.

272.title: Dometius of Byzantium
272.body: Dometius succeeds Titus as Patriarch of Constantinople.

273.title: Alexandria
273.body: In bitter street-fighting, Aurelian crushes a rebellion in Alexandria by Firmus, a sympathizer of Palmyra. Firmus is strangled to death.

273.title: Marcus Claudius Tacitus
273.body: Marcus Claudius Tacitus, future Roman Emperor, is consul in Rome.

273.title: Hormizd
273.body: King Hormizd I of Persia dies after a brief reign in which he has shown tolerance toward the ascetic, anti-materialist Manichean faith. He is succeeded by his brother Bahram I, who has been governing the province of Atropatene. Bahram proceeds to crush a rebellion by various vassal kings.

273.title: Aurelian
273.body: Emperor Aurelian defeats an incursion by the Carpi into Moesia and Thrace.

273.title: Palmyra
273.body: Aurelian sacks the city of Palmyra, after putting down a second revolt.

274.title: Mani
274.body: Mani, a sage of Persia, dies at Gundeshapur after 30 years of preaching his "heresy" at the court of the late Sassanian King Shapur I and on long journeys to Khorasan, India and China. He is executed or allowed to die in prison, and claims to be a prophet of God. Mani combines Zoroastrian dualism with Christian theology, and his disciples gain wide support for Manichaeism, despite opposition from Roman Emperors.

274.title: Battle of Châlons
274.body: Battle of Châlons: The Emperor Aurelian invades Gaul to campaign against the Gallic Empire (Gaul and Britain). In the Catalaunian Plains, the Romano-Gallic Emperor Tetricus I surrenders to Aurelian and leaves his army without an emperor. The Gallic army is then crushed by Aurelian in a major battle.

274.title: Aksumite Empire
274.body: The Kingdom of Aksum attains great prosperity thanks to its control of Red Sea trade.

274.title: Restitutor Orbis
274.body: Rome greets Aurelian as Restitutor Orbis ("Restorer of the World") and accords him a magnificent triumph (victory procession), which is graced by his captives Zenobia, Tetricus I, and his son Tetricus II.

274.title: Germanic peoples
274.body: Germanic tribes take advantage of the destroyed Roman forces of the Rhine to raid Gaul.

275.title: Byzantium
275.body: November – Aurelian is assassinated near Byzantium (Turkey). Aurelian had developed a reputation for punishing corruption with severity, and his secretary Eros was under suspicion. As a result, Eros, fearing for his life, forged a list of high-ranking officers marked for execution.

275.title: Himyar
275.body: Approximate date – Himyar king Shammar Yahri'sh conquers Hadhramaut, Najran and Tihamah, unifying the territory later known as Yemen.

275.title: Goths
275.body: The Goths begin to raid Thrace and Asia Minor. Aurelian begins a campaign against the Goths in Thrace.

275.title: Pope Eutychian
275.body: Eutychian succeeds Felix I as the 27th pope of Rome.

275.title: Aurelian
275.body: Emperor Aurelian puts down unrest in Gaul, and defeats Germanic incursions into Gaul and Raetia (these problems had been caused by Aurelian's defeat and overthrow of the Gallic Empire).

276.title: Bahram
276.body: King Bahram I of Persia dies after a 3-year reign, in which the Zoroastrian priests at Ctesiphon (Iran) put pressure on him to persecute Buddhists, Christians, and Manichaeans. He is succeeded by his son Bahram II.

276.title: Cilicia
276.body: Florianus holds power for some weeks and fights indecisively against Probus in Cilicia, but his soldiers, many of whom are from the colder Rhine and Danube frontiers, suffer from heat and disease. He is overthrown and then assassinated by his own troops near Tarsus (Turkey), in collusion with Probus. Probus, age 44, is proclaimed new Emperor of Rome.

276.title: Roman Emperor
276.body: Florianus becomes Roman Emperor with the support of the Senate, but a general in the east, Marcus Aurelius Probus, usurps power against him. Florianus breaks off his campaign against the Goths and marches east from the Bosporus with support from the Roman legions in Britain, Gaul, Spain and Italy.

276.title: Goths
276.body: Tacitus campaigns successfully against the Goths who have invaded Asia Minor, and his half-brother, the praetorian prefect Marcus Annius Florianus, continues the campaign.

276.title: Marcus Claudius Tacitus
276.body: Emperor Tacitus doubles the silver content of the aurelianianus, and halves its tariffing to 2.5 d.c. They carry the value marks X.I.

277.title: Gothicus
277.body: Emperor Probus travels with his army west across the Sea of Marmara (Turkey), and through the provinces of Thrace, Moesia, and Pannonia to defeat the Goths along the lower Danube. He acquires from the troops the title of Gothicus.

277.title: Ancient Rome
277.body: Probus enters Rome, to have his position as Emperor ratified by the Senate.

277.title: Tuoba Xilu
277.body: Tuoba Xilu succeeds his father Tuoba Liwei, as chieftain of the Tuoba clan.

278.title: Gothicus Maximus
278.body: Probus resettles the Germanic tribes in the devastated provinces of the Roman Empire. He adopts the titles of Gothicus Maximus and Germanicus Maximus.

278.title: Piracy along the coast of Lycia et Pamphylia
278.body: Piracy along the coast of Lycia et Pamphylia: The Romans besiege the city of Cremna (Pisidia) and kill the Isaurian robber Lydius.

278.title: Marcus Aurelius Probus
278.body: Emperor Probus defeats the Alamanni, advancing through the Neckar Valley. He expels the Franks from Gaul, and reorganizes the Roman defenses on the Rhine.

279.title: Winter – Conquest of Wu by Jin
279.body: Winter – Conquest of Wu by Jin: The Jin Dynasty conquers Eastern Wu, the last of the three contending powers in China during the Three Kingdoms Period.

280.title: Bahram
280.body: King Bahram II of the Sassanid Empire (Persia) sends envoys to seek peaceful relations with Rome.

280.title: Julius Saturninus
280.body: Julius Saturninus, governor of Syria, is in Alexandria, charged with the defense of the East. He is declared emperor and withdraws to Apamea. Probus besieges the city and puts him to death.

280.title: Roman Empire
280.body: Roman territory is under constant threat of raids from Franks. The cities in Gaul are reinforced with defensive walls.

280.title: Germanic people
280.body: The Germans destroy the Roman fleet on the Rhine; Bonosus is proclaimed emperor at Colonia Agrippina (Cologne).

280.title: Hanging
280.body: Probus defeats the army under Bonosus. Bonosus sees no way out and hangs himself. His family is treated with honours.

281.title: Marcus Aurelius Probus
281.body: Emperor Probus returns to Rome, where he celebrates his triumph over the Vandals and the usurpers (Bonosus, Julius Saturninus and Proculus).

281.title: King Zhao of Zhou
281.body: The tombs of King Zhao of Zhou and King Mu of Zhou were "rifled".

281.title: AD 281
281.body: Tiberianus served his first of two terms as Consul.

282.title: Marcus Aurelius Probus
282.body: Emperor Probus travels towards Sirmium (Serbia). He tries to employ his troops in peaceful projects, such as draining the swamps in Pannonia.

282.title: Germanicus Maximus
282.body: Carus defeats the Quadi and Sarmatians on the Danube; for his victories he is given the title Germanicus Maximus.

282.title: Fuzhou
282.body: A new city is constructed in Fuzhou, slightly south of the original city Ye (the main street of the city has remained unchanged since that time).

282.title: Carus
282.body: The praetorian prefect Marcus Aurelius Carus usurps power in Raetia. Probus attempts to organise a campaign against Carus but is murdered by his discontented troops in Sirmium.

282.title: Caesar
282.body: Carus appoints his sons Carinus and Numerian as Caesar.

283.title: Pope Caius
283.body: Pope Caius succeeds Eutychian as the 28th pope of Rome.

283.title: Diocletian
283.body: The officer Diocles, the future Emperor Diocletian, distinguishes himself in the war against the Persians.

283.title: Persicus Maximus
283.body: Exploiting the Persian civil war, Carus leaves Carinus in charge of much of the Roman Empire and, accompanied by his younger son Numerian, invades the Sassanid Empire. They sack Seleucia and Ctesiphon, the capital of the Persian kingdom, and they press on beyond the Tigris. For his victories, Carus receives the title of Persicus Maximus.

283.title: Augustus
283.body: Spring: Emperor Carus makes his son Carinus the Augustus in the west.

283.title: Numerian
283.body: Carinus and Numerian succeed their father Carus. Numerian, who had accompanied his father into the Persian Empire, leads the army back to Roman territory.

284.title: Numerian
284.body: Emperor Numerian travels through Bithynia (Asia Minor) on his way home to Rome. Suffering from an inflammation of the eyes, he travels in a closed litter in which soldiers find his decaying corpse.

284.title: Domestici
284.body: The commander of Numerian's domestici (household troops), Diocles, is chosen to be the new emperor. In a military assembly outside Nicomedia (modern İzmit, Turkey), Diocles claims that the praetorian prefect (and rival for the throne) Arrius Aper murdered Numerian, and he personally stabs and kills the prefect on the spot. The new emperor changes his name to the Latinised 'Diocletian'.

284.title: Rufinus of Byzantium
284.body: Rufinus succeeds Dometius as Bishop of Byzantium.

284.title: Yurye of Silla
284.body: Yurye becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Silla.

285.title: Gaul
285.body: Maximian is sent to pacify Gaul, where the Bagaudae, a band of peasants, are revolting against the Roman Empire.

285.title: Summer – Battle of the Margus
285.body: Summer – Battle of the Margus: Emperor Diocletian defeats the forces of Carinus in the valley of the Margus (Serbia). Numerous soldiers desert Carinus during the battle. Carinus then flees to the Pannonian fort of Cornacum, but he is soon slain by his officers.

285.title: Carausius
285.body: Carausius, naval commander at Bononia (modern-day Boulogne), is given the task of clearing the English Channel of Frankish and Saxon pirates.

285.title: Carinus
285.body: Spring – Emperor Carinus marches from Britain to northern Italy, and defeats the army of usurper Sabinus Julianus at Verona.

285.title: Maximian
285.body: July 21 or July 25 – Diocletian appoints his fellow-officer Maximian to the office of caesar, or junior co-emperor.

286.title: Diocletian
286.body: Emperor Diocletian campaigns successfully against Sarmatian raids. The future emperor Constantius defeats the 'Bosporian Sarmatians'.

286.title: Classis Britannica
286.body: Summer: Carausius, commander of the Classis Britannica, is accused of piracy by Maximian and is sentenced to death. He responds by declaring himself emperor of Britain and Northwestern Gaul. His forces consist of the newly built Roman fleet and three legions in Britain.

286.title: Augustus
286.body: Diocletian rewards Maximian by elevating him to co-emperor, giving him the title Augustus.

286.title: Caesar
286.body: Winter/Spring: The Caesar Maximian defeats the Bagaudae rebellion in Gaul. He then defeats a Germanic invasion into Gaul, defeating an army of Burgundians and Alemanni and another army of Chaibones and Heruli.

286.title: Chaekgye of Baekje
286.body: Chaekgye becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Baekje.

287.title: Bahram
287.body: Diocletian signs a peace treaty with King Bahram II of Persia, and installs the pro-Roman Arsacid Tiridates III as king over the western portion of Armenia.

287.title: Mesopotamia
287.body: Diocletian re-organizes the Mesopotamian frontier, and fortifies various locations including the city of Circesium (modern Busayrah) on the Euphrates. Around this time, he begins the construction of the Strata Diocletiana. Throughout his reign, similar fortification efforts are conducted on the other frontiers as well, with fortifications constructed or restored behind, on and beyond the borders.

287.title: Roman consul
287.body: On the same day that he is made consul, Maximian launches a campaign against an invasion of Gaul by the Alemanni. After defeating this invasion, he then invades Alemannia itself, entering across the Upper Rhine and returning to Roman territory via the Upper Danube.

287.title: Constantius
287.body: Around this time, the future emperor Constantius defeats and captures a Germanic king, the latter having prepared an ambush against the Romans.

288.title: Diocletian
288.body: Emperor Diocletian launches a campaign into Germanic territory from the province of Raetia (Switzerland).

288.title: Maximian
288.body: Around this time, an army loyal to Maximian, probably led by the future emperor Constantius, defeats the usurper Carausius or his Frankish allies in northern Gaul. In this or the following year, Carausius withdraws his military forces and administrative presence from Gaul, confining himself to Roman Britain.

288.title: North Sea
288.body: Far from Carausius' fleet, in the rivers of Gaul, Maximian builds a fleet to contest control of the North Sea and re-take Britain.

288.title: Flavia Maximiana Theodora
288.body: Around this time, Constantius marries Maximian's stepdaughter, Theodora, and it may also be around this time that the general Galerius marries Diocletian's daughter Galeria Valeria.

288.title: AD 288
288.body: Maximian makes an alliance with the Frankish king Gennobaudes.

289.title: Diocletian
289.body: In this or the following year, Emperor Diocletian campaigns with success against the Sarmatians. The future emperor Galerius may have distinguished himself during this campaign.

289.title: Maximian
289.body: In this or the following year, Maximian attempts to reconquer Britain from the usurper Carausius but is defeated at sea.

290.title: Diocletian
290.body: Emperor Diocletian campaigns with success against Arabic enemies.

290.title: Emperor Wu of Jin
290.body: Emperor Wu of Jin, founder of the Western Jin Dynasty, dies after a 25-year reign. He reunified north and south, but gave away many dukedoms to his kinsmen. Crown Prince Sima Zhong succeeds his father, and has to deal with conflicts among the aristocratic families in China.

290.title: Maximian
290.body: Following his victory over Emperor Maximian's fleet, the usurper Carausius invades the European mainland and re-establishes his military and administrative presence in northern Gaul.

291.title: Winter
291.body: Winter: The emperors Diocletian and Maximian convene in Milan.

291.title: Goths
291.body: A force of Goths defeat the Burgundians.

291.title: Alemanni
291.body: The Alemanni, having been expelled from part of their territory by the Burgundians, seek to regain their lost lands. These peoples had unsuccessfully invaded Gaul in tandem in 285/6, and the Alemanni had likely been weakened by the Roman counter-invasions of 287 and 288.

291.title: Blemmyes
291.body: The Blemmyes invade the Kingdom of Kush.

291.title: Tervingi
291.body: The Tervingian Goths and Taifali fight the Vandals and Gepids.

292.title: Upper Egypt
292.body: The Upper Egypt revolts of (292–293) outbreak in Egypt, centralized in the city of Thebes, primarily involving other Egyptian cities such as Busiris and Coptos. A precursor to the much larger national Egyptian rebellion that followed a few years later.

292.title: Maya civilization
292.body: The oldest known Mayan stele is erected at the capital Tikal (modern Guatemala).

292.title: Diocletian
292.body: The jurist Gregorius, at the court of Emperor Diocletian, produces the Gregorian Code, the first codification of Roman law (approximate date).

292.title: Bongsang of Goguryeo
292.body: Bongsang becomes ruler of the Korean kingdom of Goguryeo.

293.title: Franks
293.body: In this or the following year, Constantius defeats the Franks in Batavia (Netherlands).

293.title: Quattuor Principes Mundi
293.body: Emperors Diocletian and Maximian appoint Constantius I and Galerius as Caesars. This is considered the beginning of the Tetrarchy, known as the Quattuor Principes Mundi ("Four Rulers of the World"). (Some sources and scholars date Galerius' elevation to May 21.)

293.title: Gaul
293.body: Constantius retakes some of the Gallic territories from the usurper Carausius. He conquers the crucial port of Bononia (modern Boulogne).

293.title: Coptos
293.body: Galerius begins a series of two campaigns in Upper Egypt against the rebel cities of Coptos and Boresis as well as the Blemmyes and Meroitic Nubians.

293.title: Allectus
293.body: Towards the end of the year, Carausius is murdered by his finance minister Allectus, who proclaims himself the new emperor of Britain.

294.title: Tuoba Luguan
294.body: Tuoba Luguan succeeds his nephew Tuoba Fu, as chieftain of the Chinese Tuoba clan.

295.title: Petra
295.body: Petra rejoins the province of Palestine, and is converted to Christianity by the Syrian monk Barsauma.

295.title: Galerius
295.body: Caesar Galerius completes a series of two campaigns in Upper Egypt, against the rebel cities of Coptos and Boresis, as well as the Blemmyes and Meroitic Nubians.

295.title: Tuoba Luguan
295.body: Tuoba Luguan divides the territory of the Tuoba clan into three areas. His nephews Tuoba Yilu and Tuoba Yituo become chieftains of the western and central areas of (Shanxi province). Tuoba Luguan dominates the eastern area (near Hohhot).

295.title: Hermogenian Code
295.body: The jurist Hermogenianus, at the court of Diocletian, produces the Hermogenian Code. This new codification of Roman law complements the Gregorian Code of c. 292.

296.title: Constantius
296.body: In this or the previous year, Caesar Constantius I assembles two invasion fleets with the intent of overthrowing the usurper Allectus, who is based in Britain. The first is under the command of Asclepiodotus, Maximian's long-serving Praetorian Prefect. Asclepiodotus sails from the mouth of the Seine, and lands near the Isle of Wight, where his forces defeat Allectus in Hampshire.

296.title: Narseh
296.body: The Persian king Narseh invades Roman-held Upper Mesopotamia and Arsacid western Armenia, the latter territory being under the leadership of the pro-Roman king Tiridates III. With only a small army, Caesar Galerius fights three holding actions against Narseh's army in Mesopotamia. Somewhere in the open plains between Carrhae and Callinicum, Galerius' army suffers a defeat against the Persian army, which is both more numerous and contains superior numbers of high-quality cavalry.

296.title: Pope Caius
296.body: Pope Caius dies after a 13-year reign and is succeeded by Marcellinus as the 29th pope of Rome.

296.title: Franks
296.body: Having supervised the Rhine frontier during Constantius' invasion of Britain, Maximian then marches into Spain, where he fights Frankish pirates. He then crosses into North Africa to contend with the rebellion of the Quinquegentiani.

297.title: Galerius
297.body: Diocletian watches over the Syrian provinces while Caesar Galerius makes preparations for a campaign against the Persian king Narseh. He recruits veterans from Illyria and Moesia, recruits new soldiers, and strengthens his army with Gothic mercenaries and the Armenian units of Tiridates III.

297.title: Battle of Satala
297.body: Battle of Satala: Galerius launches a surprise attack against Narseh's camp in western Armenia. The Romans sack the camp and capture Narseh's wives, sisters and daughters, including his Queen of Queens Arsane. Narseh is wounded and escapes to his empire.

297.title: Diocletian
297.body: Emperor Diocletian introduces a new tax system and other economic reforms.

297.title: August
297.body: August: Domitius Domitianus launches a usurpation against Diocletian in Egypt. He is perhaps aided by popular discontent with Diocletian's taxation reform.

297.title: Autumn
297.body: Autumn: Diocletian besieges the rebels in Alexandria, Egypt.

298.title: Galerius
298.body: Caesar Galerius restores Tiridates III to the throne of Armenia and invades the Sassanid Empire. His army marches through Adiabene, Atropatene, Susiana and Lower Mesopotamia. He then retakes the strategically important city of Nisibis in Upper Mesopotamia.

298.title: Silk
298.body: The manufacture of cultured silk becomes popular from Korea to Japan.

298.title: Spring
298.body: Spring: Emperor Diocletian retakes Alexandria and crushes the usurpation of Aurelius Achilleus.

298.title: Kingdom of Kush
298.body: Diocletian then travels into Upper Egypt and possibly campaigns on the Nubian frontier. In either this year or in 300/301, he makes agreements with the Meroitic Nubians and the Blemmyes. He agrees to pay subsidies to both peoples, and he cedes the Dodecashoenos to the Nubians on the understanding that the Nubians will defend the region against the Blemmyes.

298.title: Girim of Silla
298.body: Girim becomes the king of the Korean kingdom of Silla.

299.title: Peace of Nisibis
299.body: Peace of Nisibis: Emperor Diocletian signs a treaty with the Persian king Narseh that will last for 40 years. The Persians accept Roman dominion over Armenia, the Caucasus, and Upper Mesopotamia. The pro-Roman ruler Tiridates III receives all of Armenia as far as the border with Atropatene.

299.title: Arch of Galerius and Rotunda
299.body: To celebrate his victory over the Persians, Galerius commissions the Arch of Galerius in Thessaloniki (modern Greece).

299.title: Maximian
299.body: Having first crossed into Africa in 296, Emperor Maximian concludes his campaigns against the Quinquegentiani and other Berbers. His campaigns had ranged as far as Mauretania in the west and Tripolitania in the east. Julianus, a rebel leader in Africa, throws himself into a fire after the Romans breach the walls of his stronghold.

299.title: Christians
299.body: Diocletian expels Christians from the Roman army.

299.title: Sarmatians
299.body: In this or the following year, Galerius campaigns with success against Sarmatians and the Marcomanni, attacking through a swamp to defeat a Sarmatian army.

300.title: Mayan civilization
300.body: The Mayan civilization reaches its most prolific period, the classic period, in what is now Guatemala, Belize and parts of southern Mexico adjacent to the former two. During most of this period, Tikal dominates the Mayan world.

300.title: Diocletian's Palace
300.body: Diocletian's Palace, Split, Croatia, is built. Its model is nowadays kept at the Museo della Civilta Romana, Rome.

300.title: Synod of Elvira
300.body: Approximate date of the Synod of Elvira in Elvira, Spain, which prohibits interaction with Jews, pagans, and heretics.

300.title: St Mark's Basilica
300.body: The Tetrarchs are probably made in Egypt. After 330 they are moved to Constantinople and in 1204 they are installed at the corner of the facade of the St Mark's Basilica, Venice (approximate date).

300.title: Kama Sutra
300.body: The Kama Sutra, an Indian handbook on the art of sexual love, is probably produced around this time by the sage Vatsyayana.

301.title: the Great
301.body: King Tiridates III (the Great) proclaims Christianity as the official state religion, making Armenia the first nation to adopt Christianity as its official religion (traditional date). Construction of the original Etchmiadzin Cathedral by Gregory the Illuminator begins.

301.title: San Marino
301.body: The republic of San Marino is established (approximate date).

301.title: September
301.body: September: Emperor Diocletian issues a reform that revalues the Roman currency.

301.title: November
301.body: November: Diocletian issues his Edict on Maximum Prices, which, rather than halting rampant inflation, causes widespread panic and an increase in inflation. The measure is quickly abandoned.

301.title: Galerius
301.body: Caesar Galerius begins a major war against the Carpi and Bastarnae and wins the first of several victories.

302.title: Narseh
302.body: Narseh, ruler (Shahanshah) of the Sassanid Empire, dies after a 9-year reign. He is succeeded by his son Hormizd II.

302.title: Diocletian
302.body: Emperor Diocletian persecutes the Manichaeans, accusing them of being a Persian fifth column.

302.title: Gaul
302.body: An invasion of Gaul by the Alemannic Lingones almost traps Caesar Constantius I between the enemy and the walls of a town. Constantius himself is carried onto the wall via a crane. However, within the same day, Constantius sallies forth from the walls and defeats the enemy in a major battle.

302.title: Iamblichus of Chalcis
302.body: Iamblichus of Chalcis writes a treatise on magic and the occult.

302.title: Galerius
302.body: Caesar Galerius wins his second victory over the Carpi.

303.title: Mexico
303.body: In Mexico, the civilization of Teotihuacan flourishes.

303.title: Great Persecution
303.body: Great Persecution: Emperor Diocletian launches the last and largest major persecution of Christians in the Empire. Caesar Galerius and Hierocles are said to have been the instigators. In a series of four edicts published from February 23, 303, to 304, the Christians are forbidden to worship in groups, are made to perform sacrifices, and must surrender sacred texts.

303.title: Augustus
303.body: The Augusti Diocletian and Maximian reunite in Rome to celebrate the 20th anniversary of Diocletian's accession, which is now treated as a joint anniversary for both emperors. A series of columns in the Roman Forum and a triumphal arch are dedicated to the emperors. The two emperors also agree on a plan of abdication.

303.title: Carpi
303.body: Galerius wins his third victory over the Carpi and is perhaps joined on campaign by Diocletian. The Arch of Galerius is dedicated in Thessaloniki.

303.title: Gospel
303.body: On a voyage preaching the gospel, Saint Fermin of Pamplona is beheaded in Amiens (Modern day France).

304.title: Danube
304.body: Diocletian, while inspecting the Danube border, becomes seriously ill.

304.title: Galerius
304.body: Caesar Galerius, perhaps accompanied by Emperor Diocletian, wins his fourth and final victory over the Carpi. Many of the surviving Carpi and Bastarnae are resettled in the Roman Empire, where they are split up. The Bastarnae are not attested after this time, and the Carpi are attested only once more in the 310s.

304.title: Sixteen Kingdoms
304.body: Sixteen Kingdoms: The Xiongnu establishes the Han-Zhao state under Liu Yuan, often seen as the start of the Upheaval of the Five Barbarians.

304.title: Cheng-Han
304.body: Cheng-Han earns its independence from Jin dynasty.

304.title: Biryu of Baekje
304.body: Biryu becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Baekje.

305.title: curiales
305.body: Landowners dominate the Roman Empire, and enjoy the title of senator, which exempts them from the crushing taxes imposed on the rest of the population. The Senate has lost all its power and the landowners almost never attend Senate sessions. Members of municipal senates (curiales or decuriones) are charged with the responsibility of collecting taxes and paying arrears; smaller landowners are held responsible for providing recruits for the Roman army, and with keeping wastelands under cultivation.

305.title: Council of Illiberis
305.body: The Council of Illiberis decrees that priests must be celibate. Additionally, it condemns visiting the homes of Jews and prohibits Christian women from marrying Jews, unless they have converted.

305.title: Constantine the Great
305.body: Constantius requests leave for his son Constantine I to join him in the west, who has been living at the courts of Diocletian and Galerius as a hostage. Galerius allows Constantine to return.

305.title: Diocletian
305.body: Emperor Diocletian abdicates and retires to his palace at Salona (modern Split) on the Adriatic coast, after a reign of nearly 21 years, in which the last vestiges of republican government have disappeared.

305.title: Patron saint
305.body: Patron of Pozzuoli, Saint Proculus, and patron of Naples, Saint Januarius are thrown to wild beasts in Pozzuoli's Flavian Amphitheater, then beheaded at Solfatara.

306.title: Maxentius
306.body: Maxentius, son of former Western Emperor Maximian, leads a revolt by the Praetorian Guard and members of the Senate in Rome, and is proclaimed Emperor. Southern Italy supports Maxentius, as do Africa, Corsica, Sardinia and Sicily. Maxentius recalls Maximian from retirement, who joins his son in Rome.

306.title: Roman Britain
306.body: Christianity is established in Roman Britain. British bishops participate in the councils of Arles (314), Nicaea (325) and Arminum (349).

306.title: Arch of Galerius and Rotunda
306.body: Galerius has the Rotunda of Galerius built in Thessaloniki (Macedonia).

306.title: Diocletian
306.body: Building on the efforts of Diocletian, Galerius introduces the poll tax to central and southern Italy and truncates the size of the Praetorian Guard, with plans to disband the Guard altogether.

306.title: Constantius
306.body: Constantius I dies outside Eboracum (modern-day York). Constantine, aged 23 or 24, is declared emperor by his troops. Emperor Galerius grants Constantine the title of Caesar, and elevates Flavius Valerius Severus to emperor in the Western part of the Roman Empire.

307.title: Late summer or autumn
307.body: Late summer or autumn: Galerius invades Italy but Maxentius remains behind the walls of Rome. Galerius finds he cannot besiege the city, and the image of an emperor making efforts against Rome hurts Galerius' image among the troops. The fact that Maxentius is his son-in-law does not help, and Maxentius makes an effort to bribe Galerius' troops.

307.title: Diocletian
307.body: Near the end of the year, Galerius gives his wife (Diocletian's daughter) Galeria Valeria the title of Augusta.

307.title: Summer
307.body: Summer: Anticipating an offensive by Galerius, Maximian travels to Gaul to make an alliance with Constantine I.

307.title: Emperor Hui of Jin
307.body: Emperor Hui of Jin dies after a 16-year reign, in which eight dukes of the imperial family have conducted a civil war (War of the Eight Princes) against each other in a struggle for power. Huai of Jin, age 23, succeeds his father and becomes the third ruler of the Jin Dynasty.

307.title: Valerius Severus
307.body: Galerius sends Valerius Severus with the army of northern Italy, to suppress the rebellion in Rome. However, faced with their former emperor Maximian, the soldiers desert him, and Severus flees to Ravenna. Maximian besieges Severus in Ravenna, who then surrenders.

308.title: The Conference of Carnuntum
308.body: The Conference of Carnuntum: Attempting to keep peace within the Roman Empire, Galerius recalls Diocletian briefly from retirement, and they convene with Maximian. Diocletian persuades Maximian to return to retirement, and he and Galerius declare Maxentius a public enemy. Licinius is proclaimed Augustus of the west, while rival contender Constantine I is again declared Caesar.

308.title: Pope Marcellus
308.body: May 27 or June 26 – Pope Marcellus I succeeds Pope Marcellinus as the 30th pope.

308.title: April
308.body: April: In Rome, Emperor Maximian attempts to depose his son Maxentius, but the soldiers in Rome side with Maxentius and force Maximian to flee to the court of Constantine I.

308.title: Rhine
308.body: Constantine raids the territory of the Bructeri and builds a bridge across the Rhine at Cologne.

308.title: Christians
308.body: Maxentius institutes toleration of the Christians in his territories.

309.title: Hormizd
309.body: King Hormizd II, ruler of the Sassanid Empire, demands that the king of the Ghassanids pays tribute. After the king refuses, Hormizd invades Ghassanid territory. The Ghassanids seek aid from Maximinus Daza, but before a Roman army can arrive, Hormizd defeats the Ghassanid army and kills their king.

309.title: Pope Eusebius
309.body: Eusebius succeeds Marcellus I as the 31st pope, but is himself banished on August 17 to Sicily (these events may have also taken place in 310).

309.title: Pope Marcellus
309.body: Pope Marcellus I is banished from Rome by Emperor Maxentius.

309.title: Gaius Ceionius Rufius Volusianus
309.body: Gaius Ceionius Rufius Volusianus, the Praetorian Prefect of Emperor Maxentius, defeats the usurper Domitius Alexander and purges Africa of his supporters.

309.title: Shapur
309.body: Hormizd is succeeded by his infant son Shapur II following the brief reign and murder of Adur Narseh.

310.title: caesar
310.body: Maximinus Daza, caesar under Emperor Galerius, is acclaimed augustus by his troops. Galerius is forced to recognize him as co-ruler of the East. The Roman Empire is thus divided between seven simultaneous emperors: Galerius (East), Maximinus II (East), Licinius (Middle), Constantine I (West), Maximian (West), Maxentius (Italy), and Domitius Alexander (Africa).

310.title: solidus
310.body: At Trier, Constantine orders the minting of a new coin, the solidus, in an effort to offset the declining value of the denarius and bring stability to the imperial currency by restoring a gold standard. The solidus (later known as the bezant) will be minted in the Byzantine Empire without change in weight or purity until the 10th century.

310.title: Pope Eusebius
310.body: Pope Eusebius succeeds Pope Marcellus I as the 31st pope, but is banished on August 17 by the Emperor Maxentius to Sicily, where he dies, perhaps from a hunger strike.

310.title: Marseille
310.body: July – Maximian flees to Marseille where he is besieged and surrenders. Constantine encourages his suicide and Maximian, age 60, hangs himself. Emperor Maxentius condemns the killing of his father.

310.title: Maximian
310.body: Maximian, retired emperor, rebels against Constantine I in Arles while the latter is campaigning against the Franks.

311.title: Galerius
311.body: On his deathbed, Emperor Galerius declares religious freedom and issues the Edict of Serdica, ending the Diocletianic Persecution of Christians in the eastern half of the Roman Empire.

311.title: Colorectal cancer
311.body: Galerius dies at age 51 from a gruesome disease, possibly colorectal cancer or Fournier gangrene.

311.title: Pope Miltiades
311.body: Pope Miltiades succeeds Eusebius as the 32nd pope of Rome.

311.title: Christians
311.body: Maximinus recommences the persecution of Christians, having encouraged his subjects to petition him to do so.

311.title: Disaster of Yongjia
311.body: Disaster of Yongjia: Xiongnu soldiers led by Liu Cong conquer and pillage the Jin dynasty capital at Luoyang and capture Emperor Huai; 30,000 residents in the city are massacred.

312.title: labarum
312.body: Battle of the Milvian Bridge: Constantine defeats Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge, becoming the only Roman emperor in the West. Prior to the battle, he reportedly has a vision of a cross (labarum) with the phrase "in hoc signo vinces" ("In this sign you shall conquer"). This encourages him to convert to Christianity.

312.title: adventus
312.body: Constantine enters Rome; he stages a grand adventus in the city, and is met with popular jubilation. Maxentius' body is fished out of the Tiber and decapitated.

312.title: equites singulares Augusti
312.body: Constantine forges an alliance with co-emperor Licinius, and offers him his half-sister, Constantia, in marriage. The Praetorian Guard and Imperial Horse Guard (equites singulares Augusti) are disbanded.

312.title: "in hoc signo vinces"
312.body: Constantine I adopts the words "in hoc signo vinces" as a motto, and has the letters X and P (the first letters of the Greek word Christ) emblazoned on the shields of his soldiers.

312.title: Maximinus Daza
312.body: Emperor Maximinus Daza campaigns unsuccessfully against the Armenians.

313.title: Arius
313.body: Arius preaches of the human nature of Jesus.

313.title: Edict of Milan
313.body: Edict of Milan: Constantine the Great and co-emperor Licinius meet at a conference in Mediolanum (modern Milan). They proclaim a policy of religious freedom for all, ending the persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire and returning property confiscated from Christians. The edict is posted in Nicomedia on June 13.

313.title: Basilica Nova
313.body: Basilica of Maxentius and Constantine (or Basilica Nova), in Rome, is finished.

313.title: February
313.body: February: Emperors Constantine I and Licinius convene in Mediolanum (modern Milan). Licinius marries Constantine's half-sister Constantia, and they issue the Edict of Milan. This edict ends the Great Persecution against the Christians and is the first piece of legislation in western history to decree freedom of religion.

313.title: Emperor Huai of Jin
313.body: Emperor Huai of Jin is executed by Liu Cong, ruler of the Xiongnu state (Han-Zhao). At the imperial new year he and a number of former Jin officials are poisoned. Crown prince Min of Jin, age 13, succeeds, in Chang'an, his uncle Huai of Jin and becomes the new emperor of the Jin Dynasty.

314.title: Pope Sylvester
314.body: Pope Sylvester I succeeds Pope Miltiades as the 33rd pope.

314.title: Picts
314.body: A large Pictish raid southwards in Roman Britain is attempted.

314.title: Synod of Ancyra
314.body: Synod of Ancyra: Consulting a magician is declared a sin earning five years of penance.

314.title: Council of Arles
314.body: Council of Arles: Confirms the pronouncement of Donatism as a schism, and passes other canons.

315.title: Lamb of God
315.body: The lamb becomes the symbol of Jesus in Christian art.

315.title: Crucifixion
315.body: Crucifixion is abolished as punishment in the Roman Empire.

315.title: Arch of Constantine
315.body: The Arch of Constantine is completed near the Colosseum at Rome to commemorate Constantine's victory over Maxentius at the Milvian Bridge. As part of the ceremony Constantine is expected to make a sacrifice to Rome's traditional gods, but he refuses to do so.

315.title: Constantine the Great
315.body: Constantine the Great and co-emperor Licinius battle the Sarmates, the Goths and the Carpians along the Danube. Constantine leads a punitive expedition into Dacia and reestablishes the Roman fortifications of the frontier.

315.title: Augusta Treverorum
315.body: Immense baths are constructed in Augusta Treverorum (modern-day Trier).

316.title: Colonia Aurelia Cibalae
316.body: Battle of Cibalae: Constantine the Great defeats his rival Licinius near the town of Colonia Aurelia Cibalae (modern-day Vinkovci, Croatia). Licinius is forced to flee to Sirmium and loses all of the Balkans except for Thrace. Peace negotiations are initiated between the two Augusti, but they are unsuccessful.

316.title: December – Battle of Mardia
316.body: December – Battle of Mardia: Constantine I defeats his rival Licinius and senior officer Valerius Valens, near the town of Harmanli (Bulgaria).

316.title: Constantine the Great
316.body: Emperor Constantine the Great issues an edict, prohibiting the punishment of slaves by crucifixion and facial branding.

316.title: Han-Zhao
316.body: Han-Zhao forces capture Chang'an, capital of the Chinese Western Jin dynasty. Emperor Min of Jin surrenders to Liu Yao, ending the Western Jin.

316.title: Caesar
316.body: Constantine I sends his half-brother Julius Constantius to Licinius at Sirmium (Pannonia), with the proposal to accept Bassianus as Caesar and give him power over Italy. Licinius refuses, and forces a conspiracy against Constantine.

317.title: Caesars
317.body: Emperor Constantine the Great and co-emperor Licinius elevate their sons Crispus, Constantine II and Licinius II to Caesars. After this arrangement Constantine rules the dioceses Pannonia and Macedonia, and establishes his residence at Sirmium, from where he prepares a campaign against the Goths and Sarmatians.

317.title: Yuan of Jin
317.body: Sixteen Kingdoms: Jingwen (later Yuan of Jin) flees with remnants of the Jin court and noble families to the south. He succeeds Emperor Min of Jin as first ruler of the Eastern Jin Dynasty and decides to make Jiankang (modern Nanjing) his new capital.

317.title: History of tea
317.body: The earliest historically verified reference to tea is recorded, although the Chinese have been drinking the beverage for centuries.

317.title: Valerius Valens
317.body: Licinius recognizes Constantine I as senior emperor and executes Valerius Valens.

318.title: Gregory the Illuminator
318.body: Gregory the Illuminator appoints his son Aristax as successor in the patriarchate of Armenia.

318.title: Constantine the Great
318.body: Emperor Constantine the Great gives the ancient Roman town Drepana (Asia Minor) the name Helenopolis, after his mother Helena, and builds a church in honour of the martyr St. Lucian.

318.title: Jin dynasty
318.body: The Eastern Jin dynasty loses its territories to the north of the Yangtze River, to the benefit of the Xiongnu and the Xianbei. The Former Zhao state is proclaimed; Liu Can and the state ruling family at Pingyang is executed in a coup d'état by Jin Zhun, who is in turn overthrown by Shi Le and Liu Yao.

318.title: Brittanicus Maximus
318.body: Constantine the Great is given the title Brittanicus Maximus, for successful engagements in Britain.

318.title: Emperor
318.body: Liu Yao becomes the new emperor of Han-Zhao and moves his capital to Chang'an.

319.title: Christianity
319.body: Christianity is introduced in Colchis, present-day Georgia.

319.title: Constantine the Great
319.body: Emperor Constantine the Great prohibits the separation of the families of slaves, during a change in ownership.

319.title: Arius
319.body: Arius travels to Nicomedia at the invitation of Bishop Eusebius, after having been accused of heresy and condemned by Alexander, the Patriarch of Alexandria. This gives rise to the Arian Controversy.

319.title: Chandragupta
319.body: King Chandragupta I succeeds his father Ghatotkacha, as ruler of the Gupta dynasty.

320.title: The Great Astronomer
320.body: Pappus of Alexandria, Greek philosopher, observes an eclipse of the sun and writes a commentary on The Great Astronomer (Almagest).

320.title: Licinius
320.body: Licinius reneges on the religious freedom promised by the Edict of Milan, and begins a new persecution of Christians in the Eastern Roman Empire. He imprisons Christians, confiscates their properties and destroys churches.

320.title: Nydam Mose
320.body: In Nydam Mose (Denmark), the Nydam oak boat is sacrificed by the Danes (the boat is excavated in the 1830s, when a local farmer finds weapons, including swords and spears).

320.title: Old St. Peter's Basilica
320.body: Construction begins on the Old St. Peter's Basilica, Rome (approximate date).

320.title: Augusta Treverorum
320.body: Crispus, eldest son of Constantine I, leads a victorious campaign against the Franks, assuring twenty years of peace along the Rhine frontier. He establishes his residence in Augusta Treverorum (modern Trier), capital of Germania.

321.title: Constantine
321.body: Emperor Constantine I expels the Goths from the Danube frontier and repairs Trajan's Bridge. He leads an expedition into the old province Dacia (modern Romania) and makes peace with the barbarians.

321.title: Synod
321.body: A synod held in Alexandria condemns Arianism.

321.title: History of the Jews in Germany
321.body: History of the Jews in Germany: Jews in modern-day Germany are documented for the first time, in Colonia Agrippinensium (modern-day Cologne).

321.title: Tuoba Heru
321.body: Tuoba Heru launches a coup d'état against his cousin Tuoba Yulü and becomes the new Prince of Dai.

322.title: Stirrups
322.body: The first dependable representation of a horse rider with paired stirrups is found in China, in a Jin Dynasty tomb.

323.title: Banquet
323.body: The poetic work Banquet (Thalia) by the Libyan-born Egyptian Christian priest Arius, age 73, expresses the doctrine that Jesus of Nazareth was not of the same substance as God but rather had a finite nature. As an ascetic, he leads a Christian community near Alexandria, and comes under suspicion of heresy. Arius writes to his former schoolmate Eusebius, bishop of Nicomedia, asking for support.

323.title: Sarmaticus Maximus
323.body: Emperor Constantine the Great defeats the invading Goths and Sarmatians north of the Danube in Dacia, and claims the title of Sarmaticus Maximus.

323.title: Emperor Ming of Jin
323.body: Crown Prince Ming of Jin succeeds his father Yuan of Jin as emperor of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.

324.title: Byzantium
324.body: (Date unknown) The Roman Emperor Constantine I seizes the Byzantine Empire's capital, Byzantium, and commences work on rebuilding the city as the Eastern Empire's capital, which he will inaugurate as Constantinople in 330.

324.title: Battle of Adrianople
324.body: Battle of Adrianople: Emperor Constantine the Great defeats his rival Licinius near Adrianople, forcing him to retreat to Byzantium. Constantine then invades Thrace with a Visigothic force and raids the countryside.

324.title: Flavia Julia Constantia
324.body: Licinius abdicates his position as Emperor. He is pardoned by Constantine I as a result of the supplication of his wife Constantia (who is Constantine's halfsister), and banished to Thessalonica as a private citizen.

324.title: July – Battle of the Hellespont
324.body: July – Battle of the Hellespont: Flavius Julius Crispus, the designated heir of his father Constantine, destroys the naval fleet of Licinius in the Dardanelles, allowing Constantine to cross over the Bosphorus into Asian provinces. Byzantium is besieged and Licinius assembles a second military force, under his newly elevated co-emperor Martinian at Lampsacus (modern-day Lapseki).

324.title: guiyou
324.body: (guiyou day of the 7th month of the 2nd year of the Tai'ning era) In Jin dynasty China, the rebel warlord Wang Dun succumbs to illness as the armies of the Emperor Ming of Jin are approaching his camp.

325.title: First Council of Nicaea
325.body: First Council of Nicaea: Constantine summons an ecumenical council of bishops in Nicaea (Turkey). The Nicene Creed, adopted on June 19, declares that the members of the Trinity (the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit) are equal. The council decides that Easter is celebrated on the first Sunday after the first full moon after the vernal equinox.

325.title: Colossus of Constantine
325.body: The Colossus of Constantine in the Basilica of Maxentius and Constantine, Rome, is perhaps remodelled at about this date. (The remains are moved to the Palazzo dei Conservatori, Rome, in the 15th century).

325.title: German and Sarmatian campaigns of Constantine
325.body: German and Sarmatian campaigns of Constantine: Emperor Constantine I personally assures the security of the Danube frontier by defeating the Goths, the Vandals and the Sarmatians.

325.title: Licinius
325.body: Constantine has deposed Emperors Licinius and Martinian executed in Thessalonica and Cappadocia respectively for conspiring and raising troops against him.

325.title: Gladiator
325.body: Constantine forbids criminals being forced to fight to the death as gladiators.

326.title: Helena
326.body: September 14 (traditional date) – Helena, mother of Constantine I, discovers the so-called True Cross and the Holy Sepulchre (Jesus's tomb) in Jerusalem. On her pilgrimage, which begins about this year, she pauses on the Aegean island of Patmos, where she is said to found the church of Panagia Ekatontapiliani.

326.title: Atonement in Christianity
326.body: Helena tells Constantine that he must atone for executing his son and wife by building churches, and at about this date construction begins on Old St. Peter's Basilica, the first church on the traditional site of Saint Peter's tomb in Rome, and on the basilica of Golgotha on Calvary outside Jerusalem. He also commissions construction of the Church of the Nativity in Bethlehem (dedicated 339).

326.title: Christianity
326.body: Christianity is introduced to the Kingdom of Iberia (modern-day Georgia) by Saint Nino (approximate date).

326.title: Constantine the Great
326.body: Emperor Constantine the Great travels to Rome to celebrate the 20th anniversary of his accession to power, but while en route at Pola he orders his older son, Crispus Caesar, to be executed, possibly on charges of adultery. Later, Fausta, second wife of Constantine I, is also executed by being suffocated or boiled in a hot bath.

327.title: Helena
327.body: Approximate traditional date – Helena, mother of Constantine, returning from her pilgrimage to the Holy Land, founds Stavrovouni Monastery on Cyprus.

327.title: Constantine the Great
327.body: Emperor Constantine the Great decrees that rural slaves can only be sold in the province where they reside, in order to resolve the shortage of labour in the Roman Empire.

327.title: Domus Aurea
327.body: Construction begins on the Great Church of Antioch, which will be completed in 341.

328.title: Constantine's Bridge
328.body: Constantine's Bridge, built over the Danube between Sucidava (Corabia, Romania) and Oescus (Gigen, Bulgaria), is officially opened by the Roman architect Theophilus Patricius.

328.title: Lakhmids
328.body: Lakhmid king Imru' al-Qays ibn 'Amr dies. His epitaph, the Namara inscription, is an important source for the Arabic Language.

328.title: Athanasius of Alexandria
328.body: Athanasius is elected pope of Alexandria following the death of Alexander I.

329.title: Han Zhao
329.body: The Han-Zhao dynasty, a Southern Xiongnu state during the Sixteen Kingdoms, ends.

329.title: Clergy
329.body: Roman restrictions on joining the clergy are initiated.

330.title: Saint Frumentius
330.body: Frumentius is the first bishop of Ethiopia (approximate date).

330.title: Nova Roma
330.body: Emperor Constantine the Great dedicates the Column of Constantine and Constantinople, or Nova Roma (modern-day Istanbul).
