He had spent four years expanding the city of Byzantium, having chosen the site for its strategic location on the Bosporus. The city is later the capital of the Eastern Roman Empire.

330.title: Bible
330.body: The Bible is translated into the Gothic language by Wulfila.

330.title: Paganism
330.body: Pagan temples begin to be progressively abandoned, destroyed or left to fall into disrepair, save those that are transformed into churches.

330.title: Goths
330.body: The Goths devastate the city of Tanais, in the Don River Delta.

331.title: Constantine the Great
331.body: Emperor Constantine the Great vigorously promotes Christianity, confiscating the property and valuables of a number of pagan temples throughout the Roman Empire.

331.title: Gregory the Illuminator
331.body: Gregory the Illuminator withdraws to a small sanctuary in the Daranali province (Armenia).

331.title: Church of the Holy Apostles
331.body: Constantine I dedicates the Church of the Holy Apostles in Constantinople.

331.title: Fifty Bibles of Constantine
331.body: The Fifty Bibles of Constantine are commissioned for use in Constantinople.

331.title: Gogugwon of Goguryeo
331.body: Gogugwon becomes ruler of the Korean kingdom of Goguryeo.

332.title: Constantinople
332.body: Constantine I announces a free distribution of food to the citizens in Constantinople, similar to the food given out in the city of Rome. The amount is approximately 80,000 rations a day, doled out from 117 distribution points around the city.

332.title: Constantine the Great
332.body: Emperor Constantine I and his son Constantine II, aged 16, defeat the Goths in Moesia. The Goths become Roman allies and protect the Danube frontier.

332.title: Trajan
332.body: Constantine I continues construction of a bridge (in imitation of Trajan and his architect Apollodorus of Damascus) across the Danube, for forward-staging grounds for planned campaigns against local tribes.

333.title: Calocaerus
333.body: Calocaerus revolts against Constantine I and proclaims himself emperor. Flavius Dalmatius, responsible for the security of the eastern frontier, is sent to Cyprus to suppress the rebellion.

333.title: Caesar
333.body: Constantine I elevates his youngest son Constans to the rank of Caesar at Constantinople.

333.title: Constantine the Great
333.body: Emperor Constantine the Great pulls Roman troops out of Britain, and abandons work on Hadrian's Wall.

333.title: Shi Hong
333.body: Shi Hong succeeds his father Shi Le as Emperor of the Later Zhao Empire, in the Period of the Sixteen Kingdoms, but Shi Hong's third cousin Shi Hu holds the real power. Empress Dowager Liu (widow of Shi Le) fails to get rid of Shi Hu, and Shi Hu has her deposed and killed.

333.title: Flavius Dalmatius
333.body: Flavius Dalmatius and Domitius Zenofilus are appointed consuls.

334.title: Constantine the Great
334.body: Emperor Constantine the Great reauthorises gladiatorial combat.

334.title: Goths
334.body: The Goths protect the Danube frontier against an invasion by the Vandals.

334.title: Flavius Dalmatius
334.body: Flavius Dalmatius puts down a revolt in Cyprus, led by Calocaerus. Calocaerus is brought to Tarsus (Cilicia), and executed.

335.title: Arius
335.body: Constantine I reinstates the Alexandrian priest Arius (declared a heretic at the First Council of Nicaea in 325) in a synod at Jerusalem about a year before Arius' death.

335.title: Church of the Holy Sepulchre
335.body: The Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem is consecrated.

335.title: Constantine the Great
335.body: Emperor Constantine I consecrates the Church of the Holy Sepulchre in Jerusalem.

335.title: Pope Sylvester
335.body: Pope Sylvester I dies at Rome after a 21-year reign. He is succeeded by Mark as the 34th pope.

335.title: Athanasius of Alexandria
335.body: Athanasius is banished to Trier, on the charge that he prevented the grain fleet from sailing to Constantinople.

336.title: Constantine
336.body: The military successes of Emperor Constantine I result in most of Dacia being reconquered by the Roman Empire.

336.title: Pope Mark
336.body: Pope Mark succeeds Pope Sylvester I as the 34th pope of the Catholic Church.

336.title: Arius
336.body: Arius, Alexandrian priest, collapses in the street in Constantinople (approximate date).

336.title: Rome
336.body: Pope Mark dies at Rome, after an 11-month reign. No successor is immediately found.

336.title: Christmas
336.body: The first celebration of Christmas is recorded.

337.title: Nusaybin
337.body: Shapur II besieges the Roman fortress of Nisibis (Syria), but is repulsed by forces under Lucilianus.

337.title: Constantine the Great
337.body: Constantine the Great, first Christian Roman emperor of the Western Empire (312–324), and of the Roman Empire (324–337), dies in Achyron, near Nicomedia, at age 65, after he is baptized by Eusebius of Nicomedia.

337.title: Shapur
337.body: King Shapur II of Persia begins a war against the Roman Empire. He sends his troops across the Tigris to recover Armenia and Mesopotamia.

337.title: Paul I of Constantinople
337.body: Paul I becomes Patriarch of Constantinople.

337.title: Athanasius of Alexandria
337.body: Constantius II announces the restoration of Athanasius, as Patriarch of Alexandria.

338.title: Shapur
338.body: Shapur II, king of the Persian Empire, begins a widespread persecution of Christians. He orders forcible conversions to the state religion, Zoroastrianism, lest the Christians disrupt his realm while he is away fighting the Romans in Armenia and Mesopotamia.

338.title: Goths
338.body: The Romans, allied with the Goths, arrive in the north of the Roman Empire to protect the Danube frontier.

338.title: Roman Empire
338.body: Non-Christians are persecuted by the Roman Empire as pagans.

338.title: Constantius
338.body: Emperor Constantius II intervenes against the Persians in Armenia.

338.title: Santa Costanza
338.body: The Church of Santa Costanza, Rome, starts to be built (approximate date).

339.title: Eusebius of Nicomedia
339.body: Eusebius of Nicomedia is made bishop of Constantinople, while another Arian succeeds Athanasius as bishop of Alexandria, under the name Gregory.

339.title: Pope Julius
339.body: Pope Julius I gives refuge in Rome to the Alexandrian patriarch Athanasius, who is deposed and expelled during the First Synod of Tyre (see 335).

339.title: Constantius
339.body: Emperor Constantius II hastens to his territory in the East, where a revived Persia under King Shapur II is attacking Mesopotamia. For the next 11 years, the two powers engage in a war of border skirmishing, with no real victor.

340.title: Constantine
340.body: Constantine II, emperor of the western part of the Roman Empire (Britain, Gaul, the Rhine provinces and Iberia), crosses the Alps and attacks the army of his brother Constans I, emperor of the central part of the Roman Empire (Upper Danube, Italy and middle Africa). They clash at Aquileia in northern Italy. Constantine is killed in a skirmish by an ambush of Constans' troops.

340.title: Roman Empire
340.body: Constans is left sole ruler of the Western part of the Roman Empire, with his other brother, Constantius II, emperor of the Eastern portion.

340.title: Constantinople
340.body: Constantinople, capital of Emperor Constantius II, becomes the largest city in the world, taking the lead from Rome, capital of his brother Constans I.

340.title: Ulfilas
340.body: Wulfila evangelizes among the Goths for 7 years.

340.title: Acacius of Caesarea
340.body: Acacius succeeds Eusebius as bishop in the see of Caesarea.

341.title: Coptic Orthodox Church in Africa
341.body: Coptic Christianity is introduced into Ethiopia by the Syrian apostle Frumentius. He and his colleague Aedesius ware captured by Ethiopians a year or two ago, and have become civil servants at the Aksumite court of King Ezana. Ezana is impressed with Frumentius' teachings and converts to Christianity.

341.title: Constans
341.body: Emperor Constans I bans pagan sacrifices and magic rituals, under penalty of death.

341.title: Antioch
341.body: The Council of Encaenia is held in Antioch.

341.title: Franks
341.body: Constans I begins a successful campaign against the Franks.

341.title: Samudragupta
341.body: Samudragupta of the Gupta Empire, during a decade, extends his kingdom and his influence. A pillar found at Allahabad sings his praises.

342.title: Hagia Sophia
342.body: The original Hagia Sophia is dedicated in Constantinople.

342.title: Constans
342.body: Emperor Constans I campaigns in Britain against the Picts.

342.title: Paul I of Constantinople
342.body: Paul I, Patriarch of Constantinople, is deposed and replaced by Macedonius I.

342.title: Goguryeo
342.body: Goguryeo is invaded by Murong Huang of the Xianbei.

343.title: Pope Julius
343.body: Pope Julius I tries to unite the Western bishops against Arianism by convoking the Council of Serdica, which acknowledges the pope's supreme authority and grants him the right to judge cases involving the legal possession of episcopal sees, but only Western and Egyptian bishops attend, and Arianism remains strong.

343.title: Constans
343.body: The Western Roman Emperor Constans I is in Britain, possibly in a military campaign against the Picts and Scots (the last visit to Britain by a legitimate emperor).

343.title: Constantius
343.body: The Eastern Roman Emperor Constantius II campaigns in Adiabene, a vassal kingdom of Armenia (Persian Empire).

344.title: Admonitions of the Imperial Instructress to Court Ladies
344.body: The making of a detail of Admonitions of the Imperial Instructress to Court Ladies (attributed to Gu Kaizhi and being from the Six Dynasties period) begins (approximate year) and is completed in 406. It is now kept at the British Museum, London.

344.title: Battle of Singara
344.body: Battle of Singara: The Roman army under Constantius wins a close victory, at the strongly fortified city of Singara (Mesopotamia). His enemy, King Shapur II, is forced to lift the siege, and withdraw the Persian army.

344.title: Eustorgius
344.body: Bishop Eustorgius I brings relics of the Three Magi from Constantinople to Milan, according to a 12th century legend.

344.title: Emperor Mu of Jin
344.body: Jin Mudi, age 1, succeeds his father Jin Kangdi as emperor of China. His mother, Empress Dowager Chu, becomes the ruling authority at court, and serves as regent.

344.title: Constantius
344.body: The Eastern Roman Emperor Constantius II campaigns in eastern Mesopotamia, against the Sassanid Persians.

345.title: Knai Thomman
345.body: Merchant Knai Thomman and 400 followers visit the Malabar Coast in Kerala (India), and assist the church there.

345.title: Constans
345.body: Constans orders the Basilica di Santa Tecla to be constructed in Milan.

345.title: Kadamba Dynasty
345.body: The Kadamba Dynasty is founded by Mayurasharma.

346.title: Korea
346.body: In Korea, the Buyeo Kingdom is absorbed by Goguryeo.

346.title: Visigoths
346.body: The Visigoths are converted to Arianism by Wulfila.

346.title: Macedonius I of Constantinople
346.body: Macedonius I, Patriarch of Constantinople, is deposed again by Paul I.

346.title: Apology Against the Arians
346.body: Emperor Constans I uses his influence to secure the return of Athanasius. He is restored as Patriarch of Alexandria, and documents are compiled relating to his expulsion, under the title Apology Against the Arians.

346.title: Geunchogo of Baekje
346.body: Geunchogo becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Baekje.

347.title: Council of Philippopolis
347.body: The Council of Philippopolis is held as the result of Eastern bishops leaving the Council of Sardica. In Philippopolis (Bulgaria), they excommunicate Pope Julius, and as a result, the Arian controversy is perpetuated.

347.title: Council of Sardica
347.body: Council of Sardica: An attempt is made to resolve the Arian controversy, and ground rules for bishops are laid down.

347.title: Li Shi
347.body: Li Shi, ruler of Cheng Han, fails in his attempt to halt a Jin expedition under Huan Wen. He flees to the capital Chengdu and surrenders his forces. Emperor Jin Mudi spares his life and makes him a marquess.

348.title: Ulfilas
348.body: Wulfila escapes religious persecution by the Gothic chieftain Athanaric, obtains permission from Constantius II to migrate with his flock of converts to Moesia, and settles near Nicopolis ad Istrum (Bulgaria).

348.title: Samudragupta
348.body: In India, Samudragupta of the Gupta Empire defeats Rudrasena in battle.

348.title: Sassanid Empire
348.body: In Persia, women are enrolled in the army to perform auxiliary services.

349.title: Shi
349.body: Shi Hu, emperor of the Jie state Later Zhao since 334, dies. The state plunges into turmoil with his sons Shi Shi, Shi Zun, Shi Jian and Shi Zhi plotting against each other and holding the emperorship in rapid succession, before ethnic Han Ran Min establishes the short-lived Ran Wei dynasty in 350, bringing the Later Zhao dynasty to an end in 351 before it is itself conquered and divided by the Former Yan and Former Qin dynasties in 351.

349.title: Shi Shi
349.body: Shi Shi, youngest of Shi Hui's sons, reigns for 33 days before being deposed and executed at age 10 by Shi Zun. After a brief reign of 183 days, Emperor Shi Zun and his mother Empress Zheng Yingtao are executed; his brother Shi Jian succeeds him, only to be toppled after 103 days in early 350 by Shi Zhi,

350.title: Anbar
350.body: The city of Anbar (Iraq) is founded by king Shapur II.

350.title: Niš
350.body: Vetranio meets Constantius II at Naissus (Serbia) and joins forces with him. Vetranio is forced to abdicate his title, and Constantius allows him to live as a private citizen on a state pension.

350.title: Nepotianus
350.body: Nepotianus, Roman usurper, proclaims himself emperor and enters Rome with a group of gladiators.

350.title: Huns
350.body: About this time the Huns begin to invade the Sassanid Empire.

350.title: Pushyavarman
350.body: King Pushyavarman establishes the Varman Dynasty in Assam.

351.title: Aquileia
351.body: Winter – Magnentius flees to Aquileia in northern Italy and fortifies the mountain passes in the Alps.

351.title: Constantius
351.body: Emperor Constantius II elevates his 25-year-old cousin Constantius Gallus to Caesar at Sirmium (Pannonia). He arranges a marriage with his sister Constantina, and puts him in charge of the Eastern portion of the Roman Empire.

351.title: Jewish revolt against Constantius Gallus
351.body: The Jewish revolt against Constantius Gallus breaks out. After his arrival at Antioch, the Jews begin a rebellion in Palestine. The Roman garrison in the town of Diocesarea is wiped out.

351.title: Sugar
351.body: In India, a new process makes possible the extraction of sugar from sugarcane.

351.title: Macedonius I of Constantinople
351.body: Macedonius is restored as Patriarch of Constantinople.

352.title: Constantius
352.body: Emperor Constantius II invades northern Italy in pursuit of the usurper Magnus Magnentius, who withdraws with his army to Gaul. He declares an amnesty for Magnentius' soldiers, many of whom desert to him.

352.title: magister equitum
352.body: Constantius Gallus sends his general (magister equitum) Ursicinus to forcefully put down the Jewish revolt in Palestine. The rebels destroy the cities Diopolis and Tiberias, while Diocesarea is razed to the ground. Ursicinus gives the order to kill thousands of Jews, even children.

352.title: Pope Julius
352.body: Pope Julius I dies after a 15-year reign in which he has made himself the chief opponent of Arianism. He is succeeded by Pope Liberius as the 36th pope, who immediately writes to Constantius II requesting a council at Aquileia to discuss the former Alexandrian patriarch Athanasius, who opposes the Arian belief to which the emperor subscribes.

352.title: Milan
352.body: By the end of the year Constantius enters Milan.

352.title: Huns
352.body: War begins between the Huns and the Alans.

353.title: Battle of Mons Seleucus
353.body: Battle of Mons Seleucus: Emperor Constantius II defeats the usurper Magnentius, who commits suicide in Gaul in order to avoid capture. Constantius becomes sole emperor, and reunifies the Roman Empire.

353.title: Paulus Catena
353.body: Constantius II sends his official Paulus Catena to Britain, to hunt down the opponents supporting Magnentius. Flavius Martinus, vicarius of Britain and supporter of Constantius, opposes the persecutions; he is then accused by Catena of being a traitor. In response, Martinus tries to kill Catena with a sword; he fails and then commits suicide.

353.title: conciliabulum
353.body: Constantius II assembles a conciliabulum at Arles, and condemns Athanasius as Patriarch of Alexandria.

353.title: Wang Xizhi
353.body: Wang Xizhi, Chinese calligrapher, produces "Preface to the Poems Composed at the Orchid Pavilion" in running script style. It becomes a model for future calligraphers.

354.title: Constantius
354.body: Emperor Constantius II recalls his Caesar (and cousin) Constantius Gallus to Constantinople after hearing unfavorable reports about him. Gallus, Caesar of the East, has suppressed revolts in Syria Palaestina and central Anatolia. Constantius strips him of his powers and later has him executed in Pola (Croatia).

354.title: Libanius
354.body: Libanius becomes a teacher of rhetoric in Antioch; his students include John Chrysostom and Theodore of Mopsuestia.

354.title: Late Roman army
354.body: As a result of the armies of the West having been largely withdrawn by the usurper Magnus Magnentius, to fight Constantius II, hordes of barbarians (Franks and Alemanni) cross the upper Rhine into Gaul and invade the lands of the Helvetians.

354.title: Chronography of 354
354.body: The Roman Calendar of 354, an illuminated manuscript, is drawn up and becomes the earliest dated codex.

354.title: Bulgars
354.body: The Bulgars are first mentioned in extant European chronicles.

355.title: Caesar
355.body: In Mediolanum (Italy), Emperor Constantius II raises his cousin Julian the Apostate to the rank of Caesar. He takes command of the western provinces and marries Constantius' sister, Helena.

355.title: Lentienses
355.body: The Lentienses, a Germanic tribe, are fined by the Roman commander Arbetio under Constantius II for several incursions against the Roman Empire.

355.title: Pope Liberius
355.body: Pope Liberius refuses to sign a condemnation of Athanasius, Patriarch of Alexandria, imposed at Milan by Constantius II. Liberius is exiled to Beroea (Greece) and replaced by Felix II. He becomes an antipope and bishop of Rome.

355.title: Claudius Silvanus
355.body: Claudius Silvanus, accused of treason, proclaims himself Roman Emperor. After 28 days, Ursicinus arrives from Rome and has Silvanus murdered.

355.title: Huns
355.body: The Huns of Central Asia begin their great drive westwards with an advance into Scythia (modern Ukraine). They overcome and absorb the Alans, a nomadic and warlike horse breeding people from the steppes northeast of the Black Sea.

356.title: Constantius
356.body: Emperor Constantius II issues a decree closing all pagan temples in the Roman Empire, and ordering the banishment once again of the anti-Arian patriarch of Alexandria, Athanasius. He tries to have him arrested during a vigil service, but Athanasius flees to the Nitrian desert in Upper Egypt.

356.title: Rhaetia
356.body: Rhaetia (Switzerland) is invaded by the Alemanni.

356.title: Christianity
356.body: The veneration of non-Christian images is banned in the Roman Empire.

356.title: Basilica
356.body: Construction begins on the first basilica of Saint Peter in Rome.

356.title: pictured
356.body: Anthony the Great (pictured) dies at his hermitage near the Red Sea in mid-January at age 105 (approximate), having preached against Arianism, and having tried to codify guidelines for monastic life. His followers subsequently establish the Monastery of Saint Anthony, beginning the tradition of Coptic monasticism.

357.title: Andrew the Apostle
357.body: At about this date, the relics of St Andrew the Apostle are taken from Patras to Constantinople by order of the Emperor Constantius II, and deposited in the Church of the Holy Apostles.

357.title: Constantius
357.body: Emperor Constantius II enters Rome for the first time to celebrate his victory over Magnentius. He address the Senate and the Roman people.

357.title: Ammianus Marcellinus
357.body: Ammianus Marcellinus describes the Pantheon as being "rounded like the boundary of the horizon and vaulted with a beautiful loftiness".

357.title: Pope Liberius
357.body: Late in the year Pope Liberius travels to Sirmium (Pannonia) and agrees to sign documents that effectively undo the Nicene Creed (which implicitly disavows Arianism) and to sever his relationship with the former Alexandrian patriarch Athanasius, who is replaced as patriarch of Alexandria by his Arian opponent George of Cappadocia.

357.title: Caesar
357.body: Summer – Battle of Strasbourg: Julian, Caesar (deputy emperor) and supreme commander of the Roman army in Gaul, wins an important victory against the Alemanni at Strasbourg (Argentoratum), driving the barbarians back behind the Rhine.

358.title: Pope Liberius
358.body: Constantius II recalls Pope Liberius to Rome, where he receives a joyous welcome from the Christians. Antipope Felix II prudently retires to his estate near Porto (Portugal).

358.title: Constantius
358.body: Emperor Constantius II builds new forts to secure upper Mesopotamia. Persia's king Shapur II sends an emissary to Constantinople with gifts and a letter wrapped in white silk. He requests that Constantius return the lands of his ancestors from the Euphrates to the frontier of Macedonia.

358.title: Great Sanhedrin
358.body: The last universally binding decision, of the Great Sanhedrin, establishes a fixed Hebrew calendar.

358.title: Earthquake
358.body: An earthquake destroys Nicomedia, and damages 150 cities in Macedonia, Asia and Pontus.

358.title: foederati
358.body: The Salian Franks capitulate to Julian the Apostate in Gaul. He allows them to form a Roman foederati in Toxandria. Frankish settlers are established in areas in the north and the east to help with the defense of the Rhine frontier.

359.title: Siege of Amida
359.body: Siege of Amida: Shapur II besieges the Roman fortress of Amida (modern Diyarbakir). After seventy-three days the city is conquered and the population is massacred by the Persians. Ammianus Marcellinus is a fortunate survivor and flees to Singara (Iraq).

359.title: Constantius
359.body: July – Emperor Constantius II convenes the Council of Rimini, to resolve the crisis over Arianism in the Church. Some 400 bishops of the Western portion of the Roman Empire attend, while the Eastern bishops simultaneously hold a meeting at Seleucia. Given Saint Jerome's comment that, "The whole world groaned in astonishment to find itself Arian", it appears to have failed.

359.title: Prefect
359.body: The first known Prefect of the city of Constantinople, Honoratus, takes office.

359.title: Sarcophagus of Junius Bassus
359.body: The Sarcophagus of Junius Bassus, in the Old St. Peter's Basilica, Vatican, is made (approximate date).

359.title: Shapur
359.body: King Shapur II the Great of the Persian Empire invades southern Armenia. The Romans implement a scorched earth policy and place strong guards at the Euphrates crossings.

360.title: Alamanni
360.body: The Alamanni raid Raetia (Switzerland), but are pushed back behind the Rhine by Julian, into the Black Forest.

360.title: Julian the Apostate
360.body: February – Julian, Roman Caesar, is proclaimed emperor in Lutetia (modern Paris).

360.title: Hagia Sophia
360.body: 14 February – The first Hagia Sophia church is consecrated in Constantinople.

360.title: Huns
360.body: The Huns invade the Pontic steppes by the thousands, spreading terror as they take over territories held for generations by Alans, Heruls, Ostrogoths and Visigoths.

360.title: Roman Britain
360.body: Roman authorities in Britain export wheat to supply the legions on the Rhine; they have encouraged production of wheat for that purpose.

361.title: Julian the Apostate
361.body: 361–363 – A Julian the Apostate coin is issued. It is now kept at the British Museum, London.

361.title: Roman Empire
361.body: Julian becomes sole emperor of the Roman Empire; he rules from Constantinople, and tries to restore paganism. Constantius II is buried in the Church of the Holy Apostles.

361.title: Christianity
361.body: Emperor Julian tries to organize a pagan church and substitute it for Christianity. Pope Liberius repudiates the Arian creed, and declares that the Council of Ariminum has no authority to issue decrees.

361.title: George of Cappadocia
361.body: George of Cappadocia, the Arian intruding bishop of Alexandria, is murdered in his see and Athanasius of Alexandria returns to his native city in triumph.

361.title: Julian
361.body: July – Julian begins leading his troops eastwards against Constantius II.

362.title: Hellenistic-era warships
362.body: Julian builds a fleet of 50 warships, and more than 1,000 transport boats at Samosata, for his expedition in Persia against King Shapur II the Great.

362.title: Apollo
362.body: The temple of Apollo at Daphne, outside Antioch, is destroyed in a mysterious fire.

362.title: Athanasius of Alexandria
362.body: Athanasius returns to Alexandria and convenes a council, at which he appeals for unity among Christians who differ in terminology, but Emperor Julian orders Athanasius to leave Alexandria. He will remain in exile in Upper Egypt, until after Julian's death the next year.

362.title: Julian the Apostate
362.body: Emperor Julian arrives at Antioch with an expeditionary force (60,000 men), and stays there for nine months, to launch a campaign against the Persian Empire. He secures the co-operation of King Arsaces of Armenia, who fights a bloody guerrilla war against the Persians.

363.title: Battle of Samarra
363.body: Battle of Samarra: Julian is mortally wounded in a skirmish and dies from a wound received during the fighting near Samarra (Iraq). Jovian, general of the Guard, succeeds him and is proclaimed Emperor by the troops.

363.title: Battle of Ctesiphon
363.body: Battle of Ctesiphon: Julian reaches the vicinity of the strongly fortified capital Ctesiphon. King Shapur II in charge of a large Persian army adopts a scorched earth policy, leaving the Romans desperately short of supplies.

363.title: Venus
363.body: The planet Venus occults the planet Jupiter.

363.title: Huns
363.body: The Huns first appear in Europe and reach the Caspian Sea.

363.title: Late Roman army
363.body: The Roman army starts its retreat northward to Corduene (Armenia). Julian marches back up the Tigris and burns his fleet of supply ships. During the withdrawal Julian's forces suffer several attacks from the Persians.

364.title: Augustus
364.body: Valens, brother of Valentinian I, is appointed co-emperor (Augustus) in the palace of Hebdomon (Turkey). He rules the Eastern portion of the Roman Empire, from the Danube to the Persian border, and begins the first anti-pagan persecutions.

364.title: Valentinian
364.body: Valentinian I is proclaimed Emperor by officers of the Roman army at Nicaea in Bithynia. He addresses the soldiers (who threaten to riot) in a speech. He founds the Valentinianic dynasty and rules the Western portion of the Roman Empire, from Caledonia (Scotland) to the Rhine frontier, ensuring it a few years of relative security.

364.title: Jovian
364.body: Emperor Jovian dies after a reign of eight months. He is found dead in his tent at Tyana (Asia Minor) en route back to Constantinople, in suspicious circumstances.

364.title: Theon of Alexandria
364.body: Theon of Alexandria, Greek mathematician, observes a solar eclipse (June 16) and a lunar eclipse (November 25).

364.title: Roman Britain
364.body: Britain is forced to endure fierce barbarian raids.

365.title: Alamanni
365.body: The Alamanni cross the Rhine and invade Gaul. Emperor Valentinian I moves to Paris to command the army and defend the Gallic cities.

365.title: Antipope Felix
365.body: Antipope Felix II dies after a 9-year reign, ending the double occupancy of the papacy.

365.title: Procopius
365.body: Procopius revolts and bribes two legions passing by Constantinople. He proclaims himself Emperor, and takes control of Thrace and Bithynia.

365.title: 365 Crete earthquake
365.body: An earthquake and tsunami devastate Crete and Alexandria and affects Italy, Greece, and Palestine.

365.title: Valens
365.body: Emperor Valens orders the expulsion of the Alexandrian bishop Athanasius from his see, but instead of going into exile Athanasius, now about 67, moves to the outskirts of Alexandria.

366.title: Pope Liberius
366.body: Pope Liberius dies after a 14-year reign and is succeeded by Damasus I as 37th pope. Romans unhappy with this choice elect the antipope Ursicinus.

366.title: Buddhism
366.body: Buddhist monk Lè Zūn has a vision of "golden rays of light shining down on 1,000 Buddhas", resulting in the creation of the Mogao Caves.

366.title: Pontoon bridge
366.body: Valens builds a pontoon bridge across the Danube, and drives the Visigoths further north, where they will come under pressure from the advancing Huns.

366.title: Athanasius of Alexandria
366.body: Athanasius of Alexandria returns from his fifth exile. He has spent four months in his ancestral tomb outside Alexandria.

366.title: Alamanni
366.body: The Alamanni cross the frozen Rhine in large numbers, and invade the Gallic provinces. They capture Alsace and a large part of the Swiss Plateau.

367.title: Great Conspiracy
367.body: Great Conspiracy: The Roman garrison on Hadrian's Wall revolts and allows Picts from Caledonia to devastate Britain. Simultaneously Attacotti, the Scotti from Hibernia (Ireland), and the Saxons from Germania invade the island's mid-western and south-eastern borders. They sack the cities and murder, rape or enslave Romano-British civilians.

367.title: Korea
367.body: The first Korean envoy arrives in Japan, emissary of the government of Kudara.

367.title: New Testament
367.body: The first Listing of the New Testament (Bible) is made by St. Athanasius of Alexandria.

367.title: Battle of Solicinium
367.body: Battle of Solicinium: Emperor Valentinian I launches a punitive expedition against the Alamanni, due to the crises in Britannia and Gaul. The Alamanni re-cross the Rhine and plunder Moguntiacum (modern Mainz).

367.title: Franks
367.body: Winter – Valentinian I mobilises a massive army for his campaign against the Alamanni and the Franks. He summons the Italian and Illyrian legions for a spring offensive.

368.title: Earthquake
368.body: 11 October – An earthquake strikes Nicaea (modern Turkey).

368.title: Hadrian's Wall
368.body: Winter – The barbarians are driven back to their homelands, Hadrian's Wall is retaken and order returns to the Roman diocese. Theodosius reorganises the abandoned forts and mounts punitive expeditions in Hibernia (Ireland).

368.title: Comes Britanniarum
368.body: Great Conspiracy: Picts, Scotti and Saxons reach Roman London and plunder the city. Theodosius, a general (Comes Britanniarum), is sent with a relief force to Britannia. He marches from Richborough, Kent, to deal with the invaders.

368.title: Augusta Treverorum
368.body: Spring – Emperor Valentinian I and his 8-year-old son, Gratian, cross the Rhine with an army into Alamannic territory. He defeats the Alemanni and burns food stores along the border. A temporary peace is signed with Macrian, king of the Bucinobantes, and Valentinian returns to his capital Augusta Treverorum (modern Trier).

369.title: Shapur
369.body: King Shapur II occupies the pro-Roman kingdom of Armenia. He besieges Artogerassa in modern Georgia, where Papas (Pap), son of King Arsaces II (Arshak II), defends the fortress and the royal treasure against Persian forces.

369.title: Fritigern
369.body: Fritigern becomes king of the Visigoths; amidst hostilities with his rival Athanaric, he asks Valens and the Thracian field army to intervene. They end the civil war, and Fritigern converts to Christianity.

369.title: Valens
369.body: Spring – Emperor Valens crosses the Danube, and attacks the Gothic tribes (Greuthungi and Tervingi). Valens and Athanaric, the Gothic king, eventually sign a treaty.

369.title: Ulfilas
369.body: Wulfila creates a Gothic alphabet composed of letters based on Greek and Roman letters, as well as some Germanic runes. He converts the Goths to Arian Christianity.

369.title: Theodosius the Elder
369.body: Theodosius the Elder brings Britain fully back to the Empire after the Great Conspiracy of 367.

370.title: Valentinian
370.body: A law of Valentinian I and Valens bans marriages between Romans and barbarians under penalty of death.

370.title: Athanaric
370.body: Athanaric, Gothic leader of the Tervingi, advances eastwards and takes up a defensive position along the banks of the Dniester (Romania).

370.title: Germanic Invasions
370.body: Germanic Invasions: Various Germanic tribes are forced against the northern borders of the Roman Empire as they flee the invading Huns.

370.title: Huns
370.body: The Huns migrate westward from the Volga into Europe and subjugate the Alans and the Ostrogoths. With their arrival, a tradition of composite bows is introduced.

370.title: Basil of Caesarea
370.body: Basil the Great becomes bishop of Caesarea (Cappadocia).

371.title: Sasanian Empire
371.body: The Persian Empire attains the zenith of its power under King Shapur II as the Romans renew their war against Persia. Hostilities will continue for the next 5 years.

371.title: Augustine of Hippo
371.body: Augustine of Hippo, age 17, travels to Carthage to continue his education in rhetoric.

371.title: Danube
371.body: The fortified cities of the Danube, with Sirmium (Pannonia) at the forefront, contribute to stop an invasion of the Quadi.

371.title: Ausonius
371.body: Roman poet Ausonius writes of a voyage on the Rhine and the Moselle in his work 'Mosella'.

371.title: Baekje
371.body: Baekje forces sack the city of P'yongyang in Goguryeo.

372.title: Buddhism
372.body: Buddhism is adopted as the official religion of Gorguryeo.

372.title: Huns
372.body: The Huns attack the Tervingi on the Dniester, overwhelming them with light cavalry (horse archers), and devastating the settlements of the Goths. King Athanaric is defeated and seeks refuge in Caucaland in the Carpathian Mountains.

372.title: Seonggyungwan
372.body: The national academy of Chinese learning, called Taehak, is established in the kingdom of Goguryeo (Korea).

372.title: Valentinian
372.body: Emperor Valentinian I is engaged in operations against the Alamanni, Quadi and Sarmatians, while his subordinates are dealing with the Roman usurper Firmus in Africa and the Picts in Britain.

373.title: Valens
373.body: Emperor Valens is converted to Arianism, and orders the persecution of Trinitarian Christians in the Roman East.

373.title: Aqueduct of Valens
373.body: The Aqueduct of Valens is inaugurated near Constantinople (modern Istanbul); the aqueduct has a length of 971 meters.

373.title: Battle of the Tanais River
373.body: Battle of the Tanais River: The Huns defeat the Alans near the Don, sending the remnants fleeing westward.

373.title: Shapur
373.body: King Shapur II declares war as a result of Valens' support of Armenia. Emperor Valens makes Antioch his military base for the campaign against Persia.

373.title: Saint Martin of Tours
373.body: Saint Martin of Tours undertakes the Christianization of Gaul.

374.title: Spearthrower Owl
374.body: Spearthrower Owl ascends to the throne and becomes ruler of Teotihuacan (Mexico).

374.title: Mediolanum
374.body: The people of Milan astonish Ambrosius, governor of Aemilia-Liguria, by acclaiming him bishop. He is the second son of the former praetorian prefect of Gaul, and becomes a creative thinker whose ideas will provide the paradigm for medieval church-state relations.

374.title: Quadi
374.body: The Quadi cross the Danube and begin ravaging Pannonia. They avoid the fortified cities and plunder the unprotected countryside.

375.title: Maronite Church
375.body: The Maronite Church is founded by Saint Maron in Lebanon.

375.title: Valentinian
375.body: Emperor Valentinian I concludes an enduring peace with the Alamanni in Germany, then marches into Illyricum to repel an invasion of the Quadi and the Sarmatians on the Danube frontier. While negotiating with the Quadi, Valentinian, age 54, becomes so enraged that he dies in a fit of apoplexy at Brigetio (Hungary). Extreme cruelty has marked his 11-year reign, but he has also founded schools and provided physicians to serve the poor of Constantinople.

375.title: Augusta Treverorum
375.body: Gratian, age 16, takes over the government at Augusta Treverorum (modern Trier), but ministers wishing to retain the loyalty of the Illyrian army fear a usurper. They proclaim Valentinian's 4-year-old son Valentinian II co-emperor with his mother, Justina, as regent. Gratian reserves for himself the administration of the Gallic provinces, and hands over Italy, Illyrium, Hispania and Africa to his stepmother, who makes Mediolanum (Milan) her residence.

375.title: Jerome
375.body: Saint Jerome retires to the desert of Chalcis (Syria).

375.title: Berber people
375.body: In Africa, the dissident Berber prince Firmus is delivered to the Romans by his brother Gildon.

376.title: Edirne
376.body: Lupicinus attacks the Visigoths 9 miles outside Marcianople with hastily gathered local troops. His force (5,000 men) is annihilated and the Goths equip themselves with Roman armour and weapons. Fritigern marches south towards Adrianople (Turkey).

376.title: Greuthungi
376.body: The Greuthungi, led by Alatheus and Saphrax, displaced by the predations of the Huns and Alans, request asylum within the Roman Empire. They are refused. The Roman frontier forces stretched to the breaking point, they slip across the Danube and unite with Fritigern.

376.title: Gothic War
376.body: Gothic War: Emperor Valens permits the Visigothic chieftain Fritigern and his people to cross the Danube from Thrace (later Romania), and settle on Roman soil in Lower Moesia, on the condition that they provide soldiers to the legions. The Visigoths embark by troops on boats and rafts, and canoes made from hollowed tree trunks. The river is swollen by frequent rains; a large number try to swim and are drowned in their struggle against the force of the stream.

376.title: Abuse
376.body: The Romans fail to disarm the Visigoths, bungle administration of the refugees, and mistreat them, taking some of their children as slaves. The Goths break out of their containment area along the Danube and strike south towards the low-lying fertile region near Marcianople (Bulgaria). Although defying the local Roman officials, they are not in open revolt.

376.title: Siege
376.body: Winter – Fritigern attempts an unsuccessful siege of Adrianople. His troops try to storm the city walls, but abandon the attacks and break into small bands, better able to forage and feed themselves. Roman prisoners switch sides and give the Goths a valuable source of local intelligence.

377.title: Battle of the Willows
377.body: Battle of the Willows: The Romans abandon the guerrilla strategy and are attacked by the Goths. The battle is indecisive but both sides suffer heavy casualties. The only Roman army available to face the Goths is no longer a fighting force.

377.title: Sassanid Empire
377.body: Valens concludes a peace with the Persian Empire and leaves enough troops to defend the eastern frontier. The Saracens under Queen Mavia revolt and devastate a swath of territory stretching from Phoenicia and Palestine as far as the Sinai (Egypt). Valens successfully brings the uprising under control.

377.title: Gothic War
377.body: Gothic War: Famine in Lower Moesia (occupied by the Goths) takes a fearsome toll. Fritigern and his followers appeal for help, but the governors Lupicinus and Maximus regard them as second-class citizens. Little help is forthcoming, and thousands starve to death.

377.title: Shapur
377.body: Persian king Shapur II pushes the Huns back across the Caucasus.

377.title: Food
377.body: Autumn – Bands of predatory "barbarians" spread throughout the province in search of food, supplies and booty. Most Roman troops are bottled up in the towns. Some elite units remain in the field and skirmish with the Goths.

378.title: Lauriacum
378.body: Gratian sets out from Lauriacum (Austria) with a body of light armed troops. His force is small enough to travel by boat down the Danube. He halts for four days at Sirmium (Serbia) suffering from fever.

378.title: Fritigern
378.body: Fritigern concentrates his army at Cabyle (Bulgaria). The Goths are mainly centred in the river valleys south of the Balkan Mountains, around the towns of Beroea, Cabyle and Dibaltum.

378.title: magister militum
378.body: Spring – Emperor Valens returns to Constantinople and mobilises an army (40,000 men). He appoints Sebastianus, newly arrived from Italy, as magister militum to reorganize the Roman armies in Thrace.

378.title: Roman aqueduct
378.body: Valens completes the aqueduct of Constantinople begun by Constantine I.

378.title: Siyaj K'ak'
378.body: Siyaj K'ak' begins to replace Mayan kings with relatives of Spearthrower Owl, emperor of Teotihuacan.

379.title: Niall of the Nine Hostages
379.body: Niall becomes High King of Ireland.

379.title: Former Qin
379.body: Former Qin captures the city of Xiangyang from Eastern Jin. The Buddhist monk Dao'an is transferred from Xiangyang to Chang'an.

379.title: Augustus
379.body: Emperor Gratian elevates Flavius Theodosius at Sirmium, giving him the title Augustus with power over all the eastern provinces. Theodosius comes to terms with the Visigoths and settles them in the Balkans as military allies (foederati).

379.title: Roman Britain
379.body: Britain is forced to endure fierce Barbarian raids.

379.title: John Chrysostom
379.body: John Chrysostom writes a book on the Christian education of children.

380.title: Roman Empire
380.body: Rome's enemies (the Germans, Sarmatians and Huns) are taken into Imperial service; as a consequence, barbarian leaders begin to play an increasingly active role in the Roman Empire.

380.title: Chandragupta
380.body: The annexation of western provinces by Chandragupta II gives him commerce with Europe and Egypt.

380.title: Edict of Thessalonica
380.body: Edict of Thessalonica: Theodosius I, with co-emperors Gratian and Valentinian II, declare their wish that all Roman citizens convert to trinitarian Christianity, in accordance with the patriarchs of Rome and Alexandria, implicitly rejecting the Arianism of the patriarch of Constantinople as heretical.

380.title: Ticonius
380.body: Ticonius writes a commentary on the Bible's Book of Revelation.

380.title: Visigoths
380.body: The Visigothic chieftain Fritigern dies after ravaging the Balkans; his rival Athanaric becomes king of the entire Gothic nation.

381.title: Mediolanum
381.body: Emperor Gratian moves the capital to Mediolanum (modern-day Milan). Because of his Christian beliefs, he eliminates Pontifex Maximus as Imperial title. Gratian also refuses the robe of office, insulting the pagan aristocrats of Rome.

381.title: foederati
381.body: The Visigothic chieftain Athanaric becomes the first foreign king to visit the Eastern Roman capital of Constantinople. He negotiates a peace treaty with emperor Theodosius I that makes his people foederati as "one body within the imperial soldiery". Athanaric dies 2 weeks later after an 18-year reign in which he has been undisputed king of all the Goths for just 1 year.

381.title: First Council of Constantinople
381.body: First Council of Constantinople (some authorities date this council to 383): Theodosius I calls a general council to affirm and extend the Nicene Creed, and denounce Arianism and Apollinarism. Most trinitarian churches consider this an Ecumenical council.

381.title: Sciri
381.body: The Sciri together with the Huns attack along Rome's lower Danubian frontier.

381.title: Council of Aquileia
381.body: Council of Aquileia: Ambrose and the council depose the Arian bishops Palladius of Ratiaria and Secundianus of Singidunum.

382.title: foederati
382.body: Emperor Theodosius I commands his general Saturninus to conclude a peace treaty with the Visigoths, allowing them to settle south of the Danube. They are installed as foederati in Moesia and Thrace with the title of "Allies of the Roman People", in exchange for furnishing a contingent of auxiliary troops to defend the borders.

382.title: Council of Rome
382.body: The Council of Rome establishes Biblical canon in the Catholic Church. Pope Damasus I commissions a revision of the Vetus Latina, eventually resulting in the Vulgate of Jerome.

382.title: Blessed Virgin Mary
382.body: The first sermons declaring the virginity of Mary are given by John Chrysostom.

382.title: Gratian
382.body: Emperor Gratian refuses the divine attributes of the Imperial cult and removes the Altar of Victory from the Senate.

382.title: Trinitarianism
382.body: The same council adopts Trinitarianism as doctrine, condemning Apollinarism. Theodosius I orders the death of members of the Manichaean monks.

383.title: Stilicho
383.body: Theodosius I sends Flavius Stilicho as an envoy to the Persian court of King Shapur III at Ctesiphon, to negotiate a peace settlement relating to the partition of Armenia.

383.title: Magnus Maximus
383.body: Magnus Maximus withdraws Roman troops from the region of Wales, marking the effective end of Roman rule in Wales.

383.title: First Council of Constantinople
383.body: First Council of Constantinople (some authorities date this council to 381): Theodosius I calls a general council to affirm and extend the Nicene Creed, and denounce Arianism and Apollinarism. Most trinitarian Christian churches consider this an Ecumenical council.

383.title: Gratian
383.body: Emperor Gratian, age 24, is assassinated at Lugdunum (modern-day Lyon), leaving a young widow Laeta. Pannonia and Africa maintain their allegiance to co-emperor Valentinian II, now 12, whose mother, Justina, rules in his name.

383.title: Roman Britain
383.body: Roman troops in Britannia proclaim Magnus Maximus as their Emperor. He crosses over to the continent and makes Trier his capital. Gaul, the Italian provinces and Hispania proclaim loyalty to him.

384.title: Shapur III
384.body: King Shapur III signs the Peace of Acilisene with Theodosius I. Armenia is divided in two kingdoms and becomes a vassal state of the Roman Empire and Persia. The friendly relations survive for 36 years.

384.title: Edict
384.body: An edict of Theodosius I closes pagan temples in the Nile Valley (Egypt).

384.title: Pontifex Maximus
384.body: Pope Siricius succeeds Damasus I as the 38th pope. He takes the title Pontifex Maximus, after it is relinquished by the late emperor Gratian.

384.title: Forum of Theodosius
384.body: The Forum of Theodosius ("Forum of the Bull") is built in Constantinople.

384.title: Chimnyu of Baekje
384.body: King Chimnyu ascends to the throne of Baekje (Korea); he welcomes the Indian Buddhist monk Marananta into his palace, and later declares Buddhism the official religion.

385.title: Synod
385.body: The Roman synod exiles the prophet Jerome, who has incorporated ideas first propounded by the Roman statesman Cicero. He departs for Egypt, Bethlehem, and Jerusalem, accompanied by the Christian ascetic Paula, who will edit Jerome's translation of the Bible, which becomes the Latin Vulgate.

385.title: Priscillian
385.body: Priscillian, Spanish bishop, is accused of Manichaeism and magic, and beheaded at Trier . He becomes the first person in the history of Christianity to be executed for heresy.

385.title: Pope Siricius
385.body: Pope Siricius issues the Directa Decretal, proclaiming the primacy of Rome and the priestly obligation of celibacy.

385.title: Jinsa of Baekje
385.body: Jinsa of Baekje becomes the 16th king of the ancient Korean kingdom of Baekje.

385.title: Aurelios Zopyros
385.body: Aurelios Zopyros becomes the last reported athlete at the Ancient Olympic Games. He is a victor in "junior boxing" (pankration).

386.title: Magnus Maximus
386.body: Magnus Maximus invades Italy; he destroys Novara for supporting his rival Valentinian II.

386.title: Theodosius
386.body: Emperor Theodosius I signs a peace treaty with King Shapur III; they divide Armenia into two kingdoms (vassal states). The treaty establishes friendly relations between the Roman Empire and Persia for the next 36 years.

386.title: Saint Ambrose
386.body: Saint Ambrose defends the rights of the Catholic Church with respect to those of the State.

386.title: List of Roman victory columns
386.body: A column is constructed at Constantinople in honour of Theodosius I. Reliefs depict the emperor's victory over the barbarians in the Balkan.

386.title: Northern Wei Dynasty
386.body: The Northern Wei Dynasty begins in China. The Tuoba clan of the Xianbei tribe (proto-Mongol people) is politically separated from the Chinese dynasties established in Jiankang (modern Nanjing). The Northern Wei rulers are ardent supporters of Buddhism.

387.title: Magnus Maximus
387.body: Magnus Maximus, usurping emperor of the West, invades Italy. Emperor Valentinian II, age 16, is forced out of Rome. He flees with his mother Justina and sisters to Thessaloniki (Thrace).

387.title: Peace of Acilisene
387.body: Peace of Acilisene: King Shapur III signs a treaty with Theodosius I. Armenia is divided in two kingdoms, and becomes a vassal state of the Roman Empire and Persia.

387.title: Augustine of Hippo
387.body: Augustine is baptized on Easter Vigil by Saint Ambrose, Bishop of Milan.

387.title: Theodosius
387.body: Spring – Emperor Theodosius I increases the taxes in Antioch. A peasant uprising leads to a riot, and public buildings are set afire. Theodosius sends imperial troops to quell the disturbance, and closes the public baths and theatres.

387.title: Paralysis
387.body: Oribase, Greek doctor, publishes a treatise on paralysis and bleedings.

388.title: Shapur III
388.body: King Shapur III dies after a reign in which he has partitioned Armenia with the Roman Empire. He is succeeded by his son Bahram IV, who becomes the twelfth Sassanid king of Persia.

388.title: Christians
388.body: A group of Christians storms the synagogue of the city Callinicum (Syria), at the Euphrates.

388.title: Colonia Claudia Ara Agrippinensium
388.body: Frankish invasion of 388: Rebellious Franks led by Marcomer, Sunno and Genobaud invade the Roman Empire with plunder and threaten Colonia Claudia Ara Agrippinensium (Cologne). A part of the invaders are destroyed by the Romans in the charcoal forest (present-day Belgium). The Roman general Quintines pursues the invaders on Frankish soil and is ambushed.

388.title: Paternus
388.body: Paternus becomes bishop of the Episcopal see of Braga (Portugal).

388.title: Battle of the Save
388.body: Battle of the Save: Emperor Theodosius I defeats Magnus Maximus near Emona (modern Slovenia). Theodosius is in command of an army including Goths, Huns and Alans. Valentinian II, now 17, is restored as Roman Emperor.

389.title: Paganism
389.body: All pagan buildings in Alexandria, including the library, are destroyed by fire.

390.title: 390–401 – Priestess of Bacchus
390.body: 390–401 – Priestess of Bacchus: Late Antiquity ivory diptych; documents the relationship of the senators Quintus Aurelius Symmachus and Virius Nicomachus Flavianus. It commemorates the marriage of the two families. The right panel is inscribed "Symmachorum", with an elaborately dressed priestess who makes an offer on an altar.

390.title: Obelisk
390.body: Theodosius I brings an obelisk from Egypt to the Hippodrome of Constantinople.

390.title: Kama Sutra
390.body: The Kama Sutra is revised by Vatsyayana.

390.title: April – Massacre of Thessalonica
390.body: April – Massacre of Thessalonica: Resentment among the citizens of Thessalonica (Macedonia) breaks out into violence after the arrest of a popular charioteer. Butheric, military commander of Illyricum, is murdered. Emperor Theodosius I orders vengeance, despite the pleas for mercy by Ambrose, bishop of Milan; more than 7,000 inhabitants are massacred by the Roman army.

390.title: Jerome
390.body: Jerome, having finished the Latin translation of the New Testament, begins translating the Old Testament.

391.title: Library of Alexandria
391.body: Flames destroy the great Library of Alexandria, established in the Mouseion in the fourth century BC. Among the items lost in the fire are works of science, including parchments by the Greek astronomer Aristarchus of Samos asserting that the Earth orbits the Sun, and dozens of dramatic works by Euripides and Sophocles.

391.title: Theodosius
391.body: Emperor Theodosius I establishes Christianity as the official state religion. All non-Christian temples in the Roman Empire are closed. The eternal fire in the Temple of Vesta at the Roman Forum is extinguished, and the Vestal Virgins are disbanded.

391.title: urban prefect
391.body: Quintus Aurelius Symmachus, urban prefect of Rome, pleads for traditional cult practices. He petitions Theodosius I to re-open the pagan temples, but is opposed by Ambrose.

391.title: Theophilus I of Alexandria
391.body: Patriarch Theophilus destroys all pagan temples in Alexandria under orders from Theodosius I. Christians go on an iconoclastic rampage, smashing religious symbols or monuments through the city and destroying the Temple of Serapis. The "Order of Monks" known as the Parabalani take charge of patrolling the streets.

391.title: Gwanggaeto the Great
391.body: King Gwanggaeto the Great of Goguryeo (Korea) ascends to the throne.

392.title: magister militum
392.body: Stilicho, Roman general (magister militum), defeats the Visigoths and Huns in Thrace. Emperor Theodosius I permits Alaric to go free on condition he provides, as foederati, military services to the Roman Empire.

392.title: Edict
392.body: Theodosius I issues an edict reinforcing the prohibition of prayers or sacrifices at non-Christian temples, leading to the persecution of pagans and the end of the almost 2,000-year-old Eleusinian Mysteries. He also bans items of pagan spiritual significance that could be used in the home, such as incense or spiritual figures.

392.title: Valentinian
392.body: Emperor Valentinian II, age 21, dies while advancing into Gaul against the Frankish usurper Arbogast. He is found hanging in his residence at Vienne under unknown circumstances.

392.title: Eugenius
392.body: Arbogast nominates Eugenius, Roman teacher of rhetoric, as the next emperor of the Western Roman Empire. He sends ambassadors to Theodosius's court asking for his recognition.

392.title: Asin of Baekje
392.body: Asin becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Baekje.

393.title: Paganism
393.body: Theodosius I demands the destruction of pagan temples, holy sites, and ancient objects throughout the Roman Empire.

393.title: Synod of Hippo
393.body: Synod of Hippo: A council at Hippo Regius (Algeria) is hosted by the Church. The bishops approve a canon of Sacred Scripture that correspond to the Roman Catholic Church.

393.title: Ancient Olympic Games
393.body: Theodosius I abolishes the Greek Olympic Games, ending a thousand years of festivals, as part of the general Christian policy to establish universal Christian worship in accordance with the doctrines set forth in the Nicene Creed (the next Olympic Games will not be held until 1896).

393.title: Augustus
393.body: Emperor Theodosius I proclaims his son Honorius, age 8, co-ruler (Augustus) of the Western Roman Empire.

393.title: Astronomer
393.body: Chinese astronomers observe the guest star SN 393.

394.title: Huns
394.body: Winter – The Huns cross the frozen Danube and destroy the villages built by the Goths. Theodosius I, six hundred miles away in Italy, sends no reinforcements to defend the northern frontier.

394.title: Notitia Dignitatum
394.body: Late Roman army: The Notitia Dignitatum shows the development of forces in the Roman Empire. By now 200,000 soldiers guard the borders, and a reserve force of 50,000 is available for deployment. Many non-Roman soldiers are from Germanic tribes: Alamanni, Franks, Goths, Saxons and Vandals.

394.title: Egyptian hieroglyphs
394.body: The last known hieroglyphic inscription, known as the Graffito of Esmet-Akhom, is written in Philae, Egypt.

394.title: Rome
394.body: In Rome, the sacred fire stops burning (see Vesta and Vestal Virgins).

394.title: magister militum
394.body: Battle of the Frigidus: Emperor Theodosius I defeats and kills the usurper Eugenius. The forces of Theodosius are bolstered by numerous auxiliaries, including 20,000 Visigoth federates under Alaric. The Frankish general Arbogast (magister militum) escapes into the Alps and commits suicide.

395.title: Agricultural land
395.body: An estimated 330,000 acres of farmland lie abandoned in Campania (southern Italy), partly as a consequence of malaria from mosquitoes bred in swampy areas, but mostly because imprudent agriculture has ruined the land.

395.title: Huns
395.body: The Huns begin their large-scale attack on the Eastern Roman Empire. They invade Armenia, Cappadocia, and enter parts of Syria, threatening Antioch.

395.title: Theodosius
395.body: Emperor Theodosius I, age 48, dies of a disease involving severe edema in Milan. The Roman Empire is divided for the final time into an eastern and a western half. The Eastern Roman Empire is centered in Constantinople under Arcadius, son of Theodosius, and the Western Roman Empire in Mediolanum under his brother Honorius.

395.title: Augustine of Hippo
395.body: Augustine, age 40, becomes bishop of Hippo Regius (modern Algeria). His assignment is the reunification of the Roman Catholic Church in Africa, primarily focusing on the Donatist movement led by Primianus of Carthage.

395.title: Aelia Eudoxia
395.body: Arcadius marries Aelia Eudoxia, daughter of the Frankish general Flavius Bauto (without the knowledge or consent of Rufinus, Praetorian prefect of the East). His seven-year-old half-sister, Galla Placidia, is sent to Rome, where she spends her childhood in the household of Stilicho and his wife Serena.

396.title: Revolt of Alaric
396.body: Revolt of Alaric I: The Visigoths, led by Alaric I, rampage through Greece and plunder Corinth, Argos and Sparta. They destroy the Temple of Eleusis, and harry the Peloponnese. Stilicho makes peace with the Goths, and allows them to settle in Epirus (Balkans).

396.title: magister militum
396.body: Stilicho, Roman general (magister militum), controls the young emperor Honorius as his regent, and becomes the de facto ruler of the Western Roman Empire. He enlists the Alemanni and the Franks, to defend the Rhine frontier.

396.title: Emperor An of Jin
396.body: Emperor Jìn Ān Dì, age 14, succeeds his father Emperor Xiaowu as ruler of the Eastern Jin dynasty after he is murdered by his concubine Honoured Lady Zhang.

396.title: Tian Wang
396.body: Lü Guang claims the title "Heavenly Prince" (Tian Wang), signifying his claim to the Later Liang Kingdom.

397.title: Mor Gabriel Monastery
397.body: Mor Gabriel Monastery is founded and located on the Tur Abdin plateau near Midyat (Turkey).

397.title: Ambrose
397.body: Ambrose, Archbishop of Milan, dies in his diocese after 23 years in office, during which he dominated the political life of the Roman Empire.

397.title: Honorius
397.body: Emperor Honorius passes a law making barbarian styles of dress illegal in the city of Rome. As a result of this law, everybody in Rome is forbidden from wearing boots, trousers, animal skins, and long hair. This law is passed in response to the increasing popularity of barbarian fashions among the people of Rome.

397.title: Confessions
397.body: Augustine of Hippo begins his Confessions, an autobiography that recounts his intellectual and spiritual development.

397.title: Xiongnu
397.body: The Xiongnu occupy the Gansu area, an economically important province situated along the Silk Road.

398.title: comes
398.body: Gildonic Revolt: Gildo, a Berber serving as a high-ranking official (comes) in Mauretania, rebels against the Western Roman Empire. The Gildonic Revolt is instigated by a powerful official in the Eastern Roman Empire named Eutropius, who wishes to undermine his enemies in the Western Roman Empire by cutting off the grain supply to Rome. After Gildo takes much of North Africa and cuts off the grain supply to Rome, Flavius Stilicho returns to Italy to raise troops against the rebels.

398.title: Edict
398.body: An imperial edict obliges Roman landowners with plantations to yield 1/3 of their fields to the "barbarians" who have been settled in the Roman Empire.

398.title: magister militum
398.body: Eutropius, Roman general (magister militum), celebrates his victory over the Huns ("the wolves of the North") in a parade through Constantinople (see 395).

398.title: Confessions
398.body: Augustine of Hippo completes his Confessions, an autobiography that recounts his intellectual and spiritual development.

398.title: John Chrysostom
398.body: John Chrysostom, Archbishop of Constantinople, receives a delegation of clergy who want to close the pagan temples at Gaza (Palestine) where worshippers are openly defying the law. John works through the eunuch Eutropius, who has great power over emperor Arcadius, and within a week an imperial Constitution is issued closing the Roman temples, but the official appointed to execute this order is bribed.

399.title: Bahram
399.body: King Bahram IV dies after an 11-year reign. He is succeeded by Yazdegerd I, who becomes the thirteenth Sassanid Emperor of Persia.

399.title: Faxian
399.body: Fa-Hien, Chinese Buddhist monk, travels to India, Sri Lanka and Kapilavastu (modern Nepal).

399.title: Pope Anastasius
399.body: Anastasius I succeeds Siricius as the 39th pope. He seeks to reconcile the churches of Rome and Antioch. Anastasius also condemns the doctrine of Origen.

399.title: magister militum
399.body: Gainas, a Gothic leader, is made magister militum and forms an alliance with deserters of Tribigild along the Bosphorus. He proclaims himself co-regent (usurper), and installs his forces in Constantinople. Gainas deposes anti-Gothic officials and has Eutropius, imperial advisor (cubicularius), executed.

399.title: munera
399.body: The Emperor Honorius of the Western Roman Empire, who is only 15 years old, closes the gladiatorial schools in Rome, and legally ends munera (gladiator games).

400.title: Mausoleum
400.body: The mausoleum of Galerius in Salonica (Greece) is converted into a church.

400.title: ad hoc
400.body: A riot breaks out in Constantinople; the Great Palace is burned to the ground. Gainas, a Gothic leader, attempts to evacuate his soldiers out of the city, but 7,000 armed Goths are trapped and killed by order of Arcadius. After the massacre, Gainas tries to escape across the Hellespont, but his rag-tag ad hoc fleet is destroyed by Fravitta, a Gothic chieftain in imperial service.

400.title: Hypatia
400.body: Hypatia, Greek philosopher, distinguishes herself as one of the first female scientists and becomes head of the Neo-Platonist school at Alexandria.

400.title: Empty tomb
400.body: Resurrection and "Two Marys with Angel near the Empty Tomb", panel of a diptych, found in Rome, is made. It is now kept at Castello Sforzesco, Milan (approximate date).

400.title: Danube
400.body: Winter – Gainas leads the remaining Goths back to their homeland across the Danube. They meet the Huns and are defeated; the Hunnic chieftain Uldin sends the head of Gainas to Constantinople, where Arcadius receives it as a diplomatic gift.

401.title: Visigoths
401.body: The Visigoths, led by king Alaric I, cross the Alps and invade northern Italy.

401.title: magister militum
401.body: Stilicho, Roman general (magister militum), leads his army in an extensive campaign against the Vandals in Rhaetia (Switzerland).

401.title: Pope Anastasius
401.body: Pope Anastasius I dies at Rome after a 2-year reign, and is succeeded by Innocent I as the 40th pope proclaiming his universal power over the whole of Christendom.

401.title: comitatenses
401.body: The old Legio II Adiutrix, part of which had always been stationed at Aquincum (modern Budapest), is divided into two comitatenses, and shipped to Britannia.

401.title: Slavery
401.body: Piracy is committed by slave-traders from Galatia (Anatolia), along the coasts of Africa.

402.title: Battle of Pollentia
402.body: Battle of Pollentia: Stilicho recalls troops from Britain and the Rhine frontier to defend Italy. He decides to attack the Goths on Easter Sunday, and manages to capture Alaric's wife and children.

402.title: Faxian
402.body: Fa-Hien, Chinese Buddhist monk, makes a pilgrimage to India, initiating Sino-Indian relations. Stirred by his faith to Buddhism, he visits the sites of Siddhartha Gautama's life.

402.title: Visigoths
402.body: The Visigoths advance on Mediolanum (modern Milan) and besiege Asti in Liguria. King Alaric I sends envoys to negotiate a peace, but the Roman government refuses to make deals with "barbarians."

402.title: Cynegius
402.body: Cynegius, acting for Emperor Arcadius at the insistence of Bishop Porphyry, orders the destruction of pagan temples in Gaza.

402.title: Gwanggaeto the Great
402.body: King Gwanggaeto the Great of Goguryeo (Korea) defeats the forces of Later Yan, seizing some of their border fortresses.

403.title: June – Battle of Verona
403.body: June – Battle of Verona: The Visigoths, under command of King Alaric I, invade Italy again, and advance through the Brenner Pass. Stilicho, with an army of 30,000 men, defeats the Goths north of Verona. Alaric makes a truce, and withdraws eastward to Illyricum.

403.title: Theodosius
403.body: Theodosius II, age 2, becomes consul of the Eastern Roman Empire.

403.title: Synod of the Oak
403.body: The Synod of the Oak deposes and banishes John Chrysostom, bishop of Constantinople, but shortly afterward he is recalled only to be banished again.

403.title: Huiyuan
403.body: Hui Yuan argues that Buddhist monks should be exempt from bowing to the emperor.

403.title: Honorius
403.body: Emperor Honorius and Stilicho are honored with a triumphal march, for the victories against the Goths and Vandals. This becomes the last victory celebrated in Rome.

404.title: John Chrysostom
404.body: Eudoxia exiles John Chrysostom, bishop of Constantinople, for criticizing her sumptuous lifestyle. He is banished to the Caucasus in Armenia. Pope Innocent I at Rome orders a synod to reinstate the bishop, but his envoys are imprisoned.

404.title: Fravitta
404.body: Fravitta, a Goth serving the Eastern Roman Empire as a high-ranking general, is executed on the behest of a powerful official named Ioannes. Fravitta is executed because he accused Ioannes of pitting Emperor Arcadius and Emperor Honorius (of the Eastern and Western Roman Empires, respectively) against each other. The execution of Fravitta results in the Eastern Roman Empire losing one of their most loyal and competent generals. (404 or 405)

404.title: On Why Monks Do Not Bow Down Before Kings
404.body: The Chinese Buddhist monk Huiyuan, who founded the Pure Land Buddhism sect and the monastery on Mount Lushan, writes the book On Why Monks Do Not Bow Down Before Kings in this year. In his book he argues that although the Buddhist clergy should remain independent and undisturbed by politics, the Buddhist laymen nonetheless make good subjects under monarchs, due to their fear of retribution of karma and desire to be reborn in paradise.

404.title: Last known gladiator fight in Rome
404.body: Last known gladiator fight in Rome: This date is usually given as the date of the martyrdom of Saint Telemachus, a Christian monk who was stoned by the crowd for trying to stop a gladiators' fight in a Roman amphitheatre.

404.title: Gwanggaeto the Great
404.body: Gwanggaeto the Great of Goguryeo (Korea) attacks Liaodong and takes the entire Liaodong Peninsula.

405.title: Africa
405.body: In the Roman province of Africa, Augustine of Hippo opposes Donatism as a heresy.

405.title: Honorius
405.body: Emperor Honorius closes the Flavian Amphitheatre (Colosseum), in an austerity move that abolishes amusements.

405.title: Jerome
405.body: Jerome's Vulgate translation of the Bible into Latin is completed by his translations of the Tanakh from the Hebrew language.

405.title: War of Radagaisus
405.body: War of Radagaisus: King Radagaisus leads an invasion with a force of 20,000 men and crosses the Alps. He spends the winter in the Po Valley and is observed by Stilicho, who lacks sufficient strength to prepare an offensive against the invading German tribes. The exact numbers of the migration are unknown, probably nearly 100,000, including Alans, Burgundians, Goths, Vandals, and other smaller tribes.

405.title: Theon of Alexandria
405.body: Theon of Alexandria, Greek mathematician, dies at age 70 (approximate), having been helped in his work by his daughter Hypatia.

406.title: Radagaisus
406.body: Radagaisus is forced to retreat into the hills of Fiesole. There he tries to escape, but is captured by the Romans.

406.title: Faxian
406.body: Faxian, Chinese traveler, arrives in India. He visits the major seats of Buddhist learning (Udyana, Peshawar, and Taxila).

406.title: Stained glass
406.body: Stained glass is used for the first time in churches in Rome.

406.title: Late Roman army
406.body: Radagaisus is executed; 12,000 "barbarians" are incorporated into the Roman army, or sold as slaves.

406.title: Suebians
406.body: Vandals, Alans and Suebians cross the Rhine at Mogontiacum (modern Mainz), beginning an invasion of Gaul (traditional date, this may have occurred in 405).

407.title: magister militum
407.body: Roman Civil war of 407–415:Constantine III, a general (magister militum), declares himself Roman emperor. To extend his dominion over Gaul and Spain, he takes practically all the Roman garrisons from Britain and crosses the English Channel. Constantine occupies Arles and establishes tenuous authority over Gaul, sharing control with marauding "barbarians".

407.title: End of Roman rule in Britain
407.body: End of Roman rule in Britain: After 360 years of occupation, the local regional British-Roman leaders raise their own levies for defence against Saxon sea rovers. They cultivate oysters, having learned the technique from the Romans.

407.title: Gratian
407.body: Gratian, Roman usurper, is installed as emperor after the death of Marcus. According to Orosius, he is a native Briton of the urban aristocracy.

407.title: Tian Wang
407.body: Liu Bobo (Xia Wuliedi) founds the state of Xia, and claims the title "Heavenly Prince" (Tian Wang).

408.title: Caesar
408.body: Roman Civil war of 407–415: The Roman usurper Constantine III establishes his headquarters at Arles (Southern Gaul) and elevates his eldest son, Constans, to the rank of Caesar. He sends him with an expeditionary force under Gerontius to Hispania, in order to suppress the revolt of some members of the Theodosian dynasty, who are loyal to Honorius. August 13 – During a review of troops at Pavia, the soldiers stationed at this city murder the dignitaries present, then rage through the city, slaying anyone they suspected of being a supporter of Stilicho.

408.title: Rex
408.body: September – Rex Alaric I of the Visigoths crosses the Julian Alps with an army of 30,000 men, and marches into the Roman heartland. He lays siege to Rome, and Gothic auxiliaries desert to join Alaric's forces. After much bargaining, the Senate agrees to pay him a ransom of 5,000 pounds of gold, 30,000 pounds of silver, 4,000 silken tunics, and 3,000 hides dyed scarlet.

408.title: Attila
408.body: Attila, age 2, is sent as a child hostage to the court at Rome and in return, the Romans send Flavius Aetius to the Huns.

408.title: Yazdegerd
408.body: King Yazdegerd I of Persia maintains cordial relations with the Roman Empire. He becomes an executor of Arcadius' will and is entrusted with the care of the young Theodosius II until he comes of age.

408.title: Hexamilion wall
408.body: The Hexamilion wall is constructed. Fortifications are built across the Isthmus of Corinth, guarding the only land route into the Peloponnese peninsula from Greece.

409.title: magister militum
409.body: Roman Civil war of 407–415: Spring – Gerontius, Roman general (magister militum), who had been a partisan of Constantine III, revolts in Hispania. He elevates Maximus, his domesticus, as emperor. October 13 – The Vandals, led by King Gunderic, cross the Pyrenees into the Iberian Peninsula.

409.title: foederati
409.body: The Vandals, led by King Gunderic, cross the Pyrenees into the Iberian Peninsula. They receive land from the Romans, as foederati, in Baetica (Southern Spain). The Alans occupy lands in Lusitania and the Suebi control parts of Gallaecia.

409.title: praefectus urbi
409.body: The Visigothic king Alaric I lays siege to Rome a second time, bringing the inhabitants close to starvation. Emperor Honorius, safe in inaccessible Ravenna, refuses to negotiate for peace, despite repeated offers from Alaric, who then comes to terms with the Senate and sets up a rival emperor, Priscus Attalus, prefect (praefectus urbi) of the city.

409.title: Danube
409.body: Honorius agrees that sons of prominent families at court in Ravenna be sent beyond the Danube as hostages; in return, later he calls up ten thousand Hun mercenaries.

409.title: Bacaudic Rebellion
409.body: Bacaudic Rebellion: Peasants in Armorica, northwestern Gallia launch a revolt. The unrest continues until 417.

410.title: Rescript
410.body: According to Zosimus, this is the year when Emperor Honorius sends his Rescript (diplomatic letters) to the Romano-British magistrates, ending Roman rule in Great Britain. However this is likely an example of scribal error. Most recently, David Woods has argued that the account refers instead to Raetia, a Roman province north of Italy.

410.title: Byzantine Empire
410.body: The Eastern Roman Empire sends six legions (6,000 men) to aid Honorius at Ravenna. He negotiates with King Alaric I, who ceremonially deposes Priscus Attalus as co-emperor.

410.title: Council of Seleucia
410.body: Council of Seleucia: Persian Christians create a national church and adopt the Nicene Creed.

410.title: Aléria
410.body: The city of Aléria on the island of Corsica is devastated by a huge fire, destroying its port and most of its inhabitants.

410.title: The City of God
410.body: The Visigoths under Alaric I sack Rome after a third siege. Slaves open the Salarian Gate and Goths loot the city for three days; according to Augustine in The City of God and others, comparatively few Roman men are killed and women raped. Only two churches are burned, and people who took refuge in churches are usually spared.

411.title: magister militum
411.body: Roman Civil war of 407–415: Emperor Honorius sends two Roman generals to deal with the usurper Constantine III in Gaul. They kill Gerontius, Constantine's rebellious general (magister militum) in Spain, then besiege Arles and defeat Constantine III. He is taken prisoner and put to death at Ravenna.

411.title: Ataulf
411.body: King Ataulf leads the Goths into Gaul at the instigation of Honorius, who promises to recognise a Visigothic Kingdom if he defeats the several usurpers who threaten the Roman Empire.

411.title: Mogontiacum
411.body: Following the defeat of Constantine III, the Burgundians and the Gallic nobility proclaim Jovinus, Gallo-Roman senator, emperor of the Western Roman Empire at Mogontiacum (modern Mainz).

411.title: Alans
411.body: The Alans establish their rule in the Roman province of Lusitania (Portugal south of the Duero River and Spain).

411.title: foederati
411.body: The Teutonic tribes in Spain join the Roman Empire as foederati (allies with military commitments).

412.title: Olympiodorus of Thebes
412.body: Winter – Olympiodorus, historical writer, is sent on an embassy by Honorius, and sails in stormy weather around Greece up the Black Sea, to meet the Huns who are located on the middle Danube (modern Bulgaria).

412.title: Walls of Constantinople
412.body: The Theodosian Walls are constructed at Constantinople during the reign of emperor Theodosius II. The work is carried out under supervision of Anthemius, notable praetorian prefect of the East.

412.title: Danube
412.body: The forts on the west bank of the Danube, which were destroyed by the Huns, are rebuilt, and a new Danubian fleet is launched.

412.title: Lazarus of Aix
412.body: Lazarus, bishop of Aix-en-Provence, and Heros, bishop of Arles, are expelled from their sees on a charge of Manichaeism.

412.title: Augustus
412.body: Roman Civil war of 407–415: The Visigoths, led by King Ataulf, move into the south of Gaul. He establishes his residence at Narbonne, and makes an alliance with Emperor Honorius, against the usurper Jovinus. Emperor Jovinus elevates his brother Sebastianus as co-emperor (Augustus) and takes control of Gaul.

413.title: Roman Civil war of 407–415
413.body: Roman Civil war of 407–415: Heraclianus, Roman usurper, lands in Italy with a large army to fight Emperor Honorius. He is defeated in Umbria and flees to Carthage, where he is put to death by envoys of Honorius. May 8 – Honorius signs an edict providing tax relief for the Italian provinces Tuscia, Campania, Picenum, Samnium, Apulia, Lucania and Calabria, which were plundered by the Visigoths.

413.title: Heraclianus
413.body: Heraclianus, Roman usurper, lands in Italy with a large army to fight Emperor Honorius. He is defeated in Umbria and flees to Carthage, where he is put to death by envoys of Honorius.

413.title: De Civitate Dei
413.body: Augustine of Hippo, age 59, begins to write his spiritual book De Civitate Dei (City of God), as a reply to the charge that Christianity was responsible for the decline of the Roman Empire.

413.title: Edict
413.body: Honorius signs an edict providing tax relief for the Italian provinces Tuscia, Campania, Picenum, Samnium, Apulia, Lucania and Calabria, which were plundered by the Visigoths.

413.title: Kumaragupta
413.body: Kumaragupta I succeeds his father Chandragupta II as emperor of the Gupta Empire (India).

414.title: Augusta
414.body: Roman Civil war of 407–415: January 1 – Galla Placidia, half-sister of emperor Honorius, is married to the Visigothic king Ataulf at Narbonne. The wedding is celebrated with Roman festivities and magnificent gifts from the Gothic booty. July 4 – Emperor Theodosius II, age 13, yields power to his older sister Aelia Pulcheria, who reigns as regent and proclaims herself empress (Augusta) of the Eastern Roman Empire.

414.title: Faxian
414.body: Fa-Hien, Chinese Buddhist monk, returns from India and begins translating Buddhist works into Chinese.

414.title: magister militum
414.body: Constantius III, Roman general (magister militum), begins a military campaign against the Visigoths in Gaul. He blockades the Gallic ports and besieges Marseille.

414.title: Galla Placidia
414.body: Galla Placidia, half-sister of emperor Honorius, is married to the Visigothic king Ataulf at Narbonne. The wedding is celebrated with Roman festivities and magnificent gifts from the Gothic booty.

414.title: Priscus Attalus
