-75.body: At the Battle of Saguntum the republican forces on the Iberian Peninsula and the Sertorian rebels fight each other to a draw. Quintus Sertorius is forced to withdraw leaving the battlefield to Pompey and Metellus (the republican commanders).

-75.title: Cicero
-75.body: Cicero is quaestor in Western Sicily.

-74.title: Third Mithridatic War
-74.body: Third Mithridatic War: Battle of Cyzicus: Roman forces under Lucius Lucullus defeat the forces of Mithridates VI of Pontus.

-74.title: Marcus Antonius Creticus
-74.body: Marcus Antonius (father of Mark Antony), a praetor, receives wide-ranging powers and considerable resources to fight the pirates in the Mediterranean Sea.

-74.title: Isauricus
-74.body: Publius Servilius Vatia returns to Rome, where he has triumphed against the pirates in Anatolia, and is given the agnomen Isauricus.

-74.title: Nicomedes IV of Bithynia
-74.body: Nicomedes IV, last king of Bithynia bequeaths his kingdom to the Roman Senate upon his death (75/4 BC).

-73.title: Iberian Peninsula
-73.body: On the Iberian Peninsula (part of the Roman Republic) rebel leader Quintus Sertorius is assassinated by some of his own lieutenants (lead by Marcus Perperna). Perperna takes command of the rebel army.

-73.title: Third Servile War
-73.body: Third Servile War: Spartacus, a Thracian gladiator, escapes with around 70 slave-gladiators from a gladiator school at Capua. They defeat a small Roman force and equip themselves with captured military equipment as well with gladiatorial weapons. Spartacus and his band of gladiators plunder the region surrounding Capua and retire to a defensible position on Mount Vesuvius.

-73.title: Battle of Mount Vesuvius
-73.body: Battle of Mount Vesuvius: Spartacus defeats a Roman militia force (3,000 men) under Gaius Claudius Glaber. The rebel slaves spend the winter of 73–72 BC training, arming and equipping their new recruits, as well as expanding their raiding territory, which includes the towns of Nola, Nuceria, Thurii and Metapontum.

-72.title: Marcus Perperna Veiento
-72.body: Perperna is defeated by Pompey the Great at the Battle of Osca, ending the Sertorian War in Spain.

-72.title: Thurii
-72.body: Winter – Spartacus decides to camp near Thurii. Marcus Licinius Crassus with ten Roman legions tries to trap the rebels in the toe of Italy. He builds a trench and a low earth rampart (with a fortified palisade).

-72.title: Battle of Cabira
-72.body: Battle of Cabira: Lucius Lucullus defeats King Mithridates VI and overruns Pontus. Mithridates flees to Armenia, ruled by his son-in-law Tigranes, who refuses to turn his father-in-law in to Lucius Lucullus.

-72.title: Iberian Peninsula
-72.body: On the Iberian Peninsula (part of the Roman Republic) rebel leader Quintus Sertorius is assassinated by some of his own lieutenants (led by Marcus Perperna). Perperna takes command of the rebel army.

-72.title: Third Servile War
-72.body: Third Servile War: Spartacus moves with his followers northward to the Po Valley. Roman forces under Lucius Gellius Publicola defeat a group of slaves (30,000 men) led by Crixus near Mount Gargano. He kills two-thirds of the rebels, including Crixus himself.

-71.title: Nessebar
-71.body: Nessebar in modern-day Bulgaria comes under Roman rule.

-71.title: Third Servile War
-71.body: Third Servile War ends; Slave rebellion under leadership of Spartacus is crushed by a Roman army under Marcus Licinius Crassus. Slaves taken prisoner are crucified naked along the Via Appia.

-71.title: Creticus
-71.body: Marcus Antonius is defeated by the Cretans, who have made an alliance with the pirates. He is compelled to concede a humiliating peace. Antonius dies in office the same year and is awarded, posthumously, with the cognomen Creticus.

-70.title: Lucullus
-70.body: Lucullus captures Sinop, then invades Armenia.

-70.title: Rome
-70.body: August – In Rome, Cicero prosecutes former governor Verres; Verres exiles himself to Marseille before the trial is over.

-70.title: Phraates III
-70.body: Phraates III becomes the king of Parthia.

-70.title: Roman censor
-70.body: The office of censor is reinstated.

-69.title: Julius Caesar
-69.body: Gaius Julius Caesar is a quaestor in Spain.

-69.title: Roman Republic
-69.body: Roman Republic troops under Lucius Lucullus defeat the army of Tigranes II of Armenia in the Battle of Tigranocerta, and capture Tigranocerta, capital of Armenia.

-69.title: Parthian Empire
-69.body: Parthians and Romans re-establish Euphrates as a frontier.

-69.title: Kydonia
-69.body: Kydonia, an ancient city on the island of Crete falls to Roman military forces.

-69.title: Rhodes
-69.body: Rhodes becomes a bulwark against pirates, the Rhodians are unable to suppress piracy in the Aegean Sea. Delos gets the status of a free port.

-68.title: Ostia Antica
-68.body: Ostia, the harbour city of Ancient Rome, is sacked by pirates. The port is set on fire and the consular war fleet is destroyed.

-68.title: Abgar II of Osroene
-68.body: Abgar II becomes ruler of Osroene.

-68.title: Consuls
-68.body: Consuls: Lucius Caecilius Metellus and Quintus Marcius Rex.

-68.title: Lucius Lucullus
-68.body: Lucius Lucullus defeats Tigranes II of Armenia in the Battle of Artaxata.

-67.title: Julius Caesar
-67.body: Julius Caesar reconciles with Pompey and Crassus

-67.title: Han dynasty
-67.body: December – The army of the Han dynasty Chinese commander Zheng Ji is victorious over the Xiongnu in the Battle of Jushi.

-67.title: Pompey
-67.body: During Pompey's war against the pirates, he raises a fleet of 500 warships and fights with great success.

-67.title: Clemency
-67.body: Pompey offers the ex-pirates and their families clemency, he settles them in agricultural colonies in eastern Mediterranean lands.

-67.title: Sulla
-67.body: Julius Caesar marries Pompeia, a granddaughter of Sulla

-66.title: Talent
-66.body: Tigranes II is forced to surrender, by a payment of 6,000 talents, and is reinstated by Pompey as a "friend of the Roman people" to hold Armenia as a buffer zone.

-66.title: Catiline
-66.body: Catiline accused of conspiring against the Roman Republic with Autronius and the younger Sulla (also in 63 BC during the consulship of Cicero).

-66.title: lex Manilia
-66.body: The lex Manilia, supported by Cicero gives Pompey command over all of Asia.

-66.title: Aristobulus
-66.body: Aristobulus II becomes king and high priest of Judea, until 63 BC.

-65.title: Tigranes
-65.body: Tigranes of Armenia is defeated and captured by Pompey, thus ending all hostilities on the northeastern frontier of Rome.

-65.title: Lex Papia
-65.body: In response to the illegal exercise of citizen rights by foreigners, the Roman Senate passes the Lex Papia, which expels all foreigners from Rome

-65.title: Pompey
-65.body: Pompey the Great subjugates the kingdom of Iberia and makes Colchis a Roman province.

-65.title: Emperor Xuan of Han
-65.body: 9th year of the reign of Emperor Xuan of Han

-64.title: Syria
-64.body: Pompey annexes Syria and captures Jerusalem, annexing Judea.

-64.title: Antiochus XIII Asiaticus
-64.body: King Antiochus XIII Asiaticus is deposed and killed by the Syrian chieftain Sampsiceramus I – this is considered by some the end of the Seleucid dynasty.

-64.title: Pompey
-64.body: Pompey destroys the kingdom of Pontus; king Mithridates VI commits suicide after escaping to the Crimea.

-63.title: Julius Caesar
-63.body: Julius Caesar is elected Pontifex Maximus and praetor for 62 BC.

-63.title: Jew
-63.body: Massacre of over 12,000 Jews on the Temple Mount in Jerusalem by Roman troops, in support of John Hyrcanus II against Aristobulus II.

-63.title: Pompey
-63.body: Pompey conquers the people of Phonecia, Coele-Syria, and Judea for the Roman Republic.

-63.title: Second Catilinarian Conspiracy
-63.body: Second Catilinarian Conspiracy against the Roman Republic is foiled by Cicero.

-63.title: Seleucid Empire
-63.body: Roman annexation of the Seleucid Empire and of Judea as a client kingdom. King Judah Aristobulus II removed from power, while his brother John Hyrcanus II is reappointed king (ethnarch) under Roman suzerainty and high priest, until 40 BC.

-62.title: Julius Caesar
-62.body: Julius Caesar divorces Pompeia, following the sacrilege of Publius Clodius Pulcher.

-62.title: Cicero
-62.body: Cicero delivers his Pro Archia Poeta in defense of Aulus Licinius Archias' claim to Roman citizenship.

-62.title: Quintus Caecilius Metellus Nepos
-62.body: Metellus Nepos, also tribune, leaves Rome.

-62.title: lex frumentaria
-62.body: Cato the Younger, as tribune, presents a lex frumentaria (enacting a grain dole).

-62.title: Catiline
-62.body: The forces of the conspirator Catiline are defeated by the loyal Roman armies of Antonius Hybrida led by Marcus Petreius in the Battle of Pistoria.

-61.title: Legio X Gemina
-61.body: Julius Caesar becomes governor in Hispania and creates Legio X Gemina (3,500 men). He puts down the Callaici and Lusitani rebellions.

-61.title: Pompey
-61.body: Pompey returns to Rome with a triumph in honor of his for victories in the eastern provinces.

-61.title: Marcus Pupius Piso Frugi Calpurnianus
-61.body: Marcus Pupius Piso Frugi as consul attempts to gain ratification of Pompey's Eastern Settlement.

-60.title: Julius Caesar
-60.body: Gaius Julius Caesar suppresses an uprising and conquers all of Lusitania for Rome.

-60.title: First Triumvirate
-60.body: Creation of the First Triumvirate, an informal political alliance between Julius Caesar, Pompey the Great and Marcus Licinius Crassus (or 59 BC).

-60.title: Seleucid Empire
-60.body: The Seleucid Empire comes to an end with the last two emperors being murdered on orders from Rome.

-60.title: Han dynasty
-60.body: The Han dynasty government establishes the Protectorate of the Western Regions, the highest military position of a military commander on the Western frontier (Tarim Basin).

-59.title: Consuls
-59.body: Consuls: Gaius Julius Caesar and Marcus Calpurnius Bibulus (known in jest as "the consulship of Julius and Caesar" due to Bibulus' Social withdrawal from public view to "consult the heavens" in an effort to invalidate Caesar's intended legislation).

-59.title: Colonia
-59.body: The colonia of Florentia, modern Florence, founded.

-59.title: The First Triumvirate
-59.body: The First Triumvirate: Caesar, Pompey and Crassus form an unofficial alliance (or 60 BC).

-59.title: Gazette
-59.body: Caesar makes the gazette Acta Diurna (Daily News) public. The Acta contains details of official decrees and appointments; births, deaths, and marriages. Even sport results—the outcome of the gladiatorial contests and chariot races at the capital.

-58.title: First year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars
-58.body: First year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars: Julius Caesar becomes a provincial governor (proconsul) and leads a Roman army (6 Roman legions; Legio VII, Legio VIII, Legio IX, Legio X, and newly levied Legio XI and Legio XII) into Gaul. He deploys auxiliaries as part of this army, including Balearic slingers, Numidian and Cretan archers, and Celtic/Gallic cavalry (such as the allied Aedui). Caesar builds a 19-mile earthwork, complete with fortifications and watchtowers, between Lake Geneva and the Jura Mountains.

-58.title: Publius Clodius Pulcher
-58.body: Publius Clodius Pulcher, Roman tribune, institutes a monthly corn dole for poor Romans, and exiles Cicero from the city.

-58.title: Berenice IV of Egypt
-58.body: Berenice IV becomes queen of Egypt after temporarily dethroning her father, King Ptolemy XII Auletes.

-58.title: Proconsul
-58.body: Julius Caesar becomes a provincial governor (proconsul) and leads a Roman army (6 Roman legions; Legio VII, Legio VIII, Legio IX, Legio X, and newly levied Legio XI and Legio XII) into Gaul. He deploys auxiliaries as part of this army, including Balearic slingers, Numidian and Cretan archers, and Celtic/Gallic cavalry (such as the allied Aedui).

-57.title: Second year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars
-57.body: Second year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars: Spring – Julius Caesar raises a further two legions (Legio XIII and Legio XIV), bringing his army in Gaul to eight legions (at which strength it remains until 54 BC). Caesar sends Servius Sulpicius Galba with Legio XII into the territory of the Nantuates, Seduni and the Veragri. He occupies Octodurus (modern-day Martigny) in Switzerland.

-57.title: Servius Sulpicius Galba
-57.body: Caesar sends Servius Sulpicius Galba with Legio XII into the territory of the Nantuates, Seduni and the Veragri. He occupies Octodurus (modern-day Martigny) in Switzerland.

-57.title: Legio XIII Gemina
-57.body: Spring – Julius Caesar raises a further two legions (Legio XIII and Legio XIV), bringing his army in Gaul to eight legions (at which strength it remains until 54 BC).

-57.title: Vikramaditya
-57.body: King Vikramaditya establishes the Vikram era.

-57.title: Bak Hyeokgeose of Silla
-57.body: Bak Hyeokgeose becomes the first ruler of the kingdom of Silla (traditional date).

-56.title: Third year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars
-56.body: Third year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars: Battle of Morbihan: Decimus Junius Brutus Albinus, one of Caesar's subordinates, defeats the Veneti of Brittany. The Gauls lose most of their warships to the Romans in a sea battle at modern-day Quiberon Bay. The strongholds on the coast are stormed, and the population is slaughtered or sold into slavery.

-56.title: First Triumvirate
-56.body: The three Roman politician-generals of the First Triumvirate — Caesar, Pompey and Crassus — meet at the Luca Conference at the town of Luca (modern Lucca, in Tuscany), near Pisa, where they renewed their fraying political alliance, and further cemented the three men's increasing consolidation of power in the Roman Republic.

-56.title: Pro Caelio
-56.body: Clodia accuses her former lover Marcus Caelius Rufus of trying to poison her. The trial ends with the defendant acquitted thanks to the Pro Caelio speech of Cicero. There is no more reference to the formerly well-known Clodia.

-56.title: Battle of Morbihan
-56.body: Battle of Morbihan: Decimus Junius Brutus Albinus, one of Caesar's subordinates, defeats the Veneti of Brittany. The Gauls lose most of their warships to the Romans in a sea battle at modern-day Quiberon Bay. The strongholds on the coast are stormed, and the population is slaughtered or sold into slavery.

-56.title: Trinovantes
-56.body: This year, or possibly the following year, the king of the Trinovantes called Imanuentius, is overthrown and killed by his rival Cassivellaunus. His son Mandubracius flees to Gaul and appeals to Julius Caesar for help.

-55.title: Fourth year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars
-55.body: Fourth year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars: Spring – Julius Caesar starts the season campaigning in Illyricum (in the Balkan region) against the Pirustae, who have been raiding Roman territory. Summer – Julius Caesar defeats the Usipetes and the Tencteri, two Germanic tribes who have been driven across the Rhine River by the Suebi. He spreads Roman law and order, and makes the whole country as far as the Channel accessible to trade.

-55.title: Meuse
-55.body: May – Julius Caesar defeats a Germanic army and massacres the women and children near the Meuse and Rhine Rivers (now known as the city of Kessel in the Netherlands).

-55.title: Mithridates IV of Parthia
-55.body: Mithridates IV, claimant to the throne of Parthia, supported by Aulus Gabinius, Roman governor of Syria, is defeated by Surena, general under Orodes, in the Battle of Seleucia.

-55.title: curia
-55.body: Pompey's Theater, the first permanent (non-wooden) theatre in Rome is built. Built of stone on the Field of Mars, it included a temple to Venus Victorious, a public courtyard, and a meeting hall or curia in the far end near the "Sacred Area".

-55.title: Illyricum
-55.body: Spring – Julius Caesar starts the season campaigning in Illyricum (in the Balkan region) against the Pirustae, who have been raiding Roman territory.

-54.title: Fifth year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars
-54.body: Fifth year of Julius Caesar's Gallic Wars: July – Second of Caesar's Invasions of Britain: Julius Caesar receives nominal submission from the tribal chief Cassivellaunus and installs Mandubracius as a friendly king. Winter – Ambiorix revolts in Gaul. He joins with Catuvolcus in an uprising against the Roman army.

-54.title: Crassus
-54.body: Crassus arrives in Syria as proconsul and invades the Parthian Empire, initiating the Roman–Persian Wars, which were to last nearly seven centuries.

-54.title: Pompey
-54.body: Pompey builds the first permanent theatre in Rome.

-54.title: Ambiorix's revolt
-54.body: Winter – Ambiorix revolts in Gaul. He joins with Catuvolcus in an uprising against the Roman army. Caesar's senior officers Lucius Aurunculeius Cotta and Quintus Titurius Sabinus are ambushed by the Eburones, and killed along with almost their entire forces.

-54.title: Octavia the Younger
-54.body: Octavia the Younger and Gaius Claudius Marcellus marry.

-53.title: Legio V Alaudae
-53.body: Gallic War: Julius Caesar suppresses a revolt led by Ambiorix near Sabis (Northern Gaul). At Cenabum (modern Orléans) Roman merchants are massacred by the Carnutes. Vercingetorix, an Arverni chieftain, leads a revolt against Caesar in Central Gaul.

-53.title: Julius Caesar
-53.body: Julius Caesar suppresses a revolt led by Ambiorix near Sabis (Northern Gaul).

-53.title: Artavasdes II of Armenia
-53.body: Artavasdes II becomes king of Armenia.

-53.title: Parthian Empire
-53.body: The Parthian Empire achieved a decisive victory against Rome at the Battle of Carrhae, where General Surena defeated and killed Roman triumvir Marcus Licinius Crassus. According to some historical interpretations, the Mahestan (Parthian noble council) supported preparations for the defense of the empire and advised the king during the military mobilization.

-53.title: Parthian War
-53.body: Parthian War: Crassus sacks the Temple of Hierapolis and the Temple in Jerusalem on his way to engage the Parthians. May 6 – Battle of Carrhae: Romans defeated, and Crassus killed, by Parthians led by General Surena.

-52.title: Oppidum
-52.body: Gallic Wars (Julius Caesar): March – Siege and capture of Avaricum (Bourges). April–May – Siege and repulse from Gergovia. July – Battle of the Vingeanne: Julius Caesar rebuffs, with his German auxiliaries, a Gallic cavalry attack of Vercingetorix.

-52.title: Pro Milone
-52.body: Milo is tried for the murder of Clodius. Despite Cicero's legal defence (Pro Milone) he is found guilty and exiled in Massilia (modern Marseille).

-52.title: Consuls
-52.body: Consuls: Quintus Caecilius Metellus Pius Scipio Nasica and Gnaeus Pompeius Magnus.

-52.title: September – Battle of Alesia
-52.body: September – Battle of Alesia: Julius Caesar defeats the Gallic allies coming to aid Vercingetorix, led by his cousin Vercassivellanus. Vercingetorix surrenders on October 3, signalling the Roman conquest of Gaul. The final pacification of Gaul is completed the following year.

-52.title: Cévennes
-52.body: Winter – Julius Caesar crosses Mons Cevenna (central Gaul) and sends his army through the passes covered with snowdrifts to take the rebellious Arverni by surprise.

-51.title: Ptolemy XII Auletes
-51.body: by March 22 – Pharaoh Ptolemy XII Auletes dies and is succeeded by his eldest surviving daughter Cleopatra VII and her younger brother Ptolemy XIII as co-rulers of the Ptolemaic Kingdom. By August 29, official documents start listing Cleopatra as sole ruler.

-51.title: Pompey
-51.body: Pompey demands that Julius Caesar lay down his command before he can stand for consul.

-51.title: Xiongnu
-51.body: The Xiongnu split into two hordes. The Eastern horde is subject to China.

-51.title: Consuls
-51.body: Consuls: Marcus Claudius Marcellus and Servius Sulpicius Rufus.

-50.title: Roman Senate
-50.body: The Senate recalls Julius Caesar and rescinds his authority. Caesar breaks alliance with Pompey.

-50.title: Ritual
-50.body: Initiation Rites of the Cult of Bacchus, detail of a wall painting in the Villa of the Mysteries, Pompeii, is made (approximate date).

-50.title: Roman Republic
-50.body: The Roman Republic takes control of Judea (approximate date).

-50.title: Scorpio
-50.body: The Roman artillery piece called Scorpio is invented.

-50.title: Consuls
-50.body: Consuls: Lucius Aemilius Paullus and Gaius Claudius Marcellus.

-49.title: Caesar's Civil War commences
-49.body: Caesar's Civil War commences: January 1 – The Roman Senate receives a proposal from Julius Caesar that he and Pompey should lay down their commands simultaneously. The Senate responds that Caesar must immediately surrender his command. January – Caesar leads his army across the Rubicon, which separates his jurisdiction in Cisalpine Gaul from that of the Senate in Rome, and thus initiates a civil war.

-49.title: Spain
-49.body: Caesar advances against Pompeian forces in Spain.

-49.title: Roman Senate
-49.body: The Roman Senate receives a proposal from Julius Caesar that he and Pompey should lay down their commands simultaneously. The Senate responds that Caesar must immediately surrender his command.

-49.title: Cicero
-49.body: Cicero slips out of Italy and goes to Thessaloniki.

-49.title: Crossing the Rubicon
-49.body: January – Caesar leads his army across the Rubicon, which separates his jurisdiction in Cisalpine Gaul from that of the Senate in Rome, and thus initiates a civil war.

-48.title: Alexandria
-48.body: October – Julius Caesar reaches Alexandria, a city founded by Alexander the Great. He is met by an Egyptian delegation from Ptolemy XIII. The Egyptians offer him gifts: the ring of Pompey and his head.

-48.title: Consuls
-48.body: Consuls: Gaius Julius Caesar and Publius Servilius Vatia Isauricus. Caesar is named consul for a period of five years.

-48.title: Caesar's Civil War
-48.body: Caesar's Civil War: January 4 – Julius Caesar lands at Dyrrhachium (Durazzo). March – Mark Antony joins Julius Caesar. April – Siege of Dyrrhachium: Julius Caesar builds a fortified line of entrenchments and besieges Pompey the Great.

-48.title: Winter – Siege of Oricum
-48.body: Winter – Siege of Oricum: Julius Caesar captures the strategic city port of Oricum in Epirus (modern Albania). The garrison opens the town's gate and Lucius Manlius Torquatus surrenders to Caesar.

-48.title: Mark Antony
-48.body: March – Mark Antony joins Julius Caesar.

-47.title: Julius Caesar
-47.body: Julius Caesar returns to Rome and is dictator for a year.

-47.title: Cleopatra VII of Egypt
-47.body: February – Caesar and his ally Cleopatra VII of Egypt defeat the forces of the rival Egyptian Queen Arsinoe IV in the Battle of the Nile. Ptolemy is killed; Caesar, with the aid of Mithridates I of the Bosporus, then relieves his besieged forces in Alexandria.

-47.title: Civil War
-47.body: Civil War: August – Julius Caesar leads victory against Pharnaces II of Pontus and at the Battle of Zela. August – Julius Caesar quells a mutiny of his veterans in Rome. October – Caesar's invasion of Africa, against Metellus Scipio and Labienus, Caesar's former lieutenant in Gaul.

-47.title: Ancient Rome
-47.body: August – Julius Caesar quells a mutiny of his veterans in Rome.

-47.title: Consort Feng Yuan
-47.body: Feng Yuan becomes consort to Emperor Yuan of the Han Dynasty.

-46.title: Julian calendar
-46.body: Civil War: January 4 – Titus Labienus fights Julius Caesar in the Battle of Ruspina. April - Caesar defeats Pompey loyalists at Thapsus. April 6 – Caesar defeats the combined army of Pompeian followers and Numidians under Metellus Scipio and Juba I of Numidia at Thapsus.

-46.title: Consuls
-46.body: Consuls: Gaius Julius Caesar, Marcus Aemilius Lepidus.

-46.title: Julia Paterna Arelate
-46.body: Julius Caesar establishes Julia Paterna Arelate as a colony in Gaul for veterans of the Sixth Legion, at the present day site of Arles.

-46.title: Titus Labienus
-46.body: Titus Labienus fights Julius Caesar in the Battle of Ruspina.

-46.title: Cicero
-46.body: Cicero, in Rome, writes to Varro "If our voices are no longer heard in the Senate and in the Forum, let us follow the example of the ancient sages and serve our country through our writings, concentrating on questions of ethics and constitutional law."

-45.title: Julius Caesar
-45.body: Fourth consulship of Gaius Julius Caesar (without colleague).

-45.title: Civil War
-45.body: Civil War: In his last victory, Julius Caesar defeats the Pompeian forces of Titus Labienus and Pompey the younger in the Battle of Munda. Labienus dies in battle, Pompey the younger is executed, but Sextus Pompey escapes to take command of the remnants of the Pompeian fleet.

-45.title: Legio XIII Gemina
-45.body: The veterans of Julius Caesar's Legions Legio XIII Gemina and Legio X Equestris demobilize. The veterans of the 10th legion are settled in Narbo, while those of the 13th are given somewhat better lands in Italia itself.

-45.title: Julian calendar
-45.body: The Julian calendar takes effect as the civil calendar of the Roman Empire, establishing a solar calendar that is based on the Egyptian calendar of the day.

-45.title: Roman dictator
-45.body: Caesar is named dictator for life.

-44.title: Consuls
-44.body: Consuls: Gaius Julius Caesar and Mark Antony.

-44.title: Rome
-44.body: February – Rome celebrates the festival of the Lupercal. Mark Antony twice presents Caesar with a royal diadem, urging him to take it and declare himself king. He refuses this offer and orders the crown to be placed in the Temple of Jupiter.

-44.title: Augustus
-44.body: April – Octavian returns from Apollonia in Dalmatia to Rome to take up Caesar's inheritance, against advice from Atia (his mother and Caesar's niece) and consul Antony.

-44.title: Transalpine Gaul
-44.body: June – Antony is granted a five-year governorship of northern and central Transalpine Gaul (France) and Cisalpine Gaul (Northern Italy).

-44.title: Ides of March
-44.body: March 15 (the Ides of March) – Julius Caesar, dictator of Rome, is assassinated by a group of senators, amongst them Gaius Cassius Longinus, Marcus Junius Brutus, and Caesar's Massilian naval commander, Decimus Brutus.

-43.title: Battle of Forum Gallorum
-43.body: Battle of Forum Gallorum: Mark Antony, besieging Caesar's assassin Decimus Brutus Albinus in Mutina, defeats the forces of the consul Pansa, but is then immediately defeated by the army of the other consul, Hirtius. Both consuls are killed (Hirtius does not die until after the Battle of Mutina).

-43.title: Marcus Junius Brutus
-43.body: Marcus Junius Brutus proceeds to secure his position in Thrace and Macedonia. Gaius Cassius Longinus campaigns in Syria and defeats the army of Publius Cornelius Dolabella at Laodicea.

-43.title: Consuls
-43.body: Consuls: Gaius Vibius Pansa Caetronianus and Aulus Hirtius. The Roman Senate confirms Octavian as propraetor with joint responsibility for the campaign against Antony. Hirtius and Octavian mobilize troops for the march to Mutina, while Pansa continues the levy.

-43.title: imperium
-43.body: Octavian is given imperium, marking the start of his public career.

-43.title: Philippic
-43.body: April 21 Cicero's 14th and last Philippic. Antony is again defeated in the Battle of Mutina by a coalition of Octavian, Decimus Brutus, and the two consuls of the year.

-42.title: Augustus
-42.body: Octavian returns to Rome and arranges for ± 40,000 veterans' settlements in Campania, Etruria, Picenum, Samnium, Umbria, and in northern Italy.

-42.title: Mark Antony
-42.body: July – Mark Antony lands with an army (28 legions) in Illyria, leaving Octavian ill at Dyrrachium, and marches to Amphipolis. Admiral Ahenobarbus, with the Republican fleet (130 warships), blockades the Adriatic Sea.

-42.title: First Battle of Philippi
-42.body: First Battle of Philippi: The Triumvirs Mark Antony and Octavian fight an indecisive battle with Caesar's assassins Marcus Brutus and Cassius. The Roman forces including 2,000 Spartans, who have just arrived, are routed. Octavian takes refuge in the marsh.

-42.title: Looting
-42.body: Marcus Brutus begins to plunder the cities of Asia Minor, in order to obtain money and soldiers. The inhabitants of Lycia refuse to submit to Rome, and Brutus besieges Xanthus. After destroying their suburbs, the Xanthians withdraw into the heavily fortified city.

-42.title: Sicily
-42.body: In Sicily, Sextus Pompeius leads the naval operations in the Mediterranean Sea against the triumvirs. He blockades the grain routes from Spain and Africa.

-41.title: Mark Antony
-41.body: Mark Antony meets Cleopatra VII in Tarsus (Cilicia) and forms an alliance. He returns to Alexandria with her and they become lovers in the winter of 41–40 BC. To safeguard herself and Caesarion, she has Antony order the execution of her (half) sister Arsinoe IV, who is living at the temple of Artemis in Ephesus.

-41.title: Perusine War
-41.body: Perusine War: An armed resistance breaks out across Italy; the Umbrian city of Sentinum is captured and destroyed by Quintus Salvidienus Rufus.

-41.title: Perusia
-41.body: Lucius Antonius occupies Perusia. He accepts the appeal of the local population. Lucius and Fulvia are defeated by Gaius Julius Caesar Octavian in the Battle of Perugia.

-41.title: Consuls
-41.body: Consuls: Lucius Antonius and Publius Servilius Vatia Isauricus.

-40.title: Alexandria
-40.body: Mark Antony leaves Alexandria. After receiving news of the outcome at Perusia while en route to Phoenicia, he sets sail for Italy meeting the ambassadors of Sextus Pompey in Athens.

-40.title: Pacorus I of Parthia
-40.body: Pacorus crosses, with the help of Quintus Labienus, the Euphrates and invades Syria. The capital Antioch surrenders, and the Parthians take Phoenicia and Judea. However, they cannot besiege the fortified city of Tyre, because they have no fleet.

-40.title: Parthian Empire
-40.body: Parthians conquer Jerusalem. Hyrcanus II is removed from power, while Antigonus the Hasmonean becomes king of Judea under Parthian rule. Herod the Great flees Jerusalem to Rome.

-40.title: Quintus Labienus
-40.body: Quintus Labienus occupies Cilicia and marches, with an army, into Anatolia. Most cities surrender without resistance, except for Alabanda and Mylasa. The Parthians restore their territory to nearly the limits of the old Achaemenid Empire.

-40.title: Siege of Perusia
-40.body: Siege of Perusia: trying a last attempt to break the siege, which fails; Lucius Antonius surrenders to Octavian. His life is spared, but the citizens are executed or sold into slavery. Fulvia flees with her children and is exiled to Sicyon, where she dies of a sudden illness.

-39.title: Marcus Antonius
-39.body: Marcus Antonius dispatches Publius Ventidius Bassus with 11 legions to the East and drives Quintus Labienus out of Asia Minor. Labienus retreats into Syria, where he receives Parthian reinforcements. Ventidius finally defeats him in the battle at the Taurus Mountains.

-39.title: Cataphract
-39.body: Publius Ventidius defeats Pharnastanes with his cataphracts at the Amanus Gates, and goes on to reclaim Syria, Phoenicia and Judea. Labienus flees to Cilicia, where he is captured and executed.

-39.title: Sextus Pompey
-39.body: Sextus Pompey, styles himself "son of Neptune", controls Sicily, Sardinia, Corsica and the Peloponnesus, and is recognized by the Triumvirate in the Pact of Misenum. The pact helps to assure Rome's grain supply, and the blockade on Roman Italy is lifted.

-38.title: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa
-38.body: Octavian appoints Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa governor of Transalpine Gaul, where he puts down an uprising of the Aquitanians. He also fights successfully against the Germanic tribes, and becomes the next Roman general to cross the Rhine after Julius Caesar.

-38.title: Livia
-38.body: Octavian marries Livia while she is still pregnant from a recently broken marriage. Octavian gained permission from the College of Pontiffs to wed her while she is still pregnant from another husband. Three months after the wedding she gives birth to a second son, Nero Claudius Drusus, while he and his elder brother, the four-year-old Tiberius, are living in Octavian's household.

-37.title: Augustus
-37.body: Caesar Augustus (Octavian) engineers the "Second Pact of Tarentum" which renews the Triumvirate for an additional five years. Mark Antony exchanges 120 ships, for service against Sextus Pompeius. Octavian Caesar donates 1,000 troops from the Praetorian Guard and 20,000 legionaries for the Parthian campaign in Syria.

-37.title: Jerusalem
-37.body: Romans conquer Jerusalem from the Parthians. Herod the Great becomes king of Judea and Ananelus is installed as High Priest; both positions are seized from Antigonus II Mattathias after a five-month siege. Thousands of Jews are slaughtered by Roman troops supporting Herod.

-37.title: Asia Minor
-37.body: Antony reorganizes Asia Minor under strongmen loyal to him. He raises troops from his allies: Amyntas and Archelaus, kings of Galatia and Cappadocia. The old kingdom of Pontus is restored, from Armenia to the River Halys under Polemon I.

-37.title: harpax
-37.body: Agrippa creates the harbour "Portus Julius" in the today-submersed town of Puteoli (the modern Pozzuoli, close to Naples). The port is used to train the warships for naval battles, and a new fleet is built, with 20,000 oarsmen gathered by freeing slaves. He also incorporates, on quinqueremes, a technical innovation, called the harpax ("snatcher"): a combination ballista and grappling hook, based on the corvus.

-37.title: Goguryeo
-37.body: The kingdom of Goguryeo in Korea is founded by the king Dongmyeong. (traditional date)

-36.title: Maragheh
-36.body: October – Antony abandons the siege of Phraaspa (near Maragheh, Iran). He retreats, loses many men to disease and starvation in the subsequent retreat to Egypt, and marries Cleopatra VII while still married to Octavia.

-36.title: Judea
-36.body: Judea: Aristobulus III, the last of the Hasmoneans, becomes High Priest in Jerusalem, replacing Ananelus, who has only held the position for one year.

-36.title: Publius Canidius Crassus
-36.body: Publius Canidius Crassus invades Armenia and Iberia (Georgia); he forces Parnavaz II into an alliance against Zober, king of Albania.

-36.title: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa
-36.body: August – Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa, an admiral of Octavian Caesar, secures the Lipari Islands and harasses the coast from Mylae to Tyndaris. Octavian transports his legions, via Leucopetra, to Tauromenium (modern Taormina).

-36.title: Marcus Aemilius Lepidus
-36.body: Marcus Lepidus lands 12 legions from Africa and lays siege to Lilybaeum. He loses his army to Octavian when his men mutiny. Lepidus is kept in luxurious captivity in Rome until his death.

-35.title: Phraates
-35.body: Phraates IV ascended the Parthian throne after eliminating his father Orodes II and several royal rivals. The Mahestan (the noble council of the Parthian Empire) initially opposed his rise due to concerns about his violent methods. However, Phraates IV consolidated power by executing or exiling many council members, significantly weakening the influence of the Mahestan.

-35.title: Azes
-35.body: Azes I, Indo-Scythian ruler, completes the domination of the Scythians in northern India.

-35.title: Ancient Illyria
-35.body: Illyria becomes a Roman province. Gaius Julius Caesar Octavian conducts a rendezvous with the Roman fleet under Agrippa, which is engaged in clearing the Dalmatian coast of piracy.

-35.title: Sextus Pompeius
-35.body: Sextus Pompeius defeats the governor of Asia, Gaius Furnius, with three legions and seizes Nicaea and Nicomedia (modern Izmit).

-35.title: Pannonia
-35.body: Pannonia is attacked by Octavian Caesar, who conquers and sacks the stronghold Siscia (Sisak) of the Segestani, which is taken after a 30-day siege. The country is not definitely subdued, however, until 9 BC.

-34.title: Autumn – The Donations of Alexandria
-34.body: Autumn – The Donations of Alexandria: Antony distributes the eastern kingdoms as a gift to the children of Cleopatra; he declares Caesarion, Caesar's illegitimate son, as co-ruler of Egypt and Cyprus. Alexander Helios receives Armenia and Media; to Cleopatra Selene II he gives the kingdoms of Cyrenaica and Libya. His youngest son Ptolemy Philadelphus receives the Egyptian possessions in Phoenicia, Syria and Cilicia.

-34.title: Augustus
-34.body: Gaius Julius Caesar Octavian pacifies Dalmatia and Pannonia forming the province of Illyricum, while Antony regains Armenia from Parthia. Octavian reduces the outposts defending the Liburnian town of Promona, sets up siege works and forces its surrender.

-34.title: Armenia
-34.body: Antony advances into Armenia with an expeditionary force (16 legions) and marches to the capital Artaxata. He arrests king Artavasdes II and takes him to Alexandria.

-34.title: Mark Antony
-34.body: Mark Antony becomes Roman Consul for the second time. His partner is Lucius Scribonius Libo. The latter is replaced with Aemilius Lepidus Paullus during the year.

-33.title: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa
-33.body: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa is self demoted to Aedile, and builds the Aqua Julia, one of the aqueducts on which Rome's water supply depends, as well as cleaning the Cloaca Maxima sewerage system.

-33.title: Mark Antony
-33.body: Mark Antony annexes the kingdom of Media and arranges the marriage of his son Alexander Helios with princess Iotapa, the daughter of king Artavasdes I.

-33.title: Emperor Cheng of Han
-33.body: Crown Prince Ao ascends to the throne as Emperor Cheng of Han of the Han dynasty (until 7 BC).

-33.title: de summa Republica
-33.body: Gaius Julius Caesar Octavian becomes consul for the second time. His partner is Lucius Volcatius Tullus. Octavian delivers a speech; de summa Republica in the Roman Senate, in which he subjects the Donations.

-32.title: Sparta
-32.body: Sparta under Caius Iulius Eurycles, whose father Antony had been ordered to be executed for piracy, declares his support for Octavian. Lappa (modern Argyroupoli) in Attica and Kydonia in Crete revolt against Cleopatra.

-32.title: sacramentum
-32.body: July – The Roman Senate declares war upon Mark Antony and Cleopatra VII. Octavian Caesar is proclaimed dux and the West - Gallic and Spanish provinces, Africa, Sicily and Sardinia - swear an oath (sacramentum) of loyalty to him. In order to assure this oath, Octavian forces the high priest of the Vestal Virgins in Rome to hand over Antony's will, which contains information about the Roman-conquered territories as kingdoms and plans to build a tomb in Alexandria for him and Cleopatra.

-32.title: Garrison
-32.body: Winter – Antony distributes garrisons along the west coast of Greece, stations the fleet at Actium and establishes his headquarters at Patrae.

-32.title: Spring – Final War of the Roman Republic
-32.body: Spring – Final War of the Roman Republic: Mark Antony transfers his headquarters from Samos to Athens, where he assembles a fleet of 500 combat vessels and 300 transport ships, crewed by 150,000 men.

-32.title: Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus
-32.body: Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus and Gaius Sosius become consuls of Rome.

-31.title: consul ordinarius
-31.body: Augustus, then known as Imperator Caesar, becomes Roman Consul for the third time. Antony was designated as his colleague but is deposed from office, an action that Antony does not recognise. Elected in Antony's place is Marcus Valerius Messalla Corvinus; Antony's consulship is not recorded in some sources, as Valerius is marked instead as consul ordinarius.

-31.title: Roman Civil War
-31.body: Roman Civil War: Battle of Actium: Off the western coast of Greece, Octavian Caesar defeats naval forces under Mark Antony and Cleopatra VII.

-31.title: Judean Desert
-31.body: In the Judean Desert on an isolated rock plateau, fortress Masada is completed. Herod the Great builds an armory, barracks, storehouses and a palace.

-31.title: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa
-31.body: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa sails with 300 war galleys to the western Peloponnese, and occupies strategic positions around the Gulf of Corinth to cut off Antony's line of communication.

-31.title: Herodian Kingdom of Judea
-31.body: 31 BC Judea earthquake, earthquake mentioned in catalogues of historical earthquakes. It affected the Herodian Kingdom of Judea in the Holy Land.

-30.title: Alexandria
-30.body: Octavian Caesar captures Alexandria. This marks the official annexation of Ancient Egypt to the Roman Republic. Cleopatra evacuates her court and treasury to Berenice on the west coast of the Red Sea, but king Malchus of Nabatea attacks from the desert and burns the Egyptian ships.

-30.title: Augustus
-30.body: Gaius Julius Caesar Octavianus becomes consul for the fourth time. His partner is Marcus Licinius Crassus the Younger.

-30.title: Battle of Alexandria
-30.body: Battle of Alexandria: Mark Antony achieves a minor victory over Octavian's forces, but most of his army subsequently deserts, leading to his suicide.

-30.title: Death of Cleopatra
-30.body: August 10 or 12 – With the death of Cleopatra, probably by suicide, in Alexandria and the probable murder soon afterwards of her son and nominal co-ruler Caesarion, the Ptolemaic dynasty, the last of Ancient Egypt, comes to an end. The first year of Octavian's reign in Egypt.

-30.title: Dardanelles
-30.body: Spring – Octavian leads his army to the Dardanelles, ships them across to Asia Minor and marches into Syria where Herod the Great sends him vows of loyalty and thousands of his own troops in support.

-29.title: Revolt of Thebes
-29.body: Revolt of Thebes suppressed in Egypt.

-29.title: Sofia
-29.body: Sofia, modern day capital of Bulgaria, is conquered by the Romans and becomes known as Ulpia Serdica.

-29.title: Rome
-29.body: Octavian celebrates, in Rome, three triumphs on consecutive days (August 13, August 14, and August 15) to commemorate his victories in Illyricum, Actium and Egypt.

-29.title: Marcus Licinius Crassus
-29.body: Marcus Licinius Crassus campaigns successfully in the Balkans, killing the king of the Bastarnae by his own hand, but is denied the right to dedicate the spolia opima by Octavian.

-29.title: Georgics
-29.body: Virgil probably completes the Georgics and begins composition of the Aeneid.

-28.title: Augustus
-28.body: Gaius Julius Caesar Octavian becomes Roman Consul for the sixth time. His partner Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa becomes Consul for the second time.

-28.title: imperium maius
-28.body: The Roman Senate grants Octavian Caesar imperium maius (supreme command) of the Roman armed forces (Around 28 legions).

-28.title: Sunspot
-28.body: The earliest dated record of a sunspot by Chinese astronomers.

-27.title: Augustus
-27.body: Augustus' first census of the Roman Empire (formerly the Roman Republic) reports a total of 4,063,000 citizens.

-27.title: Colossi of Memnon
-27.body: Northern statue of the Colossi of Memnon is shattered by an earthquake in Egypt (according to Strabo).

-27.title: Pantheon, Rome
-27.body: Marcus Agrippa begins the construction of the old Pantheon, Rome.

-27.title: Princeps
-27.body: Octavian formally returns full power to the Senate; they give him the titles of Princeps and Augustus. He accepts this honor, having declined the alternative title of Romulus, thus becoming first Roman emperor.

-27.title: Roman legion
-27.body: Caesar Augustus starts a new military reform. He reduces the number of legions to 26 and creates the Praetorian Guard (1,000 men).

-26.title: Zenith
-26.body: Christian Cross Asterism (astronomy) at Zenith of Lima, Peru.

-26.title: Aelius Gallus
-26.body: Disastrous campaign of Aelius Gallus in the Arabian Peninsula, then known as "Arabia Felix".

-26.title: Cantabri
-26.body: Augustus starts a campaign against the Cantabrians in northern Hispania. He leads an army (8 legions) and consolidates the north-eastern region.

-26.title: Cleopatra Selene
-26.body: Cleopatra Selene marries Juba II of Numidia, and as a wedding present Augustus makes her the queen of Mauretania in her own right.

-26.title: Tiridates II of Parthia
-26.body: Tiridates II invades Parthia and issues coins dated from March and May, 26 BC.

-25.title: Estimation
-25.body: Estimation: Rome, capital of the Roman Empire, becomes the largest city in the world, taking the lead from Chang'an, capital of China.

-25.title: Galatia
-25.body: Galatia becomes a Roman province after the death of its king. The Roman troops stationed there are relocated to Egypt.

-25.title: Temple
-25.body: The temple to Neptune on the Circus Flaminius is built.

-25.title: Emerita Augusta
-25.body: The Roman colony of Emerita Augusta is founded (present-day Mérida).

-25.title: Tributary state
-25.body: The government gives its tributary states 20,000 rolls of silk cloth and about 20,000 pounds of silk floss.

-24.title: Augustus
-24.body: Caesar Augustus becomes Roman Consul for the tenth time. His partner is Gaius Norbanus Flaccus.

-24.title: Herod the Great
-24.body: Herod the Great marries for a third time, to Mariamne II, after a 4-year hiatus from family life (after putting to death his 2nd wife Mariamne I).

-24.title: Roman Egypt
-24.body: Augustus founds the city of Nicopolis in Egypt to commemorate his final victory over Mark Antony.

-23.title: Princeps
-23.body: Augustus relinquishes the position of consul, retains that of tribune of Rome, and assumes that of Princeps, or "First Citizen." (see Roman Empire).

-23.title: Nubia
-23.body: The Nubians, led by queen Kandake Amanirenas, take the initiative against the Roman Empire, and attack the Roman province of Egypt moving towards Elephantine.

-23.title: Herod the Great
-23.body: Herod the Great builds a palace in Jerusalem and the fortress Herodium in Judaea. He also marries his third wife, named Mariamne, the daughter of high priest Simon.

-23.title: Augustus
-23.body: Caesar Augustus becomes Roman consul for the eleventh time. His co-consul is Aulus Terentius Varro Murena.

-23.title: Following coinage reform, the as is struck in reddish pure copper, instead of br…
-23.body: Following coinage reform, the as is struck in reddish pure copper, instead of bronze. The denominations of sestertius and dupondius are introduced as large bronze coins.

-22.title: Deiotariana
-22.body: The Roman governor of Egypt, Gaius Petronius, marches up the Nile with legions XXII Deiotariana and III Cyrenaica, and destroys the Nubian capital of Napata.

-22.title: Artaxias
-22.body: King Artaxias II returns, with the support of the Parthians, to Armenia and claims the throne. Artavasdes I escapes to Rome, where Caesar Augustus receives him.

-22.title: Aemilius Lepidus Paullus
-22.body: Aemilius Lepidus Paullus and Lucius Munatius Plancus are Censors.

-21.title: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa
-21.body: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa divorces Claudia Marcella Major, and marries Julia the Elder, daughter of Caesar Augustus.

-20.title: Augustus
-20.body: Emperor Augustus Caesar negotiates a peace with Parthia, making Armenia a buffer zone between the two major powers. The captured eagles of Marcus Licinius Crassus and Mark Antony are returned.

-19.title: Cantabri
-19.body: The Cantabrians, living on the northernmost coast of Spain, are brought under Roman control. The region is completely subdued until 16 BC.

-19.title: Aeneid
-19.body: The Aeneid by the Roman poet Virgil is published after his death. The Aeneid is an epic poem in 12 books that tells the story of the foundation of Rome from the ashes of Troy.

-19.title: Rome
-19.body: In Rome, Lucius Cornelius Balbus receives the honor of a triumph on the Forum Romanum, for his victories over the Garamantes in Africa.

-19.title: Herod the Great
-19.body: King Herod the Great begins renovation of the Temple in Jerusalem.

-19.title: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa
-19.body: Marcus Vipsanius Agrippa completes the Aqua Virgo; the aqueduct is 21 km (13 mi) in length and supplies the city of Rome with about 100,000,000 liters of water every day.

-18.title: Julian Laws
-18.body: Caesar Augustus introduces the Lex Julia (Julian Laws): Lex Iulia de Ambitu penalised bribery when acquiring political offices. Lex Iulia de maritandis ordinibus introduced penalties and incentives to promote marriage, and forbade marriage of senators, as well as of their children, to prostitutes and to freedmen.

-18.title: King Onjo of Baekje
-18.body: Onjo becomes the first ruler of the Korean kingdom of Baekje (traditional date).

-18.title: Lex Iulia de Ambitu
-18.body: Lex Iulia de Ambitu penalised bribery when acquiring political offices.

-18.title: Lex Iulia de maritandis ordinibus
-18.body: Lex Iulia de maritandis ordinibus introduced penalties and incentives to promote marriage, and forbade marriage of senators, as well as of their children, to prostitutes and to freedmen.

-17.title: Augustus
-17.body: Emperor Augustus adopts Gaius and Lucius Caesar.

-17.title: Secular games
-17.body: Emperor Augustus celebrates the secular games in Rome, for which Horace's hymn the "Carmen Saeculare" is commissioned.

-16.title: Noricum
-16.body: Noricum is incorporated into the Roman Empire.

-16.title: Augustus
-16.body: Caesar Augustus reorganizes the German provinces, making Trier their capital.

-16.title: Clades Lolliana
-16.body: Clades Lolliana: Roman consul Marcus Lollius is defeated by a Germanic horde.

-16.title: Norican
-16.body: The Noricans having joined with the Pannonians in invading Histria, are defeated by Publius Silius Nerva, proconsul of Illyricum.

-16.title: Roman temple
-16.body: Construction of the Roman temple of Maison Carrée at Nîmes in Gallia Narbonensis (approximate date).

-15.title: Nero Claudius Drusus
-15.body: Drusus decides to improve the passage through the Alps for military control of Noricum and Raetia and builds the Via Claudia Augusta through Italy.

-15.title: Raeti
-15.body: The Raeti tribes of the Alps are subjugated by Tiberius and Nero Claudius Drusus and the new Roman province of Raetia is established with Chur (in modern-day Switzerland) as its capital. During the campaign, Roman triremes destroy the fleet of the Vindelici in the Battle of Lake Constance. Augsburg is founded as Augusta Vindelicorum; and Legio XXI Rapax is stationed at Regensburg in the new province.

-15.title: Marcus Livius Drusus Libo
-15.body: Marcus Livius Drusus Libo and Lucius Calpurnius Piso are Roman Consuls.

-14.title: Augustus
-14.body: Caesar Augustus makes Beeroth (modern Beirut) a colonia, named Colonia Julia Augusta Felix Berytus.

-14.title: Nero Claudius Drusus
-14.body: The Roman general Nero Claudius Drusus fortifies Augusta Vangionum, the modern city of Worms, Germany.

-14.title: Legio X Fretensis
-14.body: Winter – The Roman Legio X Fretensis is stationed in Syria, and the legionaries are settled at the ancient city of Beirut.

-13.title: Ara Pacis Augustae
-13.body: The Ara Pacis Augustae ("Altar of Augustan Peace") is commissioned by the Roman Senate to honor the triumphal return of Emperor Augustus from Hispania and Gaul.

-13.title: Tiberius
-13.body: Tiberius Claudius Nero and Publius Quinctilius Varus are Roman Consuls.

-13.title: Gaul
-13.body: Drusus is granted governor of Gaul and mobilises a Roman army to beat the Germans back across the Rhine. He travels to the North Sea and pays tribute to the Frisii.

-13.title: Thrace
-13.body: Revolt in Thrace against Roman rule led by Vologases.

-13.title: Nero Claudius Drusus
-13.body: The Roman general Nero Claudius Drusus builds the stronghold of castrum Moguntiacum in the location of the modern-day city of Mainz, Germany.

-12.title: Augustus
-12.body: Emperor Augustus is given the title Pontifex Maximus.

-12.title: Pyramid of Cestius
-12.body: Approximate date – Pyramid of Cestius erected in Rome.

-12.title: Tiberius
-12.body: Tiberius Claudius Nero summoned to Pannonia due to severe revolt by the Delmatae.

-12.title: Halley's Comet
-12.body: Halley's Comet makes an appearance, as recorded by Chinese Astronomers for about 56 days. ), in the Autumn the seventh month, on the day Sing Wei, a comet swept in Gemini, went through Pollux and Castor, and passed over Leo and Virgo. It was then behind the sun about six degrees, and appeared in the east earlier in the morning.

-12.title: Roman legion
-12.body: Roman armies based at Xanten, Cologne and Mainz campaign beyond the Rhine.

-11.title: Battle of the Lupia River
-11.body: Battle of the Lupia River: Roman forces under Augustus's stepson Nero Claudius Drusus win a victory in Germania.

-11.title: Battle of Arbalo
-11.body: Battle of Arbalo: Roman forces under Augustus's stepson Nero Claudius Drusus beat off a German ambush.

-11.title: Rhine
-11.body: May – Drusus secures the Rhine frontier and builds Roman fortresses near Bonn, Dorsten, Haltern, and Oberaden.

-11.title: Quintus Aelius Tubero
-11.body: Quintus Aelius Tubero and Paullus Fabius Maximus are Roman Consuls.

-10.title: Roman Empire
-10.body: The Romans build a bridge across the Rhine near Bonn (approximate date).

-10.title: Obelisk of Montecitorio
-10.body: The Obelisk of Montecitorio is brought from Egypt to Rome by Emperor Augustus to be erected as a sundial gnomon of the Solarium Augusti. It is now in the Piazza Montecitorio.

-10.title: Military camp
-10.body: A Roman military camp is established at Speyer (Germany).

-9.title: Pannonia
-9.body: Pannonia is incorporated into the Roman Empire as part of Illyria.

-9.title: Nero Claudius Drusus
-9.body: Nero Claudius Drusus, Emperor Augustus' stepson, begins a campaign against the Marcomanni, but dies soon after, due to a fall from his horse.

-9.title: Aretas IV Philopatris
-9.body: Accession of Aretas IV Philopatris as King of the Nabataeans (modern Jordan).

-9.title: Ara Pacis
-9.body: The Ara Pacis ("Altar of Augustan Peace"), voted for by the Senate four years earlier, is dedicated.

-9.title: Ab Urbe Condita Libri
-9.body: Livy completes compilation of his Ab Urbe Condita Libri, 142 books covering the history of Rome since its foundation down to 9 BC (approximate date).

-8.title: Maroboduus
-8.body: King Maroboduus becomes ruler of the Marcomanni and fights against the Roman Empire's expansion in Bohemia.

-8.title: Augustus
-8.body: After 20 years, Emperor Augustus initiates his second census of the Roman Empire.

-8.title: Arminius
-8.body: Arminius, son of a Cheruscan chieftain, is taken as a hostage to Rome, where he receives a military education.

-7.title: Augustus
-7.body: Augustus' second census of the Roman Empire reports a total of 4,233,000 citizens. However, the specific criteria of the census are still not clear.

-7.title: Emperor Ai of Han
-7.body: Emperor Ai succeeds to the throne of Han China.

-7.title: 14 regions of Augustan Rome
-7.body: Rome is divided in 14 administrative regions.

-6.title: Tiberius
-6.body: Tiberius retires to Rhodes, to the annoyance of Emperor Augustus. He is recalled to Rome years later, in AD 4, becoming Augustus' adopted son and heir following the deaths of Lucius and Gaius Caesar.

-5.title: Supernova
-5.body: December – Probable supernova in the constellation Capricornus.

-5.title: Nova
-5.body: March – Probable nova in the constellation Aquila.

-4.title: Herod the Great
-4.body: Upon the death of Herod the Great, there is unrest in his client kingdom of Judea. His son, Herod Archelaus, becomes the new ruler. Herod Antipas becomes tetrarch of Galilee and Perea.

-4.title: Namhae of Silla
-4.body: Namhae becomes king of the Korean kingdom of Silla.

-3.title: Han China
-3.body: In Han China, a terrible drought strikes Shandong.

-3.title: Pont Julien
-3.body: Construction of the Pont Julien in modern southern France.

-2.title: pater patriae
-2.body: Augustus is proclaimed pater patriae ("father of the country") by the Roman Senate. This bestowed title is the logical consequence and final proof of Augustus' supreme position as princeps, the first in charge over the Roman state.

-2.title: Aqua Augusta
-2.body: The Aqua Alsietina (or Aqua Augusta), a Roman aqueduct in Rome, is constructed during the reign of Augustus (approximate date).

-2.title: Phraates
-2.body: Phraates V and his mother Musa become rulers of the Parthian Empire following the murder of Phraates IV.

-2.title: Date of the birth of Jesus
-2.body: Date of the birth of Jesus according to the writings of Tertullian, Eusebius and Epiphanius (probably after the statement of Jesus being "around 30 years old" in AD 29).

-2.title: Julia the Elder
-2.body: Julia the Elder, daughter of Augustus, is exiled on charges of treason and adultery to Pandateria; her mother Scribonia accompanies her.

-1.title: Aulus Caecina Severus
-1.body: Aulus Caecina Severus was appointed consul by Emperor Augustus, succeeding Cossus Cornelius Lentulus Gaetulicus and Lucius Calpurnius Piso.

-1.title: Emperor Ai of Han
-1.body: Emperor Ai dies and is succeeded by his 8-year-old cousin Ping, who is enthroned on October 17. Wang Mang is appointed regent by Empress Dowager Wang Zhengjun, his aunt.

-1.title: Nativity of Jesus
-1.body: Estimated birth of Jesus, in the Christian religion, as assigned by Dionysius Exiguus in his Anno Domini era; according to most scholars, Dionysius used the word "incarnation", but it is not known whether he meant conception or birth. However, at least one scholar thinks Dionysius placed the incarnation of Jesus in the next year, AD 1. Most modern scholars do not consider Dionysius' calculations authoritative, and place the event several years earlier.

-1.title: Gaius Caesar
-1.body: Gaius Caesar marries Livilla, daughter of Antonia Minor and Nero Claudius Drusus, in an effort to gain prestige.

-1.title: Dong Xian
-1.body: Former regent Dong Xian, who was previously Ai's lover, commits suicide with his wife.
